column3: He was seventy years of age, which was even then the common period and appointed limit of human life, as he himself declares in Psalm 89:10, saying: "The days of our years in themselves are seventy years." This remains to this day the boundary of man's life; wherefore from the age of David until our own time (a span of some 2,600 years) the age and life of men has scarcely diminished.

The Rabbis fable that this affliction was visited upon David by God as a punishment, because in the cave he had secretly cut off the hem of the garment of King Saul his lord, who was pursuing him, as related in 1 Kings 24. Others assign the cause of his coldness to the fact that he was born last of parents who were themselves old and of cold constitution. But Scripture here gives the cause as advanced old age: "He had," it says, "many days of years," and had spent them in continual hardships, wars, and labors, exhausting his strength. Abulensis adds that he was also afflicted by disease.

Galen and Francisco Vallesius, physician to Philip I, King of Spain, give the natural and medical cause of this account. Saint Jerome assigns the moral cause, and Saint Prosper the tropological. Hear Vallesius in his Sacred Philosophy, chapter 29. "They advised," he says, "that David receive a young girl rather than wine or fur garments, because that human warmth is in its substance most similar to man's natural heat, and does not exceed its proper degree." Hence Galen, in book 7 of his Method, where he treats the weakness of a cold and dry stomach, says: "Among those things which are applied externally, let a girl of good constitution, one who never grows old, lie beside him, so that she may constantly touch his abdomen." This, as Vallesius notes, was brought about in David's case at an age when he could no longer know a virgin carnally; otherwise conjugal union and consummation would have warmed him far more than the mere proximity of a young girl. Thus Rudolf of Habsburg, the first Emperor of the Austrian house, was accustomed, as Serarius reports from Cuspinian, to kiss chastely the little daughters of noblemen and princes, and even their wives, in their presence, saying that in his old age, worn out with age, he drew sweet-scented breath from their lips. This, then, is the literal and common exposition of Abulensis, Dionysius, Lyra, Serarius, Salian, Sanchez, and others. The moral cause is given by Saint Jerome, in his letter 2 to Nepotianus: "Does it not seem to you," he says, "if you follow the literal sense, that this is either a fiction from a mime or a farce from a comedy? A cold old man is wrapped in garments, and grows warm only in the embrace of a young girl. Bathsheba was still living; Abigail survived, and his other wives and concubines, whom Scripture records; all are as it were rejected as cold, and only in the arms of a young girl does the aged man find warmth. Abraham was much older than David, and yet while Sara lived he sought no other wife; Isaac was twice David's age, and with Rebecca, already grown old, he never grew cold." Here observe that Saint Jerome, like Angelomus and others, elevates and as it were overturns the literal sense, which must absolutely be established as the foundation; wherefore by these words they signify only that the literal sense, as being less profitable here, is to be passed over briefly, and one must adhere to the moral sense, which is the principal one. This Saint Jerome assigns in the same place: "Who then is this Shunammite wife and virgin, so fervent that she warmed the cold, so holy that she did not provoke the warm to lust? Let Solomon, the wisest of men, expound his father's delight, and let the man of peace tell of the warrior's embraces. Possess wisdom, possess understanding. Forget not, nor turn away from the words of my mouth; forsake her not and she shall preserve thee; love her and she shall keep thee. The beginning of wisdom — possess wisdom, and in all thy possessions acquire understanding; embrace her and she shall exalt thee; honor her and she shall embrace thee, that she may give to thy head a crown of grace. A crown of delights shall she protect thee." All the bodily virtues change in old age; all other things diminish, but wisdom grows greater. He confirms this from the etymology of the names Abisag and Shunammite, adding after much else: "But even the mystery of the name Abisag itself points to the ampler wisdom of old men; for it is interpreted as 'my father is superabundant,' or 'the groaning of my father.' The word 'superabundant' is ambiguous, but in the present passage it denotes virtue, because wisdom is more abundant, overflowing, and generous in the aged. In another place, however, 'superfluous' is taken as meaning not necessary..."

Although he was the fourth son of David, yet since the three elder sons of David were already dead, he was in effect his firstborn; hence it seemed by right of primogeniture that the kingdom should belong to him. But because he sought it with arrogance and approached it with unseemly haste, he was thereby excluded from it, and Solomon obtained it through the modesty of his spirit, the dignity and honor of primogeniture being transferred to him. Hence Saint Ambrose, in his Apology for David, book 1, chapter 5, says: "David took such great precaution that when he had learned that his son Adonijah was usurping the kingdom for himself, being pressed, he chose not the one who was eager to seize it, but the one who waited." For he knew that the kingdom was owed to Solomon by God's ordination and decree; for God had revealed this to him in 2 Kings, chapter 7, verse 12; and therefore David had sworn to Bathsheba that he would raise her son Solomon to the throne.
...as his rival in the kingdom. For he knew that the kingdom had been promised to him by David and by God, and therefore he was striving to seize it from him by anticipation.

As if to say: I and Solomon will be reckoned, and will be punished by Adonijah as sinners and rebels, because we did not consent to his invasion of the kingdom. Again, as sinners on account of the adultery which I once committed with you; for the infamy of this sin was being renewed and magnified by Adonijah in order to deprive Solomon of the kingdom, by saying to the people: "Beware lest you make Solomon king over you, seeing that he is the son of an adulteress, and therefore a man of ill repute." So Cajetan. Hence St. Jerome in his Traditions reads: *Erimus manzeres*, that is, we shall be held as bastards and persons of infamy.
As if to say: May God approve and confirm that which you have spoken, namely, that Solomon may succeed you in the kingdom. # GEM

*And he anointed Solomon.* Note. The anointing of the kings of Judah was ordinarily performed by the High Priest with holy oil. This is proved because it is said of David: "I have anointed him with my holy oil," Psalm 88, verse 21. The same is to be understood of Solomon, whom Sadoc here anointed with the oil of the tabernacle, which was holy. For kings were, as it were, the anointed of the Lord, inasmuch as they were leaders and rulers of God's faithful and holy people in the place of God. Nevertheless, some were anointed extraordinarily by a Prophet, and in such cases they appear to have used oil not from the tabernacle but some other common oil, which however seems to have been blessed by the Prophet — for example, if they were far from the temple where this oil was kept, as Elijah was, being in Samaria and on Mount Carmel, when he sent Elisha to anoint Hazael at Damascus and Jehu at Ramoth Gilead. Indeed, the kings of Israel generally, being schismatics and enemies of Judah, appear to have been anointed not with the oil of the tabernacle, which was at Jerusalem, but with another oil. Thus Absalom, in 2 Kings chapter 19, verse 10, was anointed, but not with holy oil, since he acted in rebellion, the High Priest being absent and fleeing before him with David. Joshua likewise was consecrated as leader of the people not by anointing but by the laying on of hands by Moses, Numbers chapter 27. Moreover, David, says Abulensis, was not anointed by Samuel with the holy oil at his first anointing, because that anointing was private; and had Saul learned of it, he would have severely punished them all. Nor was he anointed with it at the second anointing, when he was anointed in Hebron by the tribe of Judah; because the tabernacle, in which the holy oil was kept, was then at Gibeon, where Ishbosheth the enemy of David reigned. But at the third anointing he was anointed with it, when he was anointed and received as king by all Israel. Finally, one oil was used to anoint kings, and another to anoint High Priests and priests; for kings were anointed with simple oil, but priests with a compound oil made from aromatic spices, namely myrrh, cinnamon, cassia, and calamus, mixed with olive oil, as is clear from Exodus chapter 30, verses 23, 24, and 32, where this oil is called holy, and where it is forbidden under pain of death that any layman be anointed with it. Therefore a king could not be anointed with it, but with a lay and common oil, which was nevertheless called holy because it was taken from the holy tabernacle and consecrated by a certain rite for the anointing of the faithful king who was to rule over God's people, and was administered by the consecrated High Priest. The consecration of priests was therefore far holier than that of kings, as is evident from its rite, which is described throughout the whole of Exodus chapter 29 and Leviticus chapter 8. This is the opinion of Oleaster, Cajetan, Stella, and Lipomanus on Exodus 30; and it is favoured by St. Augustine, Question 135 on Exodus, and by Lorinus on Psalm 88, verse 21. The contrary opinion is held, however, by the Rabbis, whom Genebrardus cites and follows on Psalm 88, verse 21, as do Lyra, Dionysius here, and Isidore, book 3 of his *On Ecclesiastical Offices*, chapter 25, who hold that David and Solomon and their descendants were anointed with the same oil as that used for the anointing of High Priests. For they say that, on the counsel of Samuel and the prophets of that age, a dispensation was granted from the law which forbade this in Exodus chapter 30, verses 31 and 32, in favour of the laws of the Davidic family, in view of the grace of Christ the Lord who was to be born from it — of whose spiritual and divine anointing that outward anointing was a type, and of whose vicarship and ministry both they at that time and afterwards all kings were to be made aware. Isidore adds that they were therefore given the title of Christs, because they were anointed with the same chrism as the priests, so that it was therefore sacrilege to touch them, as in Psalm 104, verse 15. The same view is held by Pineda, book 2 on Solomon, chapter 6, and by Barradius, volume 2, book 3, chapter 41. But all these rest upon the tradition of the Hebrews, which is often false and deceptive, particularly in this place, where it fabricates...

From this it is clear that Solomon, having been publicly anointed and accepted as king by all the people, at once began actually to reign, and that David, now worn out by old age, transferred the kingdom and its administration to him. The following verses make this evident. For straightway Solomon, as king, spared Adonijah and sent him home. Hence St. Ambrose, book 3 on Luke, St. Augustine, book 17 of the City of God, chapter 8, St. Jerome, Epistle 132, Severus Sulpicius, and others assert that Solomon began to reign while his father was yet living; and Josephus states this explicitly, book 7, chapter 10, at the end: "When Solomon had been conducted into the palace," he says, "and placed upon his father's throne, from that day forward they were obedient to him." Rupert comments symbolically: "While his father was yet living," he says, "King Solomon is established; because assuredly God the Father, who appointed Christ as King and heir of all things, never has died, nor ever shall die." Moreover, David transferred the kingdom to Solomon in such a way that he himself did not lay down his royal authority and right, nor the burden and care of the kingdom, but retained it until death, yet in such a manner that he permitted Solomon to reign alongside him and committed many affairs of the kingdom to him. This is clear from chapter 2, where the dying David gives many commandments to Solomon. Verse 45. And Zadok the priest (the high priest, that is, the Pontiff) and Nathan the Prophet anointed him king in Gihon. Gihon was a hill, and on it a spring near Mount Calvary, where Christ crucified reigned from the wood of the Cross, as the Church sings from the Septuagint. Hence on the cross Pilate gave him this title: Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews. Solomon anointed in Gihon therefore prefigured the kingdom of Christ who was to be born from him: for Solomon, that is, the peaceful king, was an express type of Christ, who is our Peace, who made both one (Ephesians 2), uniting Jews and Gentiles alike in himself and reconciling them to God. Furthermore, the name of Solomon was appropriated by the Emperors of the Turks — the Selims and Solimans, so called from Solomon — by the Germans named Frederick (for Frederick in German signifies the same as "rich in peace"), and by the Greeks named Irene and Irenaeus. Finally, Solomon was anointed twice: first, here before the tribe of Judah, hastily and in tumult, in order to exclude Adonijah from the kingdom; second, publicly before all Israel, so that they might accept him as king — for which purpose David had convened them shortly before his death, at which time also Zadok was anointed as pontiff, as is expressly stated in 1 Chronicles 29:22. Solomon began to reign in the year of the world 2930, 269 years before the founding of Rome, and 1020 years before the birth of Christ, according to the chronological table I prefixed to the Pentateuch. Verse 47. And the servants of the king went in and blessed our lord King David, saying: May God make the name of Solomon more glorious than your name, and magnify his throne above your throne. They knew that this would not be cause for envy to David, but would be pleasing and welcome to him. For parents desire that their children should grow in wealth and glory and surpass what they themselves have left behind; for as a son's glory increases, so too grows the glory of his father. Moreover, the event corresponded to this wish of the people: for Solomon surpassed David in the extent of his kingdom, in wealth, in splendors, in magnificence, in wisdom, and in glory — though not in virtue and holiness, at least not in old age. Thus Claudian sang to the Emperor Honorius in his fourth consulship: "Behold, the vow is fulfilled; already his son equals him in merit, and — what is still more to be desired — surpasses him." And Sidonius Apollinaris, book 8, Epistle 7, to Audacius, writes: "I ask of God that your sons may attain to you, or — what is still more fitting to desire — may transcend you." See Seneca, book 3 On Benefits, chapters 36 and 37.

The meaning is: If Adonijah shall henceforth conduct himself uprightly, faithfully, and obediently toward me, acknowledging me as king and plotting no rebellion against me, I will pardon him for having usurped the kingdom and will bring no harm upon him. But if he shall again seek the kingdom by schemes and treachery, I will punish him according to his deserts. And because Adonijah did this very thing, he was therefore slain by Solomon, as we shall hear in the following chapter.

These are the words of Solomon, somewhat offended at Adonijah, chastising his ambition and threatening just punishment if he should attempt anything new. The meaning is: I, O Adonijah, pardon you on this occasion for your ambition and the wrong you have done me; but if hereafter you attempt anything of the kind, know that I shall be mindful of this injury, and will chastise you more severely for both offenses together. Go therefore to your house, remain there in quiet, attend to your own affairs, and meddle not in the public business of my kingdom — otherwise you shall experience the justice of my vengeance. CHAPTER TWO

That is, of all earth-born men, or of all lords dwelling upon the earth — as if to say: I am tending toward death, as other men who inhabit the earth do, that I may return to the earth and dust from which I was formed. This is a metonymy signifying the same as what is said elsewhere: *I am going the way of all flesh* — that is, the way of death, which all who are constituted of flesh must travel. Salutary indeed is this remembrance of death and the grave, according to that passage in Ecclesiasticus 7:40: *In all thy works remember thy last end, and thou shalt never sin.* And again in chapter 12: *Remember that death is not slow* — for this is the covenant of this world, that every man shall die the death. In a similar manner, St. Anthony, when dying, said to his disciples, as St. Athanasius attests: *I indeed, my little children, according to the words of the Scriptures, am walking the way of the fathers. For now the Lord invites me, and I long to behold heavenly things. But you, O my dear ones, I admonish: do not suddenly lose the labor of so long a time. Today you may think you have seized upon religious devotion — let the resolution of your will that has been begun continue to grow.* And St. Paul the First Hermit, visited near death by St. Anthony at God's prompting, as St. Jerome attests, said: *Behold, him whom you have sought with so great labor; unkempt white hair covers limbs rotted with age. Behold, you see a man who will soon be dust.* And a little later: *Because the time of my falling asleep has arrived, and what I have always desired — to be dissolved and to be with Christ — now that my course is run, there remains for me only the crown of justice. You have been sent by the Lord to cover my poor little body with earth — or rather, to give earth back to the earth.* And St. Mary of Egypt said to Zosimas: *Bury, Abba Zosimas, the poor little body of wretched Mary. Give back to the earth what is hers, and add dust to dust. Pray, however, to the Lord for me.* And Barlaam said to Josaphat: *No longer, most dear son, shall the same house and table unite us in this life. For I am now entering upon the last road of my fathers. Therefore it remains for you to show proof of your love for me by observing the divine commandments and by dwelling in this place until the very last day of your life — ordering the course of your life as you have learned, and never ceasing to remember my humble and lowly soul. Farewell abundantly, and delight and rejoice in Christ, for you have exchanged earthly and perishable things for eternal and imperishable goods, and the reward of your works is near, and already at hand is He who renders recompense.* So writes Damascenus in their history.

*Guard* — that is, the precepts which God has commanded you and all others to observe and keep. This is clear from what follows. *Custodies* (guards) are therefore here called the things to be guarded, by metonymy. *That thou mayest understand all that thou doest* — that is, that thou mayest act intelligently and prudently in whatever thou undertakest; for this is the meaning of the Hebrew word *baskei*. The Chaldean translates it: *that thou mayest make prosperous all that thou doest*; others: *that thou mayest be happy in all that thou undertakest*. For he who acts prudently in his affairs acts prosperously in them. Prudence is the mother of prosperity and happiness, just as imprudence is the mother of misery. **Verse 6:** *And he put the blood of war* (which, that is, could be the cause of war) *in peace, and placed the blood of battle in his girdle.* And so forth, *and in his sandals* — as if to say: Joab treacherously killed Abner and Amasa, feigning friendship and so close and tight an embrace with them that in treacherously stabbing them in that embrace, his belt and sandals were stained with the blood of those he had slain. So say Lyran, Vatable, and others. He calls this the blood of war and of battle, because there was danger of war arising from this slaughter — namely, lest all Israel rebel against David and wish to avenge the treacherous killing of their leaders Abner and Amasa by Joab; and therefore David here commands him to be put to death. The phrase *blood of war* alludes to the battle in which Abner justly slew Asahel, the brother of Joab, whose death Joab accordingly avenged by the slaying of Abner — as if to say: Joab wished to avenge the blood of his brother Asahel by killing Abner, but he did so wrongfully and unjustly. For Abner killed Asahel justly in battle; but Joab killed Abner unjustly and treacherously in time of peace, when Abner had already been reconciled to David, and was indeed bringing all Israel back to him. Therefore Joab killed Abner and Amasa in peace as though in battle, and so treacherously converted peace into war — fighting and drawing the sword, he fraudulently slew both of them while they believed themselves to be at peace, and that everything between themselves and David and Joab was settled, on account of the peace concluded with him; and this Joab feigned while treacherously embracing and kissing each of them — which was a great act of fraud, injustice, and perfidy on the part of Joab. **Verse 6 continued:** *Do therefore according to thy wisdom* (that is, having sought out a convenient opportunity and just pretext, punish him), *and bring not down his grey head to the grave in peace* — as if to say: thou shalt not permit him to die peacefully of old age in his bed, but thou shalt punish him with a violent death. For before Christ, all who died descended to the underworld, at least to the limbo of the fathers. The law of *lex talionis* has been enacted by natural, divine, and human law, so that he who kills another should be killed. You may ask: Why did David not kill Joab himself, but commanded it to Solomon? The answer is: because when Joab killed Abner and Amasa, David did not have sufficient power and strength to do so. For he was not yet firmly established in the kingdom, and he feared that Joab might stir up Israel against him, as indeed Abner had done a little before. Afterwards, however, David, entangled in continual wars, could not accomplish it — especially because he needed Joab, for he had no other man equally suited to place in command of the army. He therefore entrusted this to Solomon, who enjoyed complete peace, and therefore wielded full dignity and authority. So says Josephus. Another reason why David commanded Joab to be killed is offered by Theodoret: he feared, he says, lest Joab, following his customary malice, would despise the youth of Solomon, and either kill him under the guise of goodwill, as he had killed the aforementioned men, or openly array troops in battle order and divide Israel by thrusting in Adonias as king, for whom he had shown favor.

The Septuagint renders it as *grievous*; the Chaldean as *bitter*. The Hebrew word is *nimresret*, which the Hebrews, according to St. Jerome, explain in such a way that through the five letters of which this word is composed, the five insults which Shimei hurled at David are signified. Namely: through the first letter *nun* is signified *Nof'ri*, that is, *adulterer*, on account of the adultery with Bathsheba; through the second letter *mem*, *Moabi*, that is, *Moabite*, because David traced his lineage from the ignoble Ruth the Moabitess; through the third letter *resh*, *Rotseach*, that is, *murderer*, on account of Uriah and so many others removed from the midst; through the fourth letter *samech*, *Isarva*, that is, *leper*, because David, now driven from the city, appeared no different from lepers, whose company all men dread and execrate; through the fifth letter *tav* is designated *Tohaba*, which signifies *abomination*. *I will not kill thee with the sword.* Hence it is clear that when David said to Shimei, *Thou shalt not die*, he did not grant him full and unconditional pardon and impunity, but a limited and restricted one — for the duration of David's own life — as if to say: I will not kill you; throughout my whole life I will spare your life. But after my death, beware, for Solomon my son, prompted by me, will seek occasion to chastise your crime and your curse. For since this promise is not one that is owed, but freely and liberally given, it must be interpreted and limited according to the mind of the one who makes it, says the Abulensis. David therefore here commends justice to Solomon in regard to all men, but especially in regard to evildoers whom he himself had hitherto permitted to live out of clemency, so that Solomon might now give satisfaction to justice — which had been tacitly understood in David's own merciful forbearance, according to that passage in Psalm 71:1: *Give to the king thy judgment, O God, and to the king's son thy justice.* # GEM

He slept in Zion. Holy Scripture calls death a *sleep*, on account of faith and hope in the resurrection. For on the day of judgment, we shall awake from death as from sleep unto eternal life. Hence the pious custom of the faithful has it that when someone dies, they say: *He has fallen asleep in the Lord.* Moreover, Josephus, at the end of Book 7, bestows upon David this eulogy, as it were an epitaph: *David was a man of the highest excellence, endowed with all the virtues which a king who has the care of so many nations ought to possess. For he was brave beyond all others, and in conflicts undertaken for the protection of his subjects he was the first to expose himself to dangers, rousing his soldiers to glorious deeds by his own example, and compelling them to duty not as a master by his authority alone. He was likewise most prudent in counsel, and excellently foreseeing what would be profitable both in the present and in the future. Beyond this, he was sober, gentle, and kind to the afflicted, just and humane — which indeed are the foremost virtues of kings — nor in so great a power did he ever deviate from equity, save in what concerns the wife of Uriah. Furthermore, he left behind such great riches as no other king, either of the Hebrews or of other nations, had left.* Concerning his burial, Josephus immediately adds the following: *Solomon buried him in Jerusalem magnificently, beyond the usual solemnities at royal funerals, with great riches also brought into his monument — the magnitude of which may easily be conjectured from what we shall now say. For after three hundred years, the high priest Hyrcanus, besieged by Antiochus surnamed Pius, son of Demetrius, wishing to give him money to withdraw his army and lift the siege, and being unable to obtain it elsewhere, opened a chamber in the monument of David and drew out three thousand talents of silver from it, and having paid a portion of these to Antiochus, freed himself from the peril of the siege — as we have also indicated elsewhere. And again, after many more years had elapsed, King Herod, opening another chamber, took away a great sum of money. However, neither of them reached the burial chambers that held the ashes of the kings. For by singular art they had been placed so deep under the earth that they could not be found by those who entered the monument.* But let the credibility of these things rest with Josephus: for so great a multitude of talents buried in a sepulchre seems almost incredible, and the chambers would scarcely have been able to contain it. Furthermore, it is said of David in 1 Chronicles 29:28: *And he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and glory.* For he was himself the model and exemplar of all kings, and therefore in all things truly a type of Christ, as our Salianus has shown in detail for each particular in his year of the world 3021, numbers 92 and following. See also the praises of David in Ecclesiasticus 47:2 and following, where I have explained the same things.

Abishag had been the wife of King David; and therefore Adonijah, the son of David, sought her, so that through her he might rise as a kind of queen and ascend to the kingdom, and be acclaimed king by the people who favored him. That Joab had suggested this plan to Adonijah — Joab, who fostered his cause and was a man of shrewdness and cunning — is the opinion of Theodoret, Hugo, Abulensis, and Dionysius; for Joab feared Solomon, and with good reason, as will shortly become apparent. You will ask: How did Adonijah dare to seek his father's wife, when this is forbidden in Leviticus 18:7? Josephus, Cajetan, and Serarius reply that the law there forbids only the taking in marriage of one who has been known by the father. But Abishag had not been known by David; she remained a virgin. Hence her marriage was not forbidden by that Levitical law. However this may be, Adonijah, apparently ignorant of this law, seems to have thought such a marriage lawful; or else, blinded by love of Abishag's beauty and by ambition for the throne, he made light of the matter. Furthermore, as to whether by natural law it is lawful and valid for a son to marry his father's wife, the opinions of the Doctors vary. For our Thomas Sanchez, in book 7 on matrimonial impediments, disputation 66, teaches that marriage in the first degree of affinity in the direct line — for example, a father-in-law with his daughter-in-law, or a mother-in-law with her son-in-law — is not void by natural law, and therefore is dispensable. He proves this because such would have been the marriage of Adonijah with Abishag, the wife of his father David. For if this had been void, Adonijah would certainly not have sought her as his wife. Sanchez cites in favor of this opinion St. Thomas, Alensis, Abulensis, Gabriel, Tapperus, and several others. On the contrary side, Bellarmine, book 1 on Matrimony, chapter 28, teaches that such a marriage is void by natural law and indispensable, because we nowhere read of a dispensation being granted in such a case. For it is plainly contrary to natural decency and to the reverence owed to a father and mother alike. The same position is held by P. Aegidius Coninck, who, drawing from Josephus a Costa, book 6 of the Indies, chapter 21, relates that Pope Paul IV, together with the Council of Lima, decided that the native peoples of Peru who had contracted marriage with a stepmother or with a daughter-in-law were not to be admitted to Baptism unless they first dissolved such a marriage, as being void by natural law. As regards Abishag, Bellarmine replies: first, that she was not David's wife — for in Scripture she is not called a wife, but only the warming-companion of the aged king; second, that if she was his wife, Adonijah sought her unlawfully, as contrary to the law of Leviticus 18, verses 7 and 15, and was therefore justly ordered to be slain by Solomon. For here the judgment of Solomon, wisest of kings, ought to carry more weight than the petition of Adonijah. This opinion is followed by Paludanus, Soto, Valencia, Navarrus, Covarruvias, Sylvester, Vazquez, and very many others, cited by Sanchez in the place mentioned, and by Aegidius Coninck, disputation 32 on matrimonial impediments, doubt 3, conclusion 5. The question therefore remains a problem: whether the marriage of a father-in-law with a daughter-in-law, or of a son-in-law with a mother-in-law, is void by natural law. For there are weighty authorities on both sides — more, indeed, on the affirmative side, fewer on the negative. Both opinions are therefore probable, and the Pope may in practice follow either: either granting a dispensation if there be a just and weighty cause, or refusing it, as he has refused it up to now (and indeed in these very days in which I am residing at Rome), on account of the indecency of the thing; nor does there exist, to my knowledge, any public example of such a dispensation having been granted. This much is certain among all: that such a marriage is forbidden by natural law, and all the more by the old Law, as is plain from Leviticus 18:15, and 20:16, and Deuteronomy 27:20.

The Hebrew reads: "he will not turn away thy face," that is, he will not confound thee and cause thee to retire in shame by refusing what thou dost ask. For when something is denied to a person, that person, confounded, withdraws his face, turns away, and returns home. Solomon promises that he will grant all that his mother shall ask; understand this rightly, provided she asks what is fitting and beneficial — not if she asks what would be harmful to herself and to him, as was the request concerning the giving of Abishag as wife to Adonijah. # GEM If Solomon fulfilled all the requests of his mother, how much more does Christ grant to His mother all that she seeks in supplication on our behalf. Let us therefore approach her, and through her obtain all things. For Christ will not confound and put to shame the face of His mother, as St. Bernard frequently and earnestly insists; nor indeed can she ask, nor does she ask, anything that is unworthy of the dignity of her Son or of her own.

That is to say: If thou dost ask that Abishag be given as wife to Adonijah, thou art asking the kingdom for him. For he covets the wife of our father King David, so that through her he may be elevated as a kind of queen to royal eminence and become king; and this the more so because Abiathar the High Priest and Joab the commander of the army are aiding him toward this end. So say Rupert and others. Verse 25: "And King Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah" — that is, he sent Benaiah, so that by his hand Adonijah might be slain. This is a Hebraism. Cajetan asserts that Solomon sinned by rashness of judgment in condemning his brother on a single charge of treason, unless this was indeed the custom. But even a single act, says Serarius, suffices today if the matter be certain, especially where there is danger to the king of being deprived of his kingdom by a rival, as was the case here. Add to this that Solomon here punished the first usurpation of the kingdom committed by Adonijah, when he had caused himself to be anointed and crowned king. For although Solomon had pardoned him for that act, yet he had done so conditionally, on the understanding that Adonijah would henceforth be faithful to him and, remaining quietly at home, would give no further grounds for suspicion of ambition for the throne he had unlawfully claimed; and that if he gave such grounds, he would be punished not only for the suspicion but also for the former invasion of the kingdom. Now Adonijah, by seeking Abishag as his wife, gave Solomon just cause to suspect that he was again seeking the kingdom. For why else would he so shamelessly and arrogantly, against both natural and divine law, seek the queen — his own father's wife — unless to seize through her the throne of his father and become king? He was therefore justly punished by Solomon. For Solomon feared him as an ambitious and restless man, one who would always be scheming revolution and aspiring to the kingdom which he claimed by right of primogeniture; and he knew that while Adonijah lived, he himself would never be secure upon his throne. He therefore slew him in order to secure his own safety and the peace of his kingdom. Consider here again how unhappy David was in his sons. For Amnon violated Tamar; Absalom killed him and rebelled against his father; Adonijah coveted the kingdom, and for this was slain by his brother. This was the punishment and penance inflicted upon David by God for his adultery and the murder of Uriah, as foretold in 2 Kings 12:10, where God says: "The sword shall never depart from thy house." Behold how brief and slight a pleasure bore for him such ruin, and dragged behind it so long a train of calamities. Verse 26: "And to Abiathar the priest (the high priest) the king said: Go to Anathoth, to thine own fields, for thou art worthy of death." That is to say: Thou hast deserved to be slain, because thou didst conspire against me with Adonijah and didst anoint him king. Yet I will not slay thee, because thou didst bear the ark of the Lord before David my father; but I will only banish thee from Jerusalem, lest thou again stir up new troubles among my brethren and citizens against me. Go therefore to thine own land in Anathoth, and there live quietly and attend to thine own affairs, and henceforth meddle not in mine or in the kingdom's business. Now by this very act Solomon indirectly deposed and stripped Abiathar of the pontificate; for the high priest was required to reside in Jerusalem in order to direct the priests ministering in the Temple, to preside over the Synagogue and the council of the Sanhedrin, to serve the king as counselor, to teach the people, and so forth. By banishing Abiathar, therefore, Solomon appointed Zadok in his place.

In 1 Samuel 2:30, God threatens Eli and his descendants with the loss of life and of the pontificate, and adds in verse 35: "And I will raise me up a faithful priest" — namely, Zadok. Solomon therefore, a wise man, taking advantage of this occasion of Abiathar's rebellion, and moved by the instinct of God, fulfilled these threats of God against Eli and this promise of restoring Zadok to the pontificate; and thus he transferred the pontificate from the family of Ithamar and Eli — from which Abiathar descended — back to the original and legitimate line of Eleazar, from which Zadok descended. He did this as a punishment for the sins of both Eli and Abiathar; for Abiathar was guilty of a capital crime and of treason, in that he had favored Adonijah against Solomon, who had been established as king by God and by his father David. Solomon therefore drove him into exile — an honorable exile nonetheless; for he sent him to his homeland, to his own estates, there to live out in peace the life which he graciously spared. Let no one therefore conclude from this that emperors and kings have authority over Pontiffs, such that they may create and depose them. For Solomon deposed Abiathar not by his own authority, but — as the authority in question judges — by the authority of God the Pontiff. For God had originally assigned the pontificate to Aaron, and then to Eleazar as the firstborn, and to his descendants. Thence the pontificate had deviated to Eli, who was of the line of Ithamar; God therefore commanded that it be restored to the family of Eleazar, namely to Zadok. And this Solomon did by the instinct and command of God, not as a King, but as a Prophet, as an executor of divine justice, as Bellarmine shows, book 2 on the Pontiff, chapter 29. Add to this that now under the new law the pontificate is far more sacred and exalted than it was under the old; and therefore, even if the pontiff were then subject to the king — as Abulensis holds — he is so no longer. For he is now the Vicar of Christ, who is the King of kings and Lord of lords. See what has been said at Numbers 27:21.

This reading agrees with the Hebrew, Chaldean, Septuagint, Roman, and other versions. It is therefore incorrectly read in royal and certain other editions as: *But a messenger came to Solomon.* The meaning is as follows: A messenger came from Joab, informing him that Adonijah had been slain, so that Joab himself, who had favored Adonijah and conspired with him against Solomon, might take thought for his own flight, lest he too, as an associate and partner in Adonijah's conspiracy, be put to death by Solomon. **After Solomon** — this is how it should be read, with the Septuagint, Roman, and other versions. The Hebrew and Chaldean, however, have *after Absalom*, which Vatablus explains thus: that is, Joab, though faithful to David at the beginning and not having defected after Absalom, was nevertheless afterward unfaithful, because he conspired with Adonijah against Solomon, whom David had established as king. Others hold that Solomon is called Absalom, on the ground that Absalom in Hebrew means the same as *father of peace*, and Solomon likewise means *peacemaker*, and therefore both are father and arbiter of peace. Thus Ochozias was called Joachaz by metathesis. It is more probable that the modern Hebrew and Chaldean texts are corrupt, and that St. Jerome read in the Hebrew *after Solomon* rather than *after Absalom*. For so the Septuagint read it, and the antithesis demands it. So Sanchez. **I will not go out** — Joab hoped that the sanctity of the place would protect him, and that Solomon's reverence would not permit him to be slain there, and therefore he refused to depart. But the expectation deceived him: on account of the atrocity of his crimes he was slain on that very spot.

— in the Tabernacle, from which Joab refuses to go forth, but wishes to be cut down and to die there. For a voluntary homicide, such as Joab was, who treacherously slew Abner and Amasa, did not enjoy the immunity and protection of a sacred place, according to the text: *If any man shall kill his neighbor on purpose and by lying in wait, thou shalt take him away from my altar that he may die* (Exodus 21). This principle is much more firmly established, not by divine law only but by human law in the New Testament, in which Christians are bound to be holier than Jews and further removed from wilful homicide. See, among other places, the chapter *Inter alia* on the immunity of churches, and Father Suarez, his treatise on Ecclesiastical Immunity, chapters 9 and 10. Moreover, there was at that time no law in force that the Tabernacle would be polluted by human bloodshed, whereas such a law has now been enacted for the Christian Church, so that a church in which a murder has been committed is deemed polluted and must be re-blessed. Justly, says Salvianus, could that altar, which was perpetually accustomed to be stained with blood, be profaned by shed blood; the entire violation of the place consisted in the violence which directly and in passing violated the privilege of the place. Hence it is certain that places of asylum are so called because it is not lawful to drag the guilty from their bosom, as it were. Therefore Solomon rightly judged it to be all one whether Joab were slain at the altar or were dragged thence and slaughtered elsewhere; for by either course the place was violated, unless a dispensation of the law intervened — which, given the enormity of his crimes, was not here required, as I have said. Semei was guilty of death on two counts. First, because he had transgressed the Jordan contrary to the command of Solomon, which had been given and accepted by him under pain of death. So Theodoret, Josephus, Serarius, and others. Second, because he had cursed David; and Solomon especially intended to punish this in accordance with his father's precept — yet because David his father had throughout his whole life granted Semei pardon, Solomon was unwilling simply to overturn the pardon given by his father, but rather to limit it by an honorable condition; which condition, since Semei violated it, enabled Solomon to abrogate the father's pardon and to revive Semei's guilt and punishment. # GEM **The Lord has returned your wickedness upon your own head** — that is to say: God so ordered all things that the punishment of the wickedness and the curse wherewith you cursed my father David should fall back upon your own guilty head. For so He disposed all events that you, by accepting the death-penalty appointed for you should you cross the Jordan, and then crossing it, became the very author of your own condemnation and made yourself deserving of death. Thus Theodoret, Josephus, Serarius, and others.

Eupolemus, as cited by Eusebius in his *Praeparatio Evangelica*, chapter last, asserts that this Pharaoh was the one surnamed Vaphres, who is distinct from and far earlier than the Pharaoh Vaphres, or Ephree, or Apries, who was the grandson of Pharaoh Necho, the slayer of King Josiah of Judah, and the last of the Egyptian kings, having been conquered and subdued by Nebuchadnezzar, as I noted at Jeremiah, book 5, verse 30. Solomon wished through this marriage to bind Pharaoh to himself, as a neighboring and powerful king, by whose help he might establish his kingdom and secure it against the power and might of the Chaldeans, Assyrians, Medes, and others. Moreover, this marriage between a believer and an unbeliever was lawful, both because God had expressly forbidden the Israelites only from taking as wives the daughters of the Canaanites — from whom the danger of perversion and idolatry was greatest — but not the women of other nations, even though He sufficiently showed that this too was displeasing to Him on account of similar danger, as will be apparent in chapter 11, verse 1; and also because it is probable that this Egyptian bride of Solomon was converted to Judaism, by which act it was lawful to marry her, for this God permits in Deuteronomy 21, verse 20 and following. For thus Solomon had already taken Naamah the Ammonitess, and David had taken the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur, and Boaz had taken Ruth the Moabitess, and Salmon had taken Rahab of Jericho, and Joseph had taken the daughter of Potiphera the Egyptian, and Moses had taken the daughter of Jethro the Midianite. So hold Abulensis, Tineda, Serarius, Salianus, Sanchez, and others. Less correctly, therefore, do Hebraei Sederolam, Theodoret, and Procopius judge that Solomon sinned by marrying a foreign woman, on the grounds that God had forbidden it in Exodus 24 and Deuteronomy 7:3. The Hebrews believe that the Epithalamium for these nuptials of Solomon with the daughter of Pharaoh is sung in the Song of Songs; but they err, as I showed in my commentary on the Canticle. I confess, however, that there are allusions to those nuptials therein, as when it says in Canticle 1:8: *I have compared thee, O my love, to my company of horses in Pharaoh's chariots*; and in chapter 3:11: *Go forth, O ye daughters of Sion, and behold king Solomon in the diadem wherewith his mother crowned him in the day of his espousals*; which words, though they apply literally to the nuptials of Christ with the Church, yet allude to the marriage of Solomon with the Egyptian woman, which was a type of those heavenly nuptials. Finally, that Solomon married this Egyptian woman in the first year of his reign, while David was still living, is the opinion of Josephus, Serarius, and Sanchez, although Abulensis, Cedrenus, and Salianus assign it to the third year. UNTIL HE HAD FINISHED BUILDING HIS OWN HOUSE. For Solomon, after having built the Temple, built his own house for the daughter of Pharaoh, as is told in chapter 7:8; in the meantime he assigned her a place in his palace, which was in the citadel of Sion.

One may ask what these high places were, of which such frequent mention is made in the books of Kings and the Prophets. I answer: The high places were groves, that is, woods and thickets planted on high mountains, hills, and shady recesses, in which most nations were accustomed to erect shrines and altars; as Homer, Virgil, and others attest, who teach that the ancients built sepulchres for their dead in these groves, in which they first celebrated funeral rites, or performed obsequies for the heroes of the people, and soon began to offer sacrifice to these same persons as though they were gods. They did this partly because high places are loftier than others and nearer to heaven, and therefore inspire greater reverence among an uncultivated people. Hence the altar (*altare*) takes its name from height (*altitudo*), as though it were a high platform (*alta ara*) on which sacrifice is offered to the Most High God; and the Greek word *βωμός*, meaning altar, is derived from the Hebrew *Bama*, meaning a high place — though strictly speaking the altar was not itself the high place, for Scripture in 4 Kings 23:15 distinguishes between the altar and the high place; rather, the altar stood upon the high place, that is, upon a hill or mountain. Furthermore, groves, being dense and silent, inspired in the Gentiles a certain sacred awe, so that superstition led them to believe that the gods whom they called Satyrs and Fauns — as though sylvan deities — dwelt therein. Hence temples were called *fana* from the Fauns, unless one prefers to derive the name from *fando*, that is, from giving oracles. Thirdly, woods supply abundant timber for building shrines, altars, and pyres on which victims are burned; and for this reason they erected high places within them. Finally, woods, being leafy and pleasant, enticed the Gentiles to celebrate their sacred rites therein, wherein through banquets and feasting they served Bacchus, and through licentiousness served Venus and Priapus. Thus the Persians, as Xenophon attests in book 8, sacrificed in high places; the same is taught of others by Apollonius in book 2 on Mountains. The same is evident from the Capitol, on which they worshipped Jupiter Capitolinus. Hear Virgil, book 8 of the *Aeneid*: *Hence to the Tarpeian seat and the Capitol he leads, now golden, once rough with woodland thickets. Even then religion struck dread in the timid country folk; even then they trembled at the grove and the rock. This grove*, he says, *this hill with its leafy summit — what God is uncertain — a God inhabits it; the Arcadians themselves believe they have seen Jupiter here.* The same Virgil in Eclogue 7 enumerates the trees sacred to the various gods in these groves: *The poplar is most dear to Alcides, the vine to Bacchus, the myrtle to fair Venus, his own laurel to Phoebus.* That the Canaanites likewise worshipped their gods in high places and groves is clear from the fact that God repeatedly commanded the Hebrews to burn and destroy them, as is evident from Exodus 34:13, Deuteronomy 7:5, Deuteronomy 12:2, and Deuteronomy 16:21; in which passages God clearly and expressly forbids the Hebrews to maintain high places such as the Canaanites had, in which they worshipped their false gods; and indirectly He also forbids them from sacrificing to Him on high places in woods and groves, commanding them to do so only at the Tabernacle and at the altar appointed and constructed by Moses for that purpose, and soon at the Temple to be built by Solomon. The high places of the Hebrews, therefore, of which frequent mention is made in the books of Kings and Prophets, were of two kinds: those in which, after the manner of the Gentiles, they worshipped their gods and idols — these were manifestly unlawful.

Hence Theodoret, Procopius, Abulensis, and Ribera, in book 1 on the Temple, chapter 2, hold that before the building of the Temple it was unlawful to sacrifice in high places, and that it was at least a venial sin; for this reason Solomon is here reproved for offering sacrifice in the high places, even though in all other things he loved the Lord and kept His commandments. The Hebrews, however, Lyra, Serarius, and others hold that this was lawful — not as absolutely pleasing to God, but as conceded and permitted by Him until the permanent and stable Temple should be built. So likewise St. Augustine in question 36 on Judges: *God*, he says, *tolerated rather than forbade the custom of His people, who, apart from His Tabernacle, were nonetheless not offering to strange gods but to the Lord their God; He bore with those who thus offered, even while they were going beyond bounds.* After the building of the Temple, however, high places were unlawful; and for this reason David, Hezekiah, and Josiah removed them, although Asa, Amaziah, and Jehoshaphat tolerated them for fear of a greater evil, lest namely, if the high places of God were removed, the people might be drawn to the high places of the Gentiles' gods, that is, to idols. Hence it is said of these kings: *He did right in the sight of the Lord, etc., but yet the high places he took not away*; chapter 15, verse 11 and 14, etc. 4. AND HE WENT TO GIBEON TO SACRIFICE THERE; for at Gibeon was the Tabernacle and altar of Moses, and therefore it was lawful to sacrifice there; and for this reason it is straightway called the great high place, that is, the greatest and most sacred, even though the Ark of the Covenant was not at Gibeon, but had been translated from Kirjath-jearim by David to Sion, as is expressly stated in 2 Chronicles 1, verses 4 and 5. Note here the piety of Solomon, who inaugurates his reign with the worship and invocation of God; for he offered a thousand victims, as follows, and therefore obtained from God a wisdom so great as God had never granted to any man before. # GEM

One may ask what manner of dream this was for Solomon, and whether in it he had the use of free will, so that he freely asked wisdom of God and thereby obtained it. This question is a matter of dispute. For many affirm it, many deny it, and both sides adduce probable arguments in their favor. First, then, some hold that this dream was a natural one, arising from the images of daytime thoughts recurring naturally in sleep at night; or that it was naturally sent by God to this end: that it might be known that wisdom is to be sought above all things by all men, but especially by kings and princes for the wise governance of their realms, as being supremely necessary for good government. So holds Abulensis. For since Solomon during the day, while praying and sacrificing at Gibeon, had frequently and urgently begged wisdom of God, God therefore brought it about that the same thoughts naturally recurred to him at night in sleep; so that Solomon, while sleeping, seemed to himself to be asking for wisdom, and God to be granting it. In accordance with that saying of Claudian: *All the thoughts and feelings stirred in the daytime hours, friendly slumber restores in the hours of night.* The same is taught by Aristotle in his book on Dreams and Hippocrates in his book on Visions. If one objects that this request of Solomon pleased God and that through it he truly obtained wisdom, they reply that this request made in sleep did not in itself please God, because it was the act of one dreaming, not of one consciously petitioning; but that it pleased God on account of the same Solomon's request for wisdom made while awake, from which that dream took its origin and naturally flowed forth as an effect from its cause. So St. Thomas in 1.2, question 40, article 3, ad 2: *Solomon*, he says, *did not merit wisdom by sleeping, nor receive it, but it was declared in sleep that wisdom would be infused into him by God by reason of his preceding desire.* He repeats the same in *De Veritate*, question 28, article 3, and in 2.2, question 154, article 5, ad 1. St. Augustine favors this view in book 12 of *The Literal Meaning of Genesis*, chapter 15, whom St. Thomas cites. Likewise St. Bonaventure in book 2, distinction 15, question 6, number 72; Alexander of Hales in question 101, member 3, article 3; Richard in *Quodlibet* 1, question 17; Sanchez, Salianus, Serarius, Pineda in book 3 on Solomon, chapter 8; Pererius in book 1 on Daniel, question 7. This is proved first, because Solomon was asleep and in a dream; therefore all these things were presented to him not in reality but through a dream. Secondly, because in verse 15 Scripture explicitly says: *And Solomon awoke, and understood that it was a dream.* Yet the contrary opinion is no less probable, and indeed brings forward stronger arguments: namely, that this dream was not natural but divine, and properly sent by God (though with nature and the natural daytime thoughts of Solomon also serving God's purpose), and that therefore it was not so much a dream as a prophetic vision or ecstasy, such as was the sleep of Adam when God formed Eve from his rib — which the Septuagint calls an ecstasy; for in it Adam, by divine revelation, knew of the Incarnation of Christ and the formation of the Church from His side, as St. Augustine, St. Thomas, Rupert, and others commonly teach. Such also was the dream of Abraham in Genesis 15:12, in which God revealed and promised to Abraham a great posterity and the possession of Canaan. Such likewise was the dream of Joseph concerning his future sovereignty in Egypt, Genesis 37:5, and the dream of Jacob, Genesis 28:12, in which God promised him the same things He had promised Abraham, and indeed more. This is proved first, because Scripture clearly signifies this when it says: *And the Lord appeared to Solomon* —

That is to say: I am very young, unformed and inexperienced, and therefore like a child unequal of myself to so great a burden, namely the governance of so great a people as Israel. For Solomon was at this time twenty years of age, as I said above. Explaining this, he adds: *Not knowing my going out and my coming in.* This is a Hebrew idiom signifying all the actions of a man — as if to say: I do not know how I ought to conduct myself in the administration of so great a kingdom. Hence in 2 Chronicles 1:10 he says: *Give me wisdom and understanding, that I may go out and come in before thy people, as their king and shepherd.* The Chaldean renders it: *I know not how to go out nor to come in.* He alludes to the child (which he called himself) who, taught by his mother, learns to go out and come in about the house.

That is, receiving doctrine — namely prudence and wisdom — so that he might hear it and draw it into himself: a prudent, understanding and wise heart. In Hebrew it is *a hearing heart*, one that listens and receives wisdom. Hence 2 Chronicles 1:10, explaining this, says: *Give me wisdom and understanding.* And here at verse 11, God answering him says: *Thou hast asked for thyself wisdom*, etc. Now Solomon in asking here for wisdom properly asks for prudence, by which he might wisely govern both himself and the whole people — that is, that he might prudently order all his actions, both private and public, according to the law and will of God. Hence in Wisdom 9:4 and 10, explaining this his petition for wisdom, he says: *Give me wisdom that sitteth by thy throne*, etc., *that she may be with me and labour with me, that I may know what is acceptable before thee.* He gives the reason at verse 17, saying: *Who shall know thy sense, except thou give wisdom and send thy Holy Spirit from above?* See what is said there. For wisdom in the Sapiential books signifies chiefly practical or ethical wisdom, which teaches us to live wisely, that is justly and holily; and it is accordingly called in Wisdom 10 the *knowledge of the Saints*, and in Luke 1, the *prudence of the just*. God, however, who surpasses the prayers and vows of his own, granted to Solomon a far more ample wisdom, as I shall now say. *I have given thee a wise and understanding heart* (that is, a mind) — the Chaldean adds *and considering*; the Septuagint, *and skilled* — *insomuch that there hath been no one like thee before thee, nor shall any arise after thee.* In 2 Chronicles 1:12 it is said: *Wisdom and knowledge are given to thee.* By these we are to understand: first, Ethics and Politics, for governing both himself and the people — and this especially was what Solomon had asked for; second, Natural Philosophy, Medicine, Logic, Rhetoric, Poetry, Mathematics, Architecture (for this he needed in order to build the temple), and all the natural sciences and arts. For these are called Wisdom by the Hebrews, as is plain from Exodus 31:3. Concerning these he himself says in Wisdom 7:17: *For he hath given me the true knowledge of the things that are, to know the disposition of the whole world, and the virtues of the elements, the beginning and ending and midst of the times, the alterations of their courses and the changes of seasons, the revolutions of the year and the dispositions of the stars, the natures of living creatures and the furies of wild beasts, the force of winds and the reasonings of men, the diversities of plants and the virtues of roots, and all such things as are hid and manifest I have learned: for wisdom, which is the worker of all things, taught me.* Third, the supernatural wisdom and prudence of the mysteries of faith, and even the gift of prophecy. That Solomon was an outstanding theologian is plain from his Proverbs and Wisdom; that he was also a prophet is plain from Proverbs 30:1 — see what is said there. All this knowledge, therefore, Solomon received infused by God on that night, and in a very intense degree, such that he could not forget it. So Abulensis, Serarius, Pineda, Salianus, and others. Josephus adds that Solomon learned from God exorcisms for conjuring and expelling demons, and many other things, of which Origen speaks in Tractate 35 on Matthew; Justin in the Dialogue on the Truth of the Christian Religion; and Epiphanius on the heresy of the Ebionites. See Baronius at the year of Christ 56, and Sixtus of Siena in the *Bibliotheca* under the entry for Solomon. Hence some hold that there was granted to Solomon a freely given grace (*gratia gratis data*) for composing exorcisms by which the demon might be driven out. Josephus adds further that Solomon...

# GEM Vatablus and others explain it thus: that Solomon, waking, remembered his dream, or that he had dreamed about wisdom — supposing it to have been not a real thing but a fleeting, deceptive and vanishing dream. But this cannot be said: for Solomon truly obtained wisdom here, as is plain from the words of Scripture at verse 12: *I have given thee*, it says, *a wise and understanding heart*, etc. — wherefore this interpretation directly contradicts Holy Scripture and destroys and overturns it. I say, therefore: Solomon waking understood that it was a dream, namely not a natural dream but a supernatural and divine one — that is, a vision truly and efficaciously communicated together with the very thing itself, namely with the wisdom sent down by God and instilled into him. Hence the Hebrew, the Septuagint, and the Chaldean all read: *Therefore Solomon awoke, and behold it was a dream* — that is, it had achieved its effect and appeared as real; as if to say: Solomon, upon waking, saw that he had truly been made wise, just as he had dreamed, that is, just as while sleeping he had seen through a divine vision. For he saw the forms of all things presenting themselves and hovering before his mind, by which he clearly perceived matters of every kind which he had not known before. He understood, therefore, that this dream had been real and true, inasmuch as it was supernatural and sent by God together with the thing dreamed. Hence he presently resolved most wisely the disputed case of the two harlots, and administered all other matters most wisely, as is plain from what follows. So Suárez, Serarius, Pineda. Similar dreams — not dreamed and false, but real and true — are found in Holy Scripture: as when Saint Peter, about to be freed from prison by an Angel, was bidden to dress himself and go out, and thought he was seeing a vision and dreaming (Acts 12:9), and yet was truly dressing himself and going out. So the Hebrews freed from Babylon, returning to Jerusalem, say: *When the Lord turned again the captivity of Sion, we were like those who are consoled* — in Hebrew, *like those who dream* — as if to say: So joyful, new, wondrous and unexpected was our return from exile to our homeland that it seemed to be a dream, so that we thought ourselves not truly returning but dreaming of a return. So too Solomon here, on waking, seemed to himself to have been dreaming; but presently he saw that the dream had been turned into reality. Thus also Saint Jerome, reproved by God in a dream for reading Cicero in preference to Holy Scripture, and hearing the words *Thou art a Ciceronian, not a Christian*, was sharply beaten and scourged — not in a dream, but in very truth. Hear him in his Letter 18 to Eustochium: *Nor was that a sleep or vain dreams, by which we are often deluded. I call to witness that tribunal before which I lay; I call to witness that dreadful judgment which I feared. God grant that I may never again fall into such an enquiry. I confess that my shoulders were livid, that I felt the blows after waking, and that thereafter I read the divine writings with an ardour such as I had never before given to mortal books.* And when he had come (from Gibeon, where he had asked wisdom of God and obtained it) to Jerusalem, he stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord and offered holocausts, in thanksgiving for so great a gift of wisdom bestowed upon him by God. For the Ark was in Sion, but the Tabernacle and the altar were at Gibeon, as was said at verse 4.

God so disposing it, in order to make manifest to Israel the wisdom which had a little before been instilled into Solomon by him, through the shrewd resolution of this dispute between the harlots. In place of *harlots*, the Hebrew has *muzonot*, which the Chaldean renders as *inn-keepers* or *tavern-keepers*; for *zona*, or harlot, is said of one who earns her living by prostituting either food, or services, or her body — such as a tavern-keeper, a washerwoman, a seamstress, a weaver, a purple-seller, who gains her livelihood by her craft and labour. And our commentator Sanchez holds these two to have been of this sort, because harlots in the proper sense scarcely conceive children, on account of their promiscuous intercourse and commingling of seed, since they prostitute themselves to any man. The case is otherwise with concubines, who cleave to one lover alone; for these conceive just as wives do. However, Saint Gregory, Ambrose, Prosper, Pineda, Salianus and others commonly hold that these two were harlots in the proper sense, as the Septuagint and the Vulgate render — and this is argued by the fact that no mention is made here of husbands, but the women themselves contest the suit on their own behalf. For harlots, being avaricious, greedy, shameless and quarrelsome, are likewise for that very reason litigious, and indeed the cause of many lawsuits, brawls, duels, wounds and wars, as daily experience teaches.

Because, namely, by rolling over she lay upon the infant and suffocated him by the weight of her body. For this reason, Canon Law forbids mothers from sleeping with infants, on account of the danger of overlying them, as is clear from the canon *Consuluit* (2, q. 5). CONTENDED BEFORE THE KING: because there were no witnesses, nor other proofs and judgments; hence these little women contended among themselves in their customary manner, with strife of voice and clamor. *Allegorically*: these two women represent the Synagogue of the Jews and the Church of Christians, who contend with one another concerning the child Jesus and the true and living Messiah. But Solomon — that is, Christ — settles the dispute, saying in Matthew 10: *I came not to send peace, but a sword*. For he came to divide the Law from grace, the Jews from the Christians, the faithful from the unfaithful, as St. Jerome beautifully teaches in his Epistle 101 to Pammachius, where he also adds that Solomon rendered this judgment in the twelfth year of his age, for he had begun to reign at that age; but above I have shown that the event took place in the twentieth year of his age. Again, they represent the Catholic Church and the Arian heresy, the Nestorian, and all the others, which divide Christ into two, so that in him one is God and another is man — and these heresies strive to appropriate to themselves, and to drag into their own error, the true faithful who are the living children of the Church, and thereby to destroy and kill them. So says St. Augustine, Sermon 200, *De Tempore*: *The impious and cruel heresy*, he says, *cries out that he be divided. What does "be divided" mean, unless that the Son is not equal to the Father? For in denying equality to the Son, it also denies that the Father is good and omnipotent. For if God the Father could beget a Son equal to himself and would not, he is not good; if he willed it and could not, he is not omnipotent. But the true mother, namely the Catholic Church, cries out: Give the child to her, and do not divide him. He is my Son, but let him go — let him go entirely — yet let the maternal affection remain with me. Give her the child; let not the substance of his members be taken away, let the wholeness of the Son not be divided, lest piety be taken from me. In saying: Give her the child and do not divide him — behold, I too say: Possess him wholly, and do not divide God.* These words Augustine transcribed nearly verbatim, and after him Angelomus, Eucherius, or rather Bede, and St. Prosper in his book *De Praedictis*. *Tropologically*: St. Gregory, in book 21 of the *Moralia*, chapter 8, takes these two women to represent true and false teachers. For true and good teachers seek the salvation of their disciples, while false and wicked ones seek their own glory and gain, even at the cost of their disciples' ruin. *For those masters*, says St. Gregory, *who are indeed wakeful in knowledge but sleeping in their manner of life, slay through the slumber of their torpor those hearers whom they nourish through the vigils of preaching — for while they neglect to practice what they preach, they crush by neglect those whom they seemed to feed with the milk of words. Hence very often, since they live reprehensibly and are unable to possess disciples of praiseworthy life, they endeavor to attract those belonging to others; so that while they show themselves to have good followers, they excuse before the judgment of men the evil they do, and, as it were, through the disordered life of their subjects, conceal their own negligence. Hence the woman there sought another's child because she had destroyed her own; yet the sword of Solomon found the true mother — because the last judgment of the strict Judge reveals whose fruit lives, and who truly cares.* St. Gregory then adds this further noteworthy point: that the living son is first commanded to be divided, so that afterwards he might be restored to his mother alone — because in this life the life of a disciple is, as it were, permitted to be shared, while sometimes from that life one or another draws benefit. # GEM

For nature and natural compassion judge with certainty that this is the true mother. Mark here the shrewdness and wisdom of Solomon. And therefore, says St. Ambrose, book 2, *De Officiis*, chapter 8: *not without reason was it esteemed that the understanding of God was in him, in whom the hidden things of God dwell. For what is more hidden than the testimony of the inward depths? — into which the understanding of the wise man descends as a certain arbiter of piety, and draws forth, as it were, a voice from the womb of birth, by which the maternal affection was revealed that had chosen that her own son should live even with a stranger, rather than be slain in the sight of his mother. It belonged therefore to Wisdom to distinguish hidden consciences, to bring forth truth from secret places, and, as with a certain engine of war, so to pierce with the sword of the spirit not only the recesses of the womb, but also the inward parts of the soul and mind. It belonged also to Justice that she who had slain her own child should not take another's, but that the true mother should receive her own.* Finally, even Scripture pronounces this verdict: *All Israel*, it says, *heard this judgment which the king had judged, and they feared before the king, because they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to do justice.* CHAPTER FOUR

That is, of the chief priests, namely the pontiffs. You will say: Solomon had already dismissed Abiathar and deprived him of the pontificate in chapter 2, verse 27. The answer is that all the officials of Solomon are here enumerated together, whether they were then in office, or had been, or were yet to be, as will be evident from verses 11 and 15. For Scripture wished to enumerate them all together at this point. He names Abiathar, therefore, because he had been pontiff in the time of David, and David had commended him to Solomon as faithful to himself; but because he had conspired with Adonijah against Solomon, Solomon accordingly deprived him of the pontificate. So says the Abulensis. Others respond that Abiathar was recalled from exile by Solomon, not to the pontificate, but to simple priesthood, as God had foretold by way of threatening to his great-great-grandfather Eli in 1 Kings 2:36: "It shall come to pass that whosoever remains of your house, etc., shall say: Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priestly offices." So say Theodoret and Procopius. Thirdly, Serarius responds that Abiathar is called a priest because he had been a priest. Verse 5: Zabud the son of Nathan, a priest — that is, the intimate prince of the king, as the Septuagint renders it. So too elsewhere David's men are called priests, that is, courtiers and those closest to the king, in 2 Samuel 8, final verse. Verse 11: Tapheth the daughter of Solomon he had as wife. This is a prolepsis, for he did not yet have her, but would have her later. For Solomon at this time was only about twenty years of age, and thus could not yet have a daughter of marriageable age. The same I say regarding Achimaas, who had another of Solomon's daughters as his wife, in verse 15. For all the officials and princes of Solomon are here enumerated together, whether they were then serving, or became so afterwards, as I said at verse 4. Verse 20: Judah and Israel, innumerable as the sand of the sea — that is, in the time of Solomon, the tribe of Judah and the remaining eleven tribes of Israel grew to an almost innumerable multitude; and there was then the greatest abundance of all things, so that all rejoiced and exulted in their wealth and pleasures. Allegorically, the felicity of Solomon's kingdom represented the supreme felicity of Christ's kingdom: in the Church through grace, in heaven through glory. Verse 21: Solomon moreover was sovereign over all the kingdoms from the river of the land of the Philistines to the border of Egypt. The question is asked: what is this river of the land of the Philistines? First, Gaspar Sanchez considers it to be the sea of the Palestinians, for the Philistines are adjacent to it. Second, the Abulensis considers it to be the river of Rhinocolura, which separates Philistia from Arabia. Third, others consider it to be the Jordan, or rather the source of the Jordan in Lebanon, where the two streams Jor and Dan arise, which presently converging form the Jordan. But I say that this river is the Euphrates. For the phrase "the land of the Philistines" is not to be referred to the word "river," as is clear from the Hebrew (for in Hebrew the word is nahar in the absolute state, not nahar in the construct state, as it would need to be if it governed the genitive "of the land"), but rather is to be referred to "kingdoms," meaning: Solomon held all the kingdoms of the land of the Philistines, from the river — that is, those lying between the river Euphrates all the way to the land of the Philistines inclusive; or, Solomon held all the kingdoms of the regions beginning from the river Euphrates as far as the land of the Philistines. He preferred to say "as far as" rather than "to the land," both to indicate that the kingdom of Solomon was not terminated in the land of the Philistines but extended through it as far as the borders of Egypt — meaning, from the river, passing through the land of the Philistines as far as the borders of Egypt — and also because he speaks concisely, and from other, as it were well-known, passages of Scripture concisely marks out the borders of Solomon's kingdom as promised by God. For Scripture sets the boundaries...
This Ethan was a distinguished poet and musician, as is clear from 1 Chronicles 15:19, and likewise Psalm 88 is prefaced with the title: The Understanding of Ethan.

A portion of these we have in the Book of Proverbs, where the parables begin at chapter 10 and extend to the end of the book through 658 verses, which contain an almost equal number of parables. And his songs were a thousand and five — five above a thousand. So the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Josephus agree: that is, the Book of the Odes of Solomon contained a thousand and five odes. The Septuagint however renders it: his songs were five thousand. From this it is clear that Solomon was a poet. Ancient Hebrew metre, however, forbids it; nor do we now observe metrical feet in the Psalms and other songs, except in rhythm.

That is, from the greatest things down to the least. By hyssop here understand the wall-plant, which some in the Abulensis call origanum, or else sparton. Levinus Lemnius, in chapter 26 on herbs, and Pineda call it adiantum; Serarius calls it savory, which is for that reason called in Arabic isatur. And he discoursed of beasts, and of fowl, and of creeping things, and of fishes. It is probable that these matters were once written down in books, just as Ulysses Aldrovandus, Conrad Gesner, William Rondelet, Julius Scaliger, and others have in recent times written at length on the same subjects. But all these writings have almost entirely perished over the interval of so many centuries, chiefly because printing did not then exist, but only a few manuscript copies, which perish easily. So too have perished all the writings of the Patriarchs and of the Sages of the Hebrews, the Egyptians, the Chaldeans, the Arabs, and the Greeks before Moses, of whose ancient age almost his writings alone survive. The Abulensis adds that by divine providence they perished, either because they contained matters too curious, or because we were unworthy of so great a light of knowledge as he had received through revelation. The Gloss considers that they were burned by the Chaldeans; Eusebius however, and following him Glycas, say by King Hezekiah. From this it is clear that Solomon both privately taught his own household and publicly instructed all in natural science and the knowledge of natural things, for this is what is signified by the expression "he disputed and discoursed." To this end he even built a school or academy on Mount Sion, in which he himself taught, as I noted at Proverbs 9:1, from which Pythagoras, Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle drew their doctrine, as Eusebius, Saint Ambrose, Clement of Alexandria, and others attest. Psellus adds that Solomon wrote books on precious stones, and on spirits or demons — how to summon them, confine them to certain places, and compel them to work. But certain magicians also produce Solomon's incantations, his clavicle and his ring; all of these however are magical and unworthy of Solomon. See Pineda.

As if offering congratulations to a new king upon his accession. Josephus and Eupolemus, cited by Eusebius in book 9 of the *Praeparatio Evangelica*, record letters exchanged between Hiram and Solomon, taken from the archives of the Tyrians. The title of these letters reads: *Suron to Solomon the great king, greeting*, wherein he calls him a great king — a king of kings, as it were — on account of his wisdom no less than his wealth and power. Hiram in Hebrew and in the Septuagint is called Chiram or Chiura, as if Chirom or Tyrom, that is, king of the Tyrians, according to Serarius. By the Phoenicians, Hiram is called Surom (for Tyre in Hebrew is called צׂר, *sor*, that is, a rock, because it is situated upon a cliff — whence Tyrian purple is called Sarranean purple), and by Theophilus of Antioch he is called Hiermus and Hieromenus, whose daughter, Tatian asserts, was given in marriage to Solomon. By Josephus, in book 8 of the *Antiquities*, chapter 2, and in book 1 *Against Apion*, he is called Iromus, son of Abibaal. **AND THERE WAS NO SATAN**: that is to say, there is no adversary, no enemy threatening me, with whom I must wage war, or who might hinder me from building the Temple.
