From this it appears that Hiram came to know the true God — whom the Israelites themselves worshipped — partly through his close friendship with David, and partly through his recognition of Solomon's wisdom and prosperity. For he calls Him Jehovah (as the Hebrew text has it), which is the proper name of God, belonging to Moses and to the Hebrews. For this reason also he cooperated zealously with Solomon in the building of the Temple of the true God, sending tens of thousands of workmen, timber, and stones — which he certainly would not have done had he believed that Solomon's God was not the true God but a false one; for in that case he would have been building a temple for an idol. Seleucus, king of Asia, did something similar, as recorded in 2 Maccabees 3:3. For previously Hiram had worshipped Hercules and Astarte, the gods of the Tyrians, and had built temples to them, as Josephus attests in book 1 *Against Apion*. Perhaps Hiram also worshipped the God of the Hebrews alongside these ancestral deities of his, just as the Assyrians settled in Samaria did, as we shall hear in book 6, chapter 17. Hence Eucherius and Angelomus, in their allegorical interpretations, understand by Hiram the kings of the Gentiles — such as Constantine, Theodosius, Gratian, and Charlemagne — who assisted Christ and the Apostles in building and propagating the Church. Moreover, Hiram or Chiram in Hebrew signifies *life exalted*, or *the loftiness of life*, or *principality*, or *nobility*, or *their brightness*, according to Pagninus in his *Hebrew Names*.

That is, cedars and firs. The second book of Paralipomenon 2:8 adds: *wood of the arceuthos*, that is, juniper-wood, as the Septuagint renders it — namely junipers which grow there to a great height and thickness, so that beams for building might be made from them. For although David had prepared timber and the rest of the materials for the building of the Temple, as is stated in 1 Paralipomenon 22:14, yet these did not suffice for Solomon, who contemplated structures larger and more numerous for his palaces. So Abulensis. Allegorically, Eucherius and Angelomus say: *The converted Gentiles sent to Christ certain men illustrious according to the world, yet already cast down and humbled from the mountain of their pride by the rebuke of the Lord's axe, who, once formed according to the rule of evangelical truth, were each placed in the building of the Church according to his own merit or season. They also sent craftsmen: because the Gentiles presented to the Lord philosophers converted to true wisdom, who by reason of their learning might rightly be set over the governance of peoples as well — such as was, in the very age of the Apostles, Dionysius the Areopagite, and thereafter that most sweet and most valiant Doctor and Martyr Cyprian, and very many others besides. They also sent gold, which is received in nearly the same signification, since it displayed men pre-eminent in wisdom and genius; for all these offerings the Gentiles await from God the gifts of heavenly grace.* **BUT SOLOMON PROVIDED HIRAM WITH TWENTY THOUSAND CORS OF WHEAT.** For Tyre at that time was an island, surrounded on all sides by the sea, so that nothing could be sown or reaped there. Hence Hiram, requesting wheat from Solomon in his letter preserved by Josephus, says: *We have need of wheat, for we are islanders.* Afterwards, however, Tyre was besieged by Nebuchadnezzar for thirteen years, and with earthen mounds cast into the sea so that it might be approached and stormed, it became a peninsula, as I noted on Ezekiel 29:17–18 and Isaiah 23. **VERSE 18. AND SOLOMON CHOSE WORKMEN FROM ALL ISRAEL, AND THE LEVY WAS THIRTY THOUSAND MEN.** By *levy* understand the foremost men and craftsmen, or the overseers of the works. For it is said in chapter 9:22 concerning the sons of Israel that Solomon did not appoint any of them to servile labor, but they were men of war, his ministers, princes, commanders, and captains of his chariots and cavalry.

That is, stone-cutters, who would hew stones from the mountain or cliff. For these were not Israelites, as I have already noted, but proselytes, as is clear from 2 Paralipomenon 2:17–18. These proselytes were therefore Gibeonites and the remaining Canaanites subject to the Hebrews and converted to Judaism. The total number of proselyte burden-bearers and stone-cutters employed in the building of the Temple was one hundred and fifty thousand, while the Hebrews numbered thirty thousand, as stated in verse 13 — add these to the one hundred and fifty thousand and the total comes to one hundred and eighty thousand. To these add the Tyrians and Sidonians whom Solomon had requested from Hiram and whom Hiram had sent, as is clear from verses 6 and 9, and the Egyptians whom Pharaoh, Solomon's father-in-law, dispatched to him — and the total will come to two hundred thousand and more of those who labored in the building of the Temple. Indeed, if we believe Eupolemus as cited by Eusebius in book 9 of the *Praeparatio Evangelica*, the total will reach three hundred and forty thousand: for Eupolemus asserts that Hiram sent Solomon eighty thousand Tyrians, and Pharaoh an equal number of Egyptians, making one hundred and sixty thousand, which if added to the one hundred and eighty thousand sent by Solomon yields three hundred and forty thousand. Yet this number seems excessive and almost incredible. Hence some scholars hold that there is a scribal error in the numbers of Eupolemus or Eusebius, and that eighty thousand has crept in where eight or eighteen thousand was intended. By *stone-hewers* also understand hewers of timber; but here only the stone-cutters are numbered, because there were more quarrymen than woodcutters. Likewise, under *burden-bearers* understand carters, sailors, muleteers, grooms, and the like.

You will say: In 2 Paralipomenon [2 Chronicles], three thousand six hundred are numbered. I reply: From the six hundred were drawn three hundred, who were set over the overseers themselves, whom the book of Paralipomenon adds and supplies. So Vatablus, Dionysius, Cajetan, and Salianus.

Therefore the precious stones here were not adamites, carbuncles, or smaragds, but marble stones; for these are hard and strong, so that they might be laid in foundations and there sustain the entire structure. Moreover, marble is of great price and of various kinds. One kind is called ophites, because it has spots in the likeness of serpents — for *ophis* is the Greek word for serpent. Another is called porphyrites, because it is red and marked with white spots. Another is Parian marble, which is white; another is black; another is green. Another is called sphaegites, because it shines and is translucent.

Some think that these craftsmen were called Giblites from the Hebrew word *gebul*, meaning "boundary" or "terminus," because by their hewing they would shape the stones into elegant defined forms. But I say that the Giblites, or Biblites — for the letter *g* is sometimes interchanged with the letter *b* — take their name from the city of Byblos, concerning which Adrichomius, p. 185, n. 11, writes as follows: "Biblas, or Byblos, and Biblium, once called Evea or Evcea, now called Gibel, Giblee, and Bibielli, is a renowned city of Phoenicia, which in Hebrew is called Gebal and Gobel; its inhabitants are called in Sacred Scripture Bibli or Gibii. This city is situated toward the north, three miles from the river Cane, and to the south four miles from the city of Boiro, above the sea; it is recorded as having been founded by Evaeus, the sixth son of Canaan. Of this city we read in the third book of Kings in this manner: 'Moreover, the Biblites prepared for Solomon timber and stones to build the house of the Lord.'" Finally, in Byblos there were temples of Adonis, as Strabo says, book 16. Today it is called Gaeta. CHAPTER SIX

The Hebrew *shecuphim atumim* means "looking upon closed things," that is, as the Chaldean renders it, open on the inside and closed on the outside — wider within and narrower without (perhaps also covered with lattices: hence the Septuagint translates them as "latticed" or "reticulated"), and therefore oblique, as our Vulgate renders it, so that they might admit more light. For as the Opticians or Perspectivists teach: in these oblique windows, the solar rays coming from without intersect one another at the outer surface of the window, where the opening is narrower, and then spread out and diffuse themselves far and wide through the house (whence Symmachus, for *oblique*, renders "arrow-slit windows," which, as it were, shoot forth the light like an arrow); for thus the light of the sun enters the house after the manner of an inverted pyramid. So say St. Jerome in his commentary on Ezekiel chapter 40, Ribera in book 1 *On the Temple* chapter 13, Eucherius, Angelomus, and others. Hear St. Jerome in his commentary on Ezekiel chapter 41: *The windows also were made after the manner of a net, in the likeness of lattices, yet not closed with speculary stone or glass, but with interwoven and inlaid woodwork* (for which in the Hebrew you have "oblique windows"), *which were only in three parts of the Temple, namely on the right side, on the left side, and behind, that is, toward the South, toward the North, and toward the West. But the eastern part had no windows, because the entrance itself admitted clear light within, and all the interior parts of the Temple were filled with the light from the doors, so that through each of the windows and the openings of the lattices one could look within. These windows were only in the Holy Place. For the Holy of Holies lacked windows, out of reverence for it: for God dwells in the darkness invisible to us; yet through the doorway some light was admitted into the Holy of Holies.* See what was said on Leviticus 16:13. Allegorically, these oblique windows represent the holy Doctors, through whom God teaches and illuminates the faithful, who, as Angelomus says, while they publicly open to the faithful those things which they behold in secret, fill the innermost recesses of the Temple like windows which have received the light of the sun. Hence these windows are rightly said to have been wider on the inside — that is, wider within than without — because it is necessarily the case that whoever has received for even a moment a ray of heavenly contemplation, must presently, by mortification of heart, open himself more widely to greater things, and prepare himself by free exercise to grasp still greater ones. See what was said on Ezekiel 41:17. 5. *And he built against the wall of the Temple a surrounding structure, along the walls of the house round about, both the Temple* (that is, the Holy Place) *and the Oracle* (that is, the Holy of Holies, from which God gave oracles) *and he made side-chambers round about.* Here is described the cells built around the Temple; for this is what is meant by "he made side-chambers round about." For these lateral cells flanked the Temple on its sides, surrounding it on three sides, namely toward the West, the South, and the North. For the eastern side, where the entrance and doorway were, could not have cells built around it. What is said therefore, *upon the wall*, means the same as *beside the wall*, or *around the wall of the Temple*. For in a similar manner it is said of Christ in Luke 4:39, *he stood over* — that is, beside — *her, and rebuked the fever*. And in Psalm 136:1, *Over* — that is, beside — *the rivers of Babylon we sat down and wept*, etc. The sense therefore is: Solomon built beside and around three walls of the Temple looking toward the three quarters of the world.

By *sides* he means the floor-levels of the cells, as is clear from the Hebrew and the Septuagint. For he explains these *sides* when he adds: *The lowest floor-level was five cubits in breadth, the middle floor-level six cubits in breadth, and the third floor-level seven cubits in breadth*; where for *floor-level* the Hebrew and Septuagint have *side*. He calls them *sides* therefore, both because they ran laterally along the three sides of the Temple, and more especially because one rose above another laterally, projecting outward, and the upper was wider than the lower — whence the Chaldean calls them *appendages*. The meaning therefore is as follows: these cells, or rows of cells, had three sides — that is, three floor-levels running all around — of which the lowest side or floor-level was five cubits in breadth; the second, six; and the third, seven cubits; that is, there were three rows of cells in the three floor-levels thereof, of which the second row was wider than the first, and the third wider than the second, by reason of the projecting beams, of which he presently adds: *But the beams he laid*, etc. So Salianus, Ribera, Cajetan, and others. Now the reason for this inequality of the floor-levels was the narrowness of the plot on Mount Moriah: for this caused the lower cells to be cramped, and therefore it was necessary to make the upper ones wider by means of projecting beams. So Ribera. 6. *But the beams he placed in the house round about on the outside, that they might not be fastened into the walls of the Temple.* The beams — that is, the ends of the beams projecting and protruding beyond the walls upon which they rested: whence in Hebrew they are called *migrā'ôt*, meaning *deficient*, because they did not reach as far as the adjacent wall of the Temple but fell short of it, and only the heads of the beams projected outward. The meaning is: he caused the beams to extend and project beyond their own wall by their heads, so that transverse timbers might be laid upon them, and thus the upper floor-levels might be larger and of greater breadth than the lower floor-levels. For these beams and floor-levels were not embedded in the walls of the Temple, but these cells and their floor-levels stood at a distance — albeit a small interval — from the walls of the Temple. Hence it follows necessarily that these cells had windows opposite the windows of the Temple, so that the Temple's light might pass through into them. From this it is clear that these cells and floor-levels were not projecting from the Temple itself, as Bede, Angelomus, Lyra, and Abulensis maintain, but were separated from the wall of the Temple, as Scripture here expressly affirms. Furthermore, in the lowest row, or on the pavement which was covered by its floor-level, there were cells or chambers; an equal number in the second row or middle floor-level; an equal number in the third or topmost — so that in all there were forty-two cells, all of which were interconnected, so that one might pass from one to another without a door, and from there to a seventh, etc. Although Josephus asserts that in each row there were thirty cells or small chambers, each having five cubits in length and an equal number in breadth. But in this, as in many other things, Josephus is in error, as Salianus demonstrates. Tropologically, the three rows of cells, or their three floor-levels, denote the three states and degrees of the faithful in the Church: namely, the lowest of the married, the middle or second of the continent, and the third and highest of virgins. So Bede, Rupert, and others. See what was said on Ezekiel 41:7. 7. *But the house* (the Temple) *was built of dressed and finished stones* (which had already been hewn and finished on Mount Lebanon or elsewhere, before they were brought to Zion for the building of the Temple) *and it was built so that* (that is,

So the Hebrew, Chaldean, and the rest. The Regia and Complutensian editions therefore erroneously read: *of cypress wood*; for which reason the Septuagint and Roman editions delete it. Note: The two Cherubim fashioned by command of God through Moses, Exodus 25:18, were two statues bearing the appearance and form of a beardless and winged youth; for they signified the Angels attending upon God. The Ark of the Covenant was, as it were, a chest, whose lid was the propitiatory; upon the propitiatory rested the two Cherubim, who, spreading and joining their wings, presented them as a kind of throne on which God might sit, so that the Ark served as the footstool of His feet; and they were positioned in such a manner as if two men, with faces turned toward one another, were gazing upon each other, and holding and embracing one another with their hands, as it were wings. This Ark together with the propitiatory and the Cherubim (for these could not be separated from one another) Solomon placed in the Holy of Holies; for God had commanded Moses that they should be placed there. Solomon, however, in order to add to the majesty of the Ark, the propitiatory, and the Mosaic Cherubim, added in his Temple two other Cherubim far larger, which would veil and overshadow with their bodies and wings the Ark with its own Cherubim (these being small and barely three cubits high), and cover it as with a canopy: wherefore with their faces they looked toward the Ark and toward the East, but with their backs toward the wall of the Holy of Holies and toward the West. Moreover they occupied a different and distinct position from that of the Cherubim fashioned by Moses; for the Mosaic ones were above the Ark, with wings spread on either side, grasping and embracing one another; but these of Solomon stood upright upon their feet on the pavement of the Holy of Holies, and one stood beside the other on the same side as a colleague; and each stood with wings extended lengthwise, so that one touched with the tip of one wing the tip of the wing of the other Cherub, while the other tip of his remaining wing touched the wall of the Temple; so that one with the tip of his wing touched one wall of the Temple, and the other with the tip of his wing touched the other wall — namely of the Holy of Holies. For each wing was five cubits; therefore the two wings of one Cherub were ten cubits, and the two wings of the other Cherub were likewise ten cubits; so that both together when extended were twenty cubits, which was both the length and the breadth of the Holy of Holies; for this was twenty cubits in length and as many in breadth. So Bede, Abulensis, Adrichomius, Ribera, and others. Hence God, answering from the propitiatory the questions of the High Priest and giving oracles, was said to sit upon the Cherubim — that is, to surpass in wisdom the most knowing spirits, and to transcend even the Cherubic Angels in knowledge and to tread upon them, as it were, underfoot. Hence also God is here called the Lord of Sabaoth — that is, of armies, namely of Angels, of whom He has very many and, as it were, innumerable, and these most wise and most powerful. # GEM Anagogically, the Holy of Holies represented the empyrean heaven; the Ark, the Church triumphant, or blessed humanity; the golden propitiatory, the glorious humanity of Christ; the two Cherubim, the holy Angels, supremely united in the service and praise of God. Above these the Godhead, invisible to us, towers and is enthroned; and to both the blessed Angels and the blessed men dwelling together in highest security — signified by the paired number and the faces of the Cherubim turned toward one another — God presents Himself to be seen and enjoyed, and by this glorious vision beatifies them for eternity. So Bede, Angelomus, and following them Ribera, book 2 *On the Temple*, chapter 7. See what I said on the Cherubim at Exodus 25:18 and Ezekiel 1:5.

That is, both in the Holy of Holies and in the Holy Place. For the Holy Place was the outer or exterior house; the Holy of Holies was the inner or interior house, as I have said. Therefore Solomon paved the floor of the entire Temple with fir-wood boards, which he then covered with gold, just as he also overlaid the walls of the Temple with cedar boards, which he likewise clothed with gold. So Vatablus, Sanchez, and others. 31. *And at the entrance of the oracle he made small doors of olive wood, and five-cornered posts.*

That is to say: Solomon, at the entrance of the oracle — that is, of the Holy of Holies — made two small doors, namely little gates of the same olive wood, pentagonal in form, having five angles and as many posts; and upon them he carved effigies of Cherubim, palm trees, and anaglyphs. "Anaglypha" is a Greek word, signifying images and pictures that stand out and project from the surface of a wall, panel, or door. This much concerning the doors of the Holy of Holies; now he adds the following concerning the doors of the Holy place.

**And he made at the entrance of the temple doorposts of quadrangular olive wood.** And two doors of fir wood on either side: and each door was double and folded back upon itself, so that they opened by turning inward upon one another. That is to say: Solomon, at the entrance of the Holy place, made quadrangular doorposts of olive wood, to which he joined two doors of fir wood, one on either side — so that one door was on one side, and the other answered and faced it on the opposite side. And each door was double and held together by hinges, so that one part held the other; and there were in them, as it were, two folds, as the Septuagint renders it. In Hebrew there were two, גְּלִילִים *gelilim*, that is, revolving or rolling valves. Josephus adds that upon this door of the Holy place there also hung a veil, similar to the veil that hung before the Holy of Holies. The portico, however, which stood before the Holy place, had no veil at all, but lay entirely open in front, and rose without any ceiling to a height of one hundred and twenty cubits — which was the full height of the temple — for the walls alone enclosed it on the sides. This portico was, as it were, a vestibule added to the temple to increase its beauty, majesty, and veneration. Note: There were three principal parts of the temple. The first was called in Hebrew דְּבִיר *debir*, or as the Septuagint renders it, *dabir*, that is, the oracle, namely the Holy of Holies. The second was called in Hebrew הֵיכָל *heikhal*, that is, the temple proper, namely the Holy place. The third was אוּלָם *ulam*, that is, the atrium, of which see verse 36. The first two had a roof that was not pointed but flat, so that one could walk upon it; for indeed in that region the houses of Palestine are commonly built in this manner, but they have around them a low enclosure or parapet, in accordance with the law in Deuteronomy 22:8, lest anyone walking upon the roof should carelessly fall from it to the ground and be killed. Into the Holy of Holies only the High Priest entered, and that but once a year, namely on the feast of Expiation, as in Leviticus 16; into the Holy place the other priests; into the atrium the people — which is why Christ and the Apostles taught there. Thus the Holy of Holies was the last, the innermost, and the most sacred part of the temple; after it came the Holy place, after the Holy place the portico, after the portico the court of the priests, after that the court of the clean laity, and after this last came the court of the unclean and of the Gentiles.

**And he built the inner court with three rows of hewn stones and one row of cedar beams.** The atrium, or court already mentioned, was twofold: namely an inner one adjacent to the Holy place, which was the court of the priests and Levites, in which stood the altar of burnt offerings upon which the priests sacrificed victims; and an outer court, in which the people and laity gathered, prayed, and watched the sacrifices that were performed in the court of the priests. This outer court was therefore very spacious and encompassed several stadia in its circuit, so as to accommodate the great multitude of the people — whence in 2 Chronicles 6:13 it is called a great platform. Both courts were open to the sky, both so that the smoke of so many victims might ascend into the air, and because the outer court was exceedingly large, so that —

Concerning the foundations of the temple, Villalpando says in book 2, chapter 71 and following, that there belong to them many subterranean chambers constructed with the most solid workmanship and carefully corresponding to the works above, where there were cryptoporticoes and kitchens and treasuries, storehouses, wine cellars and oil cellars, halls, gymnasia, and other places of this kind, which were so spacious and capacious that into them could be brought and preserved all the firstfruits and tithes of all Israel, so that they could in their proper seasons be distributed to the many thousands of priests and Levites. We see similar things at Rome in the ancient crypts of the Martyrs, the Pontiffs, and the faithful — so much so that subterranean Rome is a wonder not merely of a city but of the whole world. **And it came to pass that in the eleventh year of Solomon, in the month of Bul** — so it must be read with the Hebrew, Septuagint, and Roman editions, though the Royal editions wrongly have *Ethanim* — **which is the eighth month**, corresponding partly to our October and partly to November. For the Hebrews used lunar months, while we use solar ones. October is called in Hebrew שָׁבַת נָהַת *nahat*, that is, to flow down, because in October the leaves fall and the rains pour down. So Serarius. The Chaldean renders it: *the month in which new crops are gathered.* **And he built it in seven years.** Add also seven months, as is apparent to anyone computing from the second month of the fourth year of Solomon to the eighth month of his eleventh year; but Scripture records the whole numbers of years and omits the fractions. Allegorically, Radulphus in the Preface to book 17 on Leviticus says: the Temple of Solomon was built in seven years and seven months, because from the coming of Christ until His return to judgment the Church is built up through the sevenfold grace of the Spirit, until at the end she is brought to completion; and this shall take place throughout this entire age, which is accomplished in the course of seven days, as Eucherius says — and so also Angelomus. # CHAPTER SEVEN

Solomon built three houses during these thirteen years, namely his own house, the house of Pharaoh's daughter the queen, and the house of the Forest of Lebanon, as is clear from verses 2 and 8. That these were distinct houses is evident from what follows. So hold Lyra, Abulensis, Cajetan, Dionysius, Hugo, Vatablus, and many others. Yet these three houses are called one house in 3 Kings 9:10 and 2 Chronicles 8:1, because they were contiguous and connected, so that they seemed to be not so much three separate houses as three parts or three members of one and the same house. For so it is with a royal or pontifical palace, which contains within itself many dwelling-places and, as it were, many palaces. So say Josephus, Villalpando, Pineda, and others. Furthermore, just as David built his own royal house on the western part of Mount Sion near the Temple, so Solomon built his palace on the eastern part of the same Mount Sion near the Temple — both in order to fortify the Temple and the whole of Mount Sion with these two houses as with two citadels, and to defend from them the city of Jerusalem lying below, keeping it in due submission, and also so that he might easily approach the Temple, offer worship to God, and sacrifice. THIRTEEN YEARS.] More years, and therefore the royal palace was built more slowly than the Temple; for the Temple was completed in seven years, as was said in chapter 6, verse 37. The first cause was the devotion and fervor of Solomon pressing forward the work of the Temple as the house of God; the second, because David had already prepared all the materials and expenses for the construction of the Temple beforehand; the third, because the people assisted and promoted the building of the Temple with greater ardor. So Josephus. Add that Solomon during these thirteen years built three houses, namely his own, the queen's, and the Forest of Lebanon. So Abulensis.

This house was built for elegance, pleasantness, and recreation. But why is it called the forest, or grove, of Lebanon? I answer, first, because it was situated in a wooded part of Mount Sion, and was surrounded like Lebanon with trees and gardens. For a forest properly denotes a denser and pathless wood where trees spring up, in which animals are wont to graze and pasture in summer, as Valla says. Hence in 2 Chronicles 9:16, where our text reads "and the king placed them (the shields) in the armory," the Hebrew and Septuagint have: "He placed the shields in the house of the Forest of Lebanon." For in this house Solomon stored his weapons of war, and there was his armory. Second, because this house was constructed from the timbers of Lebanon. So Abulensis and Serarius. Third, because with its groves, pleasantness, greenery, and shade it bore the appearance of Lebanon. Fourth, because by the multitude of its cedar columns it imitated the heights of Lebanon. So Theodoret. Fifth, because from it one could look out upon Lebanon, and in summertime catch and enjoy its breezes and coolness. So Arias. Hence Josephus and the Chaldean Targum call it the house of refreshment. Wrongly, therefore, does William, Bishop of Tyre, in book 18 of his Sacred War, suppose that this house is called the Forest of Lebanon because it was built upon Mount Lebanon; for it was situated in Jerusalem, on Mount Sion. AND FOUR WALKS BETWEEN THE CEDAR COLUMNS.] Note: the house of the Forest of Lebanon had below it, or beneath itself, these four walks, arranged in such a way that on the lower level, extending lengthwise, there were three rows of columns enclosed between two walls of the house; from these, by the intervening spaces, four walks necessarily arose. Each row of columns had fifteen columns running lengthwise, arranged evenly opposite the columns of the other two rows, so that between the three rows of columns there were two walkways, while a third lay between one row of columns and the wall of the house on one side, and a fourth between another row of columns and the wall on the other, opposite side. Wherefore these walkways were as long as the house itself. See Abulensis, question 2 and following.
He calls the ceiling a vault; for this is made by a vaulted construction and constitutes the vaulting of the house itself.

That is to say, the length of this portico was joined to the breadth of the house of the Forest of Lebanon, which was likewise fifty cubits; while the breadth of the portico was joined transversely to the length of the same house. Hence it came about that the house, whose length was one hundred cubits, with the addition of these fifty cubits of the portico's breadth, came out to a length of one hundred and fifty cubits in all. AND ANOTHER PORTICO IN FRONT OF THE GREATER PORTICO.] That is to say, Solomon made before the face — that is, in front of — this greater portico adjoining the house of the Forest (for this portico served as the entrance and vestibule of the house itself) another and smaller portico, proportioned to the greater portico for the sake of gradation, beauty, and ornament.

This is the third portico, not a vestibule but a judicial hall, and therefore not attached to the house of the forest of Lebanon like the two preceding ones already described, but separate and standing by itself, though not far from the other two, and at some distance from the house of the forest of Lebanon. This portico is called the portico of the throne because in it stood the royal and judicial throne of Solomon, surrounded by statues of lions, of which we read in chapter 10, verse 18. So Abulensis and Pineda.

The meaning is: In this third portico there was a small chamber, in which stood the throne of Solomon already described, beside and around which were lower thrones or seats on which his counselors and princes sat together with Solomon to render judgment, as his assessors. From all of these was composed the judicial tribunal, consisting of several seats for the judges, which was enclosed within a small chamber and, as it were, screened off, so that the assessors might consult privately with Solomon on some matter or deliver their verdict, being separated from the litigants and from the people who walked about in the surrounding portico or came to seek judgment. Therefore this small chamber was a kind of compact enclosure, built of cedar and closed on every side, so as to contain only the king with his counselors, yet having its own door through which litigants and others seeking justice or some favor might approach the royal throne and petition and obtain from him either justice or grace. It also had several windows, so that when these were opened, the king, pronouncing sentence, declaring the law, or promulgating some other decree, could be seen and heard by all the people. For this was, as it were, the praetorium of Solomon. So Lyran, Dionysius, Salianus, and Pineda, book 7 on the Affairs of Solomon, chapter 7. [Concerning the palace made for the daughter of Pharaoh:] This was built for his chief wife and queen. The reason is given in 2 Chronicles 8:11, where it is said: *And the daughter of Pharaoh he brought up out of the city of David into the house that he had built for her; for the king said, My wife shall not dwell in the house of David king of Israel, because the places are holy whereunto the ark of the Lord hath come.*

That is, these things were made of marble — Parian, porphyry, ophite or serpentine, and the like — for it would have been excessively costly to construct everything in so vast a house from gems, such as emeralds, carbuncles, diamonds, and the like. The term "precious stone" here therefore denotes precious marble. And *on the outside* (which some incorrectly read as "on the inside") *as far as the great court*. From this it is clear that, in addition to the three porticos already described, there was in these buildings of Solomon a great court, of which we read in verse 12; for the porticos appear to have been made of the same precious stone or marble as the house itself, as this passage suggests.

The meaning is: The cedar timbers applied to the construction of Solomon's buildings were cut and fitted to equal measure, just as the precious stones had been cut for the outer course. And verse 12: *And the great court round about* — that is, in the buildings of Solomon already described in verse 9 — *was built* by Solomon *with three courses of hewn stone and one course of hewn cedar, and so for the inner court of the house of the Lord and for the porch of the house*. The meaning is: Just as Solomon, in the inner court of the Temple, that is, in the court of the priests, built a wall having three courses of hewn stones and one of hewn cedar, by which wall the inner court of the priests was separated from the outer court of the laity, as was said at verse 6:36, so also he did precisely the same in the courts of the other buildings he had erected, and in the portico. One might object: The court of the priests, like that of the laity, was square, as I noted at chapter 6:36; how then is it here called "round"? The answer is: It was square, consisting of seven square arches; yet it is called "round" because it was encircled on all sides by the three courses of stones already mentioned. Hence the Hebrew word for "round" is סָבִיב (*sabib*), meaning "round about" or "in circuit," which circuit was here square, not circular. So Pineda, book 5, chapter 16, on verse 14. Verse 14: *And King Solomon sent and fetched Hiram out of Tyre, the son of a widow woman of the tribe of Naphtali, whose father was a man of Tyre.* The phrase "of the tribe of Naphtali" should be referred not to the widow woman but to Hiram the craftsman. For he was from the tribe of Naphtali, as is clear from the Hebrew, Chaldee, and Septuagint; but his mother was from the tribe of Dan, as is stated in 2 Chronicles 2:13. One might ask: How then is the father of Hiram said to have been a Tyrian? The answer is: Hiram was by race a Jew, namely a Naphtalite, but by residence and the practice of his craft he was a Tyrian. For he lived at Tyre, where he had learned and exercised the art of fine metalwork. So St. Jerome, the Hebraists, Lyran, Cajetan, and others. In the same way, St. Paul is called a Tarsian because he was born and raised at Tarsus, though by people and lineage he was a Hebrew.

Boaz and Jachin. These two columns of the Temple are described here down to verse 23. "Ten and eight cubits was the height of one column." Understand this as referring to the shaft or stem of the column; for in 2 Chronicles 3:15 each column is said to be thirty-five cubits, that is, with the addition and calculation of the quantity of the base, capital, and crown with which it was adorned at the top. So hold Abulenus, Lyran, and Cajetan. However, these parts in proper architectural proportion cannot rise to so great a height as to equal the columns themselves. Wherefore others answer better that both columns joined together were thirty-five cubits; for although taken together they were thirty-six cubits (since twice eighteen makes thirty-six), nevertheless in each column a middle cubit was inserted into its capital, and being hidden there did not appear. So Vatablus and Ribera, book 2 on the Temple, chapter 12. Thirdly, and most plausibly, Vilalpandus, book 5, chapter 5, and following him Salianus, answer that each individual column with its base was seventeen and a half cubits, excluding the plinth which completes the eighteenth. The reason for excluding the plinth was that the column with its base would be hollow, and therefore it was necessary to support it upon a solid plinth, and for this reason the plinth was cast separately from the column; for the plinth is the lowest part of the base, which lies beneath the torus mouldings, so called because its form represents a brick (whence in French they are called "plinthes"); for a plinth is a surface levelled to an even thickness, like a tablet. Hence also in Vitruvius, book 3, chapter 2, the plinths of the torus mouldings are the plinths of the bases. The plinth, therefore, that is, the lowest part of the bases, is not counted here, because the architect wisely chose to found it solid, so that it might more firmly sustain and contain the entire work of the column, which was hollow. Each column, therefore, joined together, was thirty-five cubits excluding the plinth, since each individually was seventeen and a half cubits; double these and you have thirty-five cubits. "And a line of twelve cubits encompassed the column round about." The Hebrew reads: a cord of twelve cubits encircled the second column; if the second, then also the first, for these two columns were entirely equal and alike. By "line," or in Hebrew "cord," understand a measure; for we measure the thickness of things with a cord, meaning: these columns in circumference and thickness had a measure of twelve cubits; therefore "line" denotes the periphery or circumference. Now Vilalpandus and following him Salianus maintain that it was not one column but both columns joined together that had this measurement of twelve cubits, such that half of the cord or line of twelve cubits encircled one column and the other half encircled the other; so that one column was six cubits thick and the other likewise, and consequently both together were twelve cubits thick. This symmetry and proportion demands it, so that since each column was eighteen cubits tall it should be only six cubits thick, for the height is triple the thickness. However, that each individual column was twelve cubits thick is expressly taught by Josephus and Jeremiah, chapter 52, verse 21, where, describing these columns carried off by the Chaldeans when they devastated Jerusalem, he says: "A line of twelve cubits encircled it," that is, one column. So also the Septuagint here: "And its perimeter," they say, "a cord of twelve cubits encircled it" (the first column); "and so the second column," that is, it was likewise twelve cubits, etc.

That is to say: these capitals were encircled by certain bronze bands (says Josephus) in the manner of a net or chains, which had at intervals openings like rings and lattice-work, through which the elegance of the capitals might appear—for they were lily-shaped, as Josephus says, or had the form of lilies. "Seven rows of network in one capital": that is to say, these bands just mentioned encircled each capital seven times, so as to form, as it were, seven rows or orders and circuits of bands; "rows," therefore, denotes circuits.

That is to say: each capital, and consequently each column, had two small nets, or a double latticed and network-like crown, which surrounded the capital in the manner of a net, covering and adorning it. So Sanchez. Better, Ribera understands by the two orders of network two orders of pomegranates hanging from the network. This is clear from the Hebrew and the Septuagint, which read: "Two rows of bronze pomegranates woven into the nets, in hanging work, row upon row"; and from Josephus, who says: "From there hung two series of pomegranates, numbering two hundred." The meaning therefore is: the seven individual rows or circuits of network on each column had two orders of pomegranates; for one hundred pomegranates were placed and as it were set in the midst of the individual rows or circuits, as is clear from 2 Chronicles 3:16; and the remaining hundred pomegranates hung suspended alternately from those same rows or circuits, so that two orders of pomegranates were joined to each piece of network, as is said in 2 Chronicles 6:13. "To cover the capitals which were upon the top of the pomegranates": that is to say, he made these two pieces of network to cover and surround the capital, which pieces of network, distributed in seven rows or circuits, were above the top of the pomegranates; for the upper row, or upper part of the circuit, was above the top of the pomegranates hanging from it, or depending downward from the circuit. So Ribera, book 2, chapter 16. It is added in 2 Chronicles 3:16: "He also made chain-like ornaments in the Oracle": that is to say, these bands of chains or these chained network pieces were of such a kind as those which had been fashioned in the Oracle, or in the Holy of Holies. So Ribera, book 2, chapter 14. Otherwise, Cajetan says: he made chains for the Oracle, that is, for mystery and signification, namely so that those chains might signify and openly declare the future captivity of that kingdom, or of Israel. "In the same manner he made also for the second capital," namely, for the capital of the second column. # GEM

This whole passage is extremely obscure and intricate, and is therefore expounded in various ways by various interpreters; but that it may be explained briefly and clearly: Note that each of these two columns had three capitals. The first and lowest was that which was placed immediately upon the shaft, stem, or scapus of the column; wherefore it is properly called the epistylium in 2 Chronicles 4:12, and was three cubits high, as is said in 4 Kings, last chapter, verse 17. The second capital was placed upon the lowest, or epistylium, and was covered by the network already described, in which were visible the two orders of pomegranates; above this second rose the third capital. So Ribera. This verse 19 describes the lowest capital, or epistylium. The meaning therefore is: in these two columns the lowest capitals, which were upon the heads of the columns—that is, which rested upon the summit of the shaft or stem of the columns, and are therefore called epistylia—were fashioned in the form of a lily, and this in the portico of four cubits; that is, so that toward the portico, which is to say in breadth and not in height, they were four cubits; for in height they were only three cubits, as I have said. Hence the Septuagint, instead of "in the portico," renders it "next to the vestibule." Of the second capital mention has already been made at verse 16 and following, for around it was the network bearing two orders of pomegranates. Of the third, or highest, capital, mention will be made in the next verse. Thus Vitruvius, and following him the Architects, place upon columns a threefold capital: first the epistylium, then the zophorus, and finally the corona. The epistylium is the capital placed immediately upon the style or scapus of the column. The zophorus is that which is interposed between the epistylium and the corona, which is often seen adorned with pulvinar figures and various ornaments; the French call it "la frise." So here the second capital serves as the zophorus, adorned with the networks of seven circuits and two orders of pomegranates. The corona they call the very crown of the capital. Vilalpandus judges that these three capitals taken together were five cubits, as is said in verse 16; so that the epistylium was three cubits, as is found in 4 Kings, last chapter, verse 17; the zophorus two cubits; and the corona finally one. Others, however, assign those five cubits to the second capital alone, as being the only one which had the network, adding to it three cubits of the epistylium and some cubits of the corona. But in that case the capital would have been in quantity half the column, for the column was only eighteen cubits. Wherefore, that there may be a fitting proportion between the capitals and the column, it seems better to say that all the capitals together did not exceed the measure of five cubits; for this bears an architectural proportion to a column of eighteen cubits.

Here the text treats of the third, or uppermost capital, which was as it were the crown of the column, placed over against the network — that is, above the network and directly opposite it — which capital was made according to the measure of the column, that is, it had the same thickness as the column, namely twelve cubits. NOW THE POMEGRANATES WERE TWO HUNDRED IN ROWS UPON THE SECOND CAPITAL; for this second capital, as I have said, was encircled by a network, from which hung one hundred pomegranates, and an equal number were inserted or placed between them; each network therefore had two hundred pomegranates. The other column likewise, in the network of its second capital, had an equal number of pomegranates, namely two hundred. Thus when both are taken together, there were in all 400 pomegranates made of bronze by skilled craft, as is stated in verse 42 and in 2 Paralipomenon 4, verse 13. Moreover, the hanging pomegranates were precisely only 96, but Scripture counts them as one hundred in order to assign a full and complete number, and perhaps the remaining four were indeed present but hidden and not visible. For this is what is said in Jeremiah, the last chapter, verse 23: "And there were ninety-six pomegranates hanging, and all the pomegranates were a hundred, surrounded by the network" — that is, those that were placed between the networks numbered one hundred; but those that hung freely were not precisely one hundred, but ninety-six. So Ribera, book 1, On the Temple, chapter 14.

The porch in question was the vestibule of the temple, and therefore stood before the Holy Place, being interposed between the Holy Place and the court of the priests, as described in chapter 6, verse 3; so that one column stood at one door of the porch, and the other at the other door. So Villalpando states: "He set them at the first threshold of the sanctuary, on either side of the gate of the holy hall." Salianus, Adrichomius, and others agree. Those, therefore, are in error who place these columns before the court of the priests, and others who set them before the court of the laity. AND WHEN HE HAD SET UP THE RIGHT COLUMN, HE CALLED IT BY THE NAME JACHIN; LIKEWISE HE ERECTED THE LEFT COLUMN AND CALLED ITS NAME BOOZ. Jachin in Hebrew signifies direction and rectitude, from a root meaning to direct. Hence the Septuagint in 2 Paralipomenon 3 at the end renders it κατόρθωσις, that is, correction, direction, right disposition. Booz in Hebrew means in it is strength. Hence the Septuagint renders it ἰσχύς, that is, force and strength. Some, such as Bede, Abulensis, Vatablus, and Ribera, consider that Solomon intended the same meaning by both names Jachin and Booz, namely that he was expressing his desire for the stability and permanence of the temple; for Jachin also sometimes signifies firmness and fortitude. More aptly, Cajetan and others distinguish these two names, taking Jachin — that is, direction — to signify prudence and wisdom, and Booz to signify fortitude and firmness; so that by Jachin Solomon indicated that this temple was most elegantly built by wondrous skill and wisdom, and by Booz that it was most strongly founded, fortified, and established: and by these same names he made known his desire and his vow, as if to say: "Would that God might direct this temple of mine as His own Jachin, and strengthen it as His own Booz, so that it might be cast down by no enemy, by no thunderbolt! Would that God might long protect, direct, and confirm this temple of mine — or rather, His own!" Again, Cajetan says the right column Jachin signified the directive power of God both over all creatures and men — and especially over the Israelites and their temple — and over the kings and kingdom of Israel; hence both columns were crowned. The left column signified fortitude for executing those things which, through Jachin — that is, through directive governance — have been wisely ordained; for both of these reside in God and in God's providence, which governs all things wisely and mightily; and both are likewise necessary for kings. Solomon therefore congratulated God on both, and at the same time expressed the desire and petition that God might manifest both in the protection of the temple, the people, and the kingdom, and might communicate both to himself and his successors for the right conduct of their reign. # GEM Allegorically, these columns of the temple signify the wisdom and firmness of the Church; for she is the pillar and ground of truth, as the Apostle says with allusion to this very passage in 1 Timothy 3:15 — see what is said there. Wherefore Jachin represents the Doctors and Rulers of the Church; for these teach and direct others in the way of virtue and salvation; Booz signifies the Martyrs and men of valor. Hence Saint Jerome, Bede, Eucherius, and Angelomus say: "The columns are the Apostles and Doctors, because by contemplation they are raised up to heavenly things, and are strong in faith and deed. They are two, because they support both Jews and Gentiles. They stand before the doors of the temple, so that they may lead both peoples into the temple — that is, into heaven." Moreover, they are two columns so disposed as to teach us that, both in prosperity and adversity, we must ever keep before the eyes of our mind the entrance to our heavenly homeland, so that we be neither cast down by adversity nor lifted up by prosperity, but remaining steadfast in faith and virtue amid both, we may press on toward heaven. So say the authors cited. The circumference of twelve cubits, says Bede, is the norm of apostolic institution.

That is to say, upon the entire work of the columns and their capitals he placed as a crowning piece the lily — or the form of a lily fashioned from the same bronze — both to display the beauty of the temple, and to implore from God its flowering, namely that in it piety and the worship of God, pure and chaste, might flourish. For the lily is the symbol of purity and virginity, as well as of the glorious resurrection of Christ and of the Saints, according to that which the Church sings to the Martyrs in the Divine Office: "The youth of the saints shall be renewed as the eagle's, and they shall flourish as the lily in the city of the Lord." And in Paschal time: "Thy saints, O Lord, shall flourish as the lily, and shall be as the odor of balsam before Thee, Alleluia." And that word of the Bridegroom: "I am the flower of the field and the lily of the valleys," Canticle of Canticles 2:1 — see what is said there; and Ecclesiasticus 39:19, and Hosea 14:6, where I have said much concerning the symbols of lilies. Solomon therefore, in placing the lily upon the temple, prefigured the purity, splendor, blessedness, and eternal glory of Christ, the Blessed Virgin, and the Saints; for the temple was a figure of heaven, and of the heavenly manner of life, of the resurrection, and of glory. Tropologically, every faithful and holy person stands in need of a twofold column in order to become a temple of God: namely Jachin — that is, prudent and right direction for discerning what is to be done — and Booz, that is, fortitude for carrying it out. For prudence goes before like a light, showing the way of virtue and holiness, and therefore occupies the right hand; while fortitude follows like the left hand, so that what prudence has shown must be done, it may carry into effect with vigor. Both are the work of Solomon — that is, of Christ; and therefore both must be earnestly and persistently sought from Him by prayer. Neither alone is sufficient; but if both are joined together, they accomplish all things, overcome all things, and raise a man to the summit of holiness and into heaven. For the Holy Spirit must direct us in the way of salvation, and then strengthen us, so that we may steadfastly pursue it through so many enemies, temptations, and difficulties. Hence Solomon says in Proverbs 3:6: "In all thy ways think upon Him, and He shall direct thy steps." And his father David frequently makes the same petition in the Psalms, as in Psalm 118:5: "O that my ways were directed to keep Thy justifications!" and verse 133: "Direct my steps according to Thy word." Psalm 26:11: "Direct me in the right path." Psalm 89:17: "Direct Thou the work of our hands." And God says to Joshua in chapter 1, verses 6, 7, and 9: "Take courage and be strong to overcome the Canaanites; fear not and be not dismayed, for the Lord thy God is with thee in all things wheresoever thou shalt go." And to David: "My arm shall strengthen him," Psalm 88:22.

He calls this a great basin, which held a large quantity of water for washing the hands and feet of the priests — for they ministered in the temple with bare feet — the water flowing out from it for that purpose. Hence in verses 26 and 31 it is called a laver, that is, a washing-vessel, from its purpose and use; but here, from its capacity, it is called a sea, and that a molten one — that is, cast from bronze. It was round; hence its diameter was ten cubits from brim to brim, that is, from one extremity of the circumference to the other opposite to it; its height was five cubits. Furthermore, it rested upon twelve oxen made of cast bronze, which with their faces looked out toward the four quarters of the world. Moreover, carved upon this sea or basin were figures of cherubim, lions, oxen, and palm trees. Finally, this sea stood in the court of the priests beside the altar of burnt offerings, and corresponded to the bronze laver which Moses made from the mirrors of the women for the Tabernacle, Exodus 30:18 — where I have shown that this laver, no less than the sea, represented not so much baptism, as Eucherius and Angelomus would have it (for this laver, like the sea, stood not before the court but within the court itself), as rather penance, which must precede for the faithful both the sacrifice and the sacrament of the Eucharist, as Saint Gregory teaches in Homily 17 on the Gospels, and Bede in book 3 On the Tabernacle, last chapter. See Ribera applying each detail individually, book 2, On the Temple, chapter 17. This vast basin full of waters, and therefore called a sea, signifies that the contrition and penance of a sinner ought to be bitter and great, pouring forth rivers of tears — if not from the eyes, at least from the heart — according to that word: "Great as the sea is thy contrition," Lamentations 2:13. AND A LINE — the Hebrew word is קָו kaf, that is, a cord; the Chaldean has funis, which our text calls resticula, that is, a small rope or measuring cord — OF THIRTY CUBITS DID COMPASS IT ROUND ABOUT — that is to say, this sea or bronze basin could be encircled and measured all around with a cord of thirty cubits; that is, in its circumference or circuit it contained thirty cubits. For the circumference or periphery of a circle is threefold the diameter; and therefore, since the diameter of this basin was ten cubits as I have stated, it follows that its periphery or circuit was thirty cubits. Abulensis notes that the circuit was precisely thirty cubits and one and a half, because the circumference contains the diameter three times and moreover a seventh part of the diameter; but Scripture after its custom omits and suppresses the smaller number. Hence Josephus says this basin was in the form and appearance of a hemisphere, or rather, as the Greek text has it, of a half-sphere. AND THE CARVING BENEATH THE BRIM WENT ROUND ABOUT IT, COMPASSING THE SEA FOR TEN CUBITS — that is to say, immediately below the brim of the sea or basin there was a certain carving encircling the sea, not the whole of it, but as it were a third part of it, which was ten cubits; and this carving consisted of two rows of carved fluted work, of which what follows immediately treats. So Ribera. Cajetan and Abulensis interpret differently, taking "beneath the brim" to mean the lower part of the basin, which was ten cubits, so that this carving encircled the whole of that part. THE TWO ROWS OF FLUTED CARVINGS WERE CAST — that is to say, the carvings just described were two rows of fluted sculptures made of cast bronze. Striated — so it must be read with the Hebrew and the Roman edition — not historiated, as the Royal codices read, nor historiated in the sense that histories were depicted there, as others read and maintain. Striated, then, signifies having stria: and stria are small channels or grooves which are drawn through wood or bronze to produce elegance.

Those in the anterior part, that is, with their faces, looked outward toward the four quarters of the world in groups of three, but their posterior part was hidden, that is, concealed beneath the basin which they supported. Allegorically, the twelve oxen signify the twelve Apostles, who, at Christ's command, preached faith and penance through all the quarters of the world.

Laver, that is, a washing-vessel; for in Greek *louo* means "I wash," and hence *loutēr* means a laver. "Laver" therefore signifies the brazen walls already mentioned, as is clear from the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Septuagint. Vainly therefore does Adrichomius distinguish the laver from the brazen sea, placing the laver toward the North and the brazen sea toward the South in the court of the priests, so that the altar of holocausts stood midway between the two; asserting that the sacrificial victims were washed in the laver, but the priests themselves in the brazen sea. In truth, however, both purposes were sufficiently served by the brazen sea together with the ten lavers described in verse 27. The meaning therefore is as follows: the thickness of the brazen sea was three ounces, that is, four fingers or a palm, as the Septuagint renders it — that is, three inches, which is the fourth part of a foot, or a hand's-breadth in thickness; for an ounce is the twelfth part of a foot, and a foot contains sixteen fingers; therefore three ounces equal the fourth part of a foot, namely four fingers. This is what is said in 2 Paralipomenon 4:5 — *vastiludo*, that is, its thickness had the measure of a palm. *And its brim was like the brim of a cup, and like the spreading flower of a lily*: that is, the brim or lip of the brazen sea was like the brim of a cup, and like that which is repand, that is, open and curving backward like a lily. For *repandum* means the same as curved and sinuous, that which opens backward. Thus a repand neck is one that is bent; a repand chest or back is one that is curved inward. Hence Pliny, Book 11, chapter 22, writes: "To this class belongs the ostentatious display of a repand neck projecting forward." Mystically, Eucherius and Angnomus say: *Through the brim of the cup, the holy sufferings of the Lord's Passion are expressed; through the spreading leaf of the lily, the manifest glory of His Resurrection*. For both are obtained in the washing of Baptism and Penance. As Eucherius says: *Christ, who before His Passion was like a lily still closed, shone forth as a glorious man through the signs of the miracles He performed; but after His Resurrection and Ascension He showed Himself as a lily fully open to the citizens of the heavenly fatherland, inasmuch as in His assumed humanity He displayed the power of divine glory which He had with the Father before the world existed. Hence in the Song of Love He willed to designate Himself by the name of lily, saying: "I am the flower of the field and the lily of the valleys." The brim of the sea in which the priests were washed was therefore like the brim of a cup and the leaf of a spreading lily, because the saving laver by which we are made members of the High Priest purifies us in faith in His most holy Passion from all stain of sin, and, once purified, admits us to the vision of His everlasting glory*. *It held two thousand baths.* Bath, ephah, and metretes are equivalent measures, being the tenth part of a cor, as in Ezekiel 45:11. Namely, it was a measure containing three modii; for the cor contained ten times as much, that is, thirty modii. The bath was a measure of liquids, just as the ephah was of dry goods, containing as much as a Roman amphora, namely 68 sextarii, that is 960 ounces of water, as Alcazar proves at length in his treatise on Measures, proposition 14, though Villalpandus makes the bath considerably larger. You may object: in 2 Paralipomenon 4:5 the brazen sea is said to have contained three thousand metretes, that is, baths. The reply, following Villalpandus, Alcazar, and others, is that the bath was of two kinds, a larger and a smaller, the larger being one-and-a-half times the other, so that two larger baths equaled three smaller ones. Villalpandus holds that the larger bath contained 108 sextarii, the smaller 72. Alcazar, however, in his treatise on Measures, proposition 16, judges that these baths were not common ones but extraordinary and smaller, and that accordingly this bath was only one tenth part of the standard bath; and further that these baths were—

—for the ten lavers, which were supported upon these bases. This is clear from verse 38 and from 2 Paralipomenon 4:6, where it is said: *He also made ten basins, and placed five on the right side and five on the left, that they might wash in them all the things that were to be offered as holocaust; but in the sea the priests themselves washed*. The lavers therefore were great basins or tubs and bowls in which the priests washed the victims already flayed and their flesh cut into pieces, so that they might be placed whole upon the altar of holocausts to be offered and burned before God. In the brazen sea, however, the priests alone washed their hands and feet. For although the Nathineans had previously washed the victims to be slaughtered in the Probatica pool before they were led into the Temple, nevertheless they washed them whole and alive; wherefore in the Temple itself, that is, in the court, the same animals, now slaughtered and cut up, had again to be washed by the priests from blood and filth, so that they might be offered pure to God upon the altar. For this reason the brazen sea stood in the middle of the lavers, with five lavers on its right side and five on its left, as Ribera explains. Each laver held forty baths, as is stated in verse 38. Josephus says the same in the translation of Rufinus, though Gelenius wrongly substitutes *congii* for *baths*. *The bases were each four cubits in length, and four cubits in breadth, and three cubits in height.* The bases of the lavers were therefore square, being four cubits wide and the same in length; they were accordingly made and compacted from four bronze panels joined together, and since their joints were fitted so as not to appear, they were covered over with various sculptures, as follows: *And the work of the bases was interrasile, with sculptures between the joinings.* And between the little crowns and the plaited work were lions, oxen, and cherubim; and in the joinings similarly above and below the lions and oxen, as if were garlands of bronze hanging down. This passage is involved and difficult. Passing over the various explanations of various authors, I shall briefly and clearly set out the whole matter. Note first that *opus interrasile* — interrasile work — is so called because between the flat and as it were smoothed bronze panels, things project and stand out in relief, as on shells the heads of lions, men, and oxen protrude and stand out. Hence in Hebrew the word is *misgerot*, that is, enclosures, or as Vatablus renders it, *cinctures*. The Hebrews call sculptured work that stands out and projects *enclosures*, because it as it were encloses and terminates the flat smooth panel — namely the intermediate flat spaces of the tablet. Hence our author calls this work *interrasile*, that is, he calls these projecting sculptures *coronulae* and *plectae*, both because they were surrounded by little crowns, flowers, and other interwoven and intertwined things, and also because the sculpted figures themselves are called crowns and coronets, that is, ornaments, because they crown and adorn the panel or object to which they are added. For all these things there is in Hebrew a single word, *misgerot*, which our author renders now as *opus interrasile*, now as *sculpturae*, now as *coronulae*, now as *plectae*. For some were in the form of a round crown, others were interwoven in a different shape and form, and these were called *plectae*. These four terms therefore signify the same thing here. Nevertheless *plectae* may be taken to mean the very joinings of the panels themselves, as in the Hebrew, so that *plecta* is derived from *plekō*, that is, to fold, to fit together, to bind, to join; for between the joinings of the panels were these sculptures. Note secondly that for *juncturae* — joinings — the Hebrew has *scelabbim*, which Pagninus renders as *vestments*; Marinus as *steps*; Vatablus as *prominences*; but we must trust our author and the Septuagint, who consistently render it as *joinings*.

That is to say, each individual base was set upon four wheels, so that it might appear to be rolled and moved from place to place. *And through the four sides, certain projections like shoulders beneath the laver, cast in bronze, facing one another*: that is, on the bases of the lavers there were certain projecting parts, by which, as if by shoulders, the lavers were supported — cast in bronze and facing one another on opposite sides. *The mouth of the laver moreover was within at the top of the capital*: that is, the mouth of the laver, meaning the upper part of the base upon which the laver or basin of the laver was placed, was concave after the manner of a mouth, so that the laver, which was likewise concave, might be aptly inserted into it; and it was at the top of the capital, that is, it was at the summit of the base. For thus the author explains himself in verse 35. *And what appeared on the outside was one cubit all around, and likewise it had one cubit and a half.* This is clearly explained by Josephus, who says in Book 8, chapter 2: *The images of lions and eagles were so fitted together that you would think them innate, with palm-tree shrubs intervening between them. And this rounded portion had an altitude of one cubit and a half, but the half-cubit was covered by the calathurae, so that only one cubit was visible.* *But in the corners of the columns there were various engravings.* For, as Josephus notes, the panels of the bases, being square, had in their corners columns — that is, certain small columnettes for elegance — into which the sides of the bases were inserted and as it were mortised on either side; and these had their own sculptures by which they were adorned. *And the middle spaces between the columns were square, not round.* He calls the shafts or stems of the bases — which intervened as it were in the middle between the columns affixed at the corners — *intercolumnia*, or inter-column spaces; meaning that the shaft of the bases was square, not round. In Hebrew and Chaldean: *the enclosures themselves*, that is, the sculptures, *were square, not round*. He calls the sculpted and figured bases themselves *enclosures* or *sculptures*.

That is to say, such were the wheels of the bases that they appeared capable of rotating and moving, when in reality they were fixed and could not rotate: and therefore they had all the features that true and rotatable wheels possess, namely their axles, spokes, rims, and hubs. The hub in a wheel is called that very knot or centre of the wheel from which the spokes arise, which are then carried outward to the rim, that is, to the periphery or outer circumference of the wheel.
That is, not added on separately, but as it were innate and born together with it, so that they appeared to have been cast from the base itself.

That is to say, he engraved upon the uppermost panels of the bases the likenesses of lions, palms, and the like. For he calls the bronze panelling the bronze panels themselves, joined together and fitted to one another.
Behold, these are the lavers whose bases he has been describing up to this point.

He placed the bronze sea toward the south, yet turned toward the east — that is to say, it was not directly facing the south, but toward that part of the south which inclines and bends somewhat toward the east. Moreover, regarding the bronze sea and the ten lavers for washing the sacrificial victims, one should add — from Aristeas in his book on the Seventy Translators, after the introduction — that there were in the Temple hidden reservoirs of water, which were poured out to dilute the blood of slaughtered victims, whose flow was so continuous, he says, that it seemed like a perennial spring. And toward this end there are, near the base of the altar, numerous openings known only to the ministers themselves. But more wonderful still, and nearly beyond description, is the magnitude of the subterranean cisterns, which penetrate through the entire circuit of the Temple for up to five stadia. Lead pipes descend through the walls and floor of the Temple wherever needed, by which all things are washed and cleansed. I have made mention of these matters above as well. *Allegorical and Tropological sense.* The ten lavers signify the same thing as the bronze sea, namely the manifold and frequent penitence which we fragile creatures require in this life on account of our many falls and the dangers of further falling. The forty baths which each laver holds signify the forty days of Lent, all of which are devoted to penance. For the rest, see Eucherius, Angelomus, and Ribera, book 2, *On the Temple*, chapter 19, who apply each detail minutely. It was Martin Luther who, first in this age, denied and abolished the sacrament of penance. Wherefore he himself was not a laver, nor a cleanser, but rather muddy and filthy — indeed a polluter and profaner of sacred things, given that he was wholly given over to his cups, his belly, and his lusts, defiling thereby both body and soul, like the sow wallowing in the mire, as Saint Peter says in his Second Epistle, chapter 2, verse 22. *He made therefore the cauldrons, and the basins, and the fleshhooks.* In place of "basins," the Hebrew has *iaim*, which the Chaldean and more recent translators render as brooms; the Greek as small warming vessels; others translate it as tridents, flesh-forks, or censers. Saint Jerome now translates it as basins, tongs, and tridents, now as ladles and flesh-hooks. For Hebrew words are polysemous and carry many meanings. *Scutra* is a type of vessel that is deep and circular, of which *scutella* is the diminutive. Thus *scutra* is one thing, *scruta* another, though Calepinus treats the two as the same. For *scruta* denotes worn-out garments, the shoes of beasts of burden, bent nails, sulphured items, broken glass, and other such things which the most lowly dealers sell — men called *scrutarii*, of the kind we see among the Jews at Rome, of whom Horace says in book 2, Epistle 7: *"Selling worthless scraps to the tunic-clad rabble."* Therefore *scutra*, or *scuta*, is a large bowl, so named from *scutum* because, like a shield, it is round and hollow. Hence Lucilius in book 6 says: *"He struck him on the head with a small wooden scutra* (that is, *scutella*)*."* And Papias says: *"Scutra is a bronze vessel with a flat bottom, broad and open at the top."* And Priscian at the end of book 3 says: *"From scutum is derived scutula and scutella, as if small shields or scutrae."* For *scutra* borrows the letter *r* from *scetra*, which is a rounded shield of beaten metal without wood, used by the Africans and Mauritanians, as Isidore says in book 18 of the *Etymologies*, chapter 12, of which the poet writes: *"The left hand is covered by the scetra."* Moreover, diminutives change a simple *r* into *l*: thus from *mitra* comes *mitella*, from *capra* comes *capella*, from *sacrum* comes *sacellum*, from *ager* comes *agellus*, from *piger* comes *pigellus*, from *miser* comes *misellus*. *Fleshhooks* — in Hebrew *miraskolth* — are vessels for sprinkling or pouring, by which water, wine, or incense is scattered and poured out. For the root *zarac* means to scatter or sprinkle, like the flasks by which water and wine are poured into the chalice at Mass.

That is, the altar of incense in the Holy Place, before the Holy of Holies, of which I have spoken at Exodus 30:1. Hence it is clear that Solomon made a new altar of incense in the Holy Place to replace the old one made by Moses, and that he stored the old Mosaic altar in the loft of the Holy Place. For Solomon made all the vessels of the Temple new, with the exception of the Ark together with its propitiatory and the cherubim. For in the Ark was kept the Law written on tablets of stone by the finger of God. He therefore replaced in the Temple the Ark made by Moses and did not fashion a new one; but all other things he renewed. Whence follows: *And the table upon which the showbread was to be placed* (he fashioned new, in place of the old one made by Moses) *and the golden candlesticks, five on the right hand and five on the left.* Moses made only one candlestick of gold with seven branches, that is, having seven shafts and as many lamps; but Solomon, in place of this one, fashioned ten. I have spoken at length about this table of the bread and about the candlestick at Exodus 25:23 and 25:31, and so I shall not repeat those matters here. *Tongs* — that is, snuffers, as Abulensis and Vatablus say. For a *forceps* is an instrument by which something *forvum*, that is, hot, is held, says Festus. This term applies not only to the tongs with which we handle burning coals, but also to the snuffers with which we take up and trim the ash of lamps and candles.

These are vessels properly speaking in which things that must be dissolved are ground with a pestle, as Nonius Marcellus says. Hence Saint Isidore in book 20 of the *Etymologies*, chapter 11, says: *"Mortars are so called because in them seeds, already reduced to powder, are stored as if dead."* But in this passage the Hebrew is *cappoth*, a word signifying small curved vessels — for *caphaph* means to curve — that are hollow and concave, in which incense or some such thing is stored. So Vatablus and others. Hence the Septuagint renders it as *phialae*; the Chaldean and Vatablus render it as *spoons*. *Mortariola* are therefore small mortars, and so vessels shaped like mortars, curved and concave, are called by this name. # GEM

That is, those things which David had dedicated, set apart, and as it were consecrated to God for the use of the Temple. The Septuagint reads: *Solomon brought in the holy things* (the Hebrew and Chaldean say: *the sanctities*) *of David his father.* For those things which are dedicated to God are so holy that they are themselves, as it were, holiness itself. *Note:* In the Temple nothing could be seen but marble, cedar, gold, and silver — so that you might learn that in a holy, devout, and God-dedicated soul, nothing ought to be found but what is pure, solid, and precious. *Here begins Chapter Eight.*

Some read *Maie* instead of *Bella nivi*. For reading it with the Hebrew, the Septuagint, and the Roman version gives *Ethanim*, that is, "of the strong," because in this seventh month the fruits are strong, that is, ripe, and when gathered with the harvests they are laid up in granaries, by which the heart of man and his life are confirmed and strengthened, as Vatablus says. Or else the name is given on account of the frequent feasts which were celebrated in the seventh month, through which God was worshipped by fervent and pious men — for feasts, as it were, strengthen men in piety and the worship of God; so Cajetan. Or because in that month the strength and health of men is made manifest, which is most greatly tried by the sicknesses of autumn, when the leaves fall and the strength of men declines as the heat retreats. The seventh month, therefore, which is Tishri, is called Ethanim for these reasons, and corresponds partly to our September and partly to October; and it was once the first month of the civil year, before God appointed the first month of the sacred year as Nisan, that is March or April, on account of the celebration of the Passover, Exodus 12:2. Hence the Chaldean renders this passage: *Israel was assembled in the month which the ancients called the first month, which is now itself the seventh month.* One may object: the Temple was completed in the eighth month, as was said in chapter 6, final verse — how then is it said here to have been dedicated in the seventh month? Abulensis, Salianus, and others respond that when the Temple was dedicated, it was indeed complete as to its principal parts, yet it did not have its entire perfection; rather, this was added to it after the dedication, so that it was fully perfected in the eighth month. For Solomon wished to anticipate its complete perfection on account of the solemnity of the Feast of Tabernacles in the seventh month, so that he might dedicate his Temple before the whole people at that feast. Serarius answers differently, namely that the dedication was deferred to the following year and its seventh month, so that in the meantime the walls might dry more thoroughly and all other things necessary for the dedication might be more fully prepared; and therefore the Temple was dedicated in the twelfth year of Solomon's reign, in the seventh month. *On the solemn day* — that is, on the feast of Tabernacles, says Abulensis. But this cannot be maintained, for this feast began on the fifteenth day of the seventh month; yet the dedication of the Temple was celebrated over fourteen days, as is clear from verse 65, after the completion of which the people were dismissed on the twenty-third day of that same month, as is clear from 2 Chronicles 7:10. Now from the fifteenth day of the month to the twenty-third day there are not fourteen days but only eight. Therefore, the solemn day here is understood as this feast of dedication, which on account of this most magnificent dedication was most solemn.
