So Vatablus, Torniellus, Serarius, and Salianus, who hold that this feast of dedication fell on the eighth day of the seventh month, or Ethanim; for from that day to the twenty-third, on which the dedication was completed, there are fourteen days, of which verse 65 speaks. Within these fourteen days, therefore, was included the Feast of Tabernacles, which was most solemn and began on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, lasting for seven days; by reason of which all males throughout all Israel were required to come to the Temple according to the law of Deuteronomy 16:16. Accordingly, Solomon studiously seized upon this occasion of time and festival, so that the dedication might be more solemn and celebrated with the greater concourse of the people. Allegorically, the dedication of the Temple signified the dedication of the Church, and of every Christian temple; on which see our Martin de Roa, *On the Birthday*, chapter 21. Tropologically, it signified the dedication of the holy soul, especially of the Blessed Virgin, on which see Peter Damian, Sermon 2 *On the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin*, where he compares her to the Temple of Solomon. On how the soul becomes the house and temple of God, see further below.

That is, the tabernacle built by Moses; for this is properly called the tabernacle of the covenant. So Josephus, Ribera, Abulensis, Serarius, Vitalpa, Sanchez, and others, although Salianus and Emmanuel Sa understand it as the tabernacle which David had built for the ark of the covenant in Sion. For the tabernacle of Moses was not in Sion but in Gibeon. Yet there is no doubt that it was transferred by Solomon from Gibeon to Sion, and thence into the Temple. Add to this that both tabernacles — that of Moses as well as that of David — were transferred by Solomon into the Temple, just as all the other sacred vessels made by Moses, and especially the altars, were transferred into the Temple, that is, into the flooring of the Temple; and this by a prudent counsel, namely so that the worship of God might be celebrated nowhere other than in the Temple, and that the danger of superstition and idolatry might thereby be avoided, as Cajetan says.
That is, those which Solomon had newly fashioned, ten cubits high, so that they might veil the ark — which was only one and a half cubits high — and cover it with their wings.
On this matter I have spoken at length in the commentary on Hebrews.
Verses 4 and 5.

For by the cloud God represented both Himself and His glory, and at the same time veiled them. For darkness is a fitting veil of God, and as it were a sacrament. It signifies that He is invisible and inaccessible to men.

Both out of reverence for the divine majesty manifesting itself through the cloud, and because this cloud more perceptibly assailed the eyes of the priests, blunting and dulling their sight. So Cajetan. FOR THE GLORY OF THE LORD HAD FILLED THE HOUSE OF THE LORD. He calls the cloud the glory of the Lord, because it was itself the sign and symbol of the glory of the Lord, as I have said; and therefore it was luminous and resplendent. So Cajetan. This phenomenon, he says, is called both cloud, and darkness, and the glory of the Lord. From these names it may be gathered that it was a cloud after the manner of a luminous darkness, and by reason of its light it is called glory, or rather the honour of the Lord; but by reason of its form it is called darkness; and by reason of its substance, cloud — for it was as it were vapours of misty darkness combined with splendour, and for this reason it perceptibly assailed the eyes of the priests who were ministering at the altar. # GEM

He said this at Leviticus 16:13, where God commands the high priest that he should enter the Holy of Holies only while offering incense, and this for the purpose that the vapour and cloud of the incense might cover the mercy seat and God dwelling upon it; lest, if the high priest should behold Him, he be struck lifeless and die by the splendour and terror of the divinity. The same was said at Psalm 103:3: *Who makest the clouds thy chariot*, and at Psalm 17:10: *Darkness is under his feet*, and at verse 12: *He made darkness his secret place*. Hence God spoke to Moses and the Hebrews through the darkness, Exodus 24:16, Deuteronomy 4 and 5.

Hebrew: *directing toward thy throne.* The purpose of the temple, says Cajetan, is shown to be not to provide God with a dwelling, but to furnish men with a directing of their minds toward the eternal divine throne which is in the heavens; for indeed the temple was made for the directing of men toward God, who created the heavens and the earth and governs all things. **Verse 22.** *And Solomon stood before the altar* (of burnt offerings). He stood therefore between the altar and the people who surrounded him on three sides. It is added in 2 Chronicles 6:13 that he stood upon a bronze platform placed there for the person of the king, so that he might be seen by all the people, in the sight (Hebrew: *in the region of all*) of the assembly of Israel; for he had his face turned toward the altar and the sanctuary to the west (in which direction also the priests and Levites stood), while the people were arrayed on the three other sides, to the east, south, and north. For thus he began to do what in this appended prayer he taught others to do, namely that prayers to God should be poured forth with the face turned toward the temple. Moreover, Solomon poured forth this prayer as king of Israel, and therefore he wore a diadem upon his head. Hence the Chaldean Targum on Canticles 3:11, understanding the litter of Solomon to mean his temple, where our text, the Septuagint, and the Hebrew have: *Go forth and behold, O daughters of Sion, king Solomon in the diadem*, etc., renders it thus: *When king Solomon came to make the dedication of the house of the sanctuary, the herald went forth in strength and spoke thus: Go forth and behold, O inhabitants of the provinces of the land of Israel and people of Sion, the diadem and crown wherewith the house of Israel crowned king Solomon on the day of the dedication of the sanctuary; and he rejoiced with the joy of the solemnity of tabernacles, which king Solomon kept at that time for fourteen days.* And in chapter 4, verse 1, it adds: *And on that day king Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings, and his oblation was received with good pleasure before the Lord; a voice burst forth from the heavens and said: How beautiful is the heaven of Israel, and how beautiful are the princes of the congregation and the wise men dwelling in the council, because they continually enlighten the people of the house of Israel*, etc. Let kings and princes here observe the admirable piety of Solomon, whereby he himself, as king, acting almost as priest and high priest, dedicated and consecrated to God the temple he had built, and supplicated that God would hear the prayers of those praying in it, and blessed the whole people — and therefore was heard by God and honored with a miraculous cloud and fire descending from heaven upon the victims. Let princes imitate him and they shall likewise be blessed by God. *And he spread forth his hands toward heaven*, and bent his knees, as is said in 2 Chronicles 6:13, so that by this gesture he might testify his reverence and devotion toward God and God's temple, and might stir the people by his example to the same. The early Christians imitated Solomon in this, for they prayed with knees bent and hands raised toward heaven; the various reasons for this ceremony from the Fathers I reviewed at 1 Timothy 2:8, on the words: *lifting up pure hands.* *For if heaven and the heaven of heavens* (that is, the highest, most vast, and most capacious heavens) *cannot contain thee, how much less this house which I have built* — for God is immense, and therefore is everywhere, fills all things, and extends beyond all things through the immeasurable spaces of the void, and therefore could create therein thousands and infinite millions of new worlds. The philosophers said the same, whence Aratus: *Jupiter is whatsoever thou seest; all things are full of Jupiter.* And Aeschylus, in Clement of Alexandria, *Stromata* Book 5: *Jupiter is the ether, is the earth, is heaven and all things Jupiter, and whatsoever is beyond.* And Orpheus in the same place...

As if to say: in my house I shall be present; there let me be worshipped, there I will answer, there I will hear prayers, I will work wonders and show forth my glory. Hence again: *My name shall be there*, that is, the temple itself shall be called by my name the temple of the Lord, according to that word: *My house shall be called a house of prayer*, Matthew 21:13.

As if to say: if anyone, called to the temple to swear, shall there have sworn that he did not sin against his neighbor, nor took away nor harmed his property. As if Solomon says: O Lord, I pray and beseech that in this temple thou wouldst reveal the truth or falsehood of an oath, namely that thou wouldst punish the one who perjures himself in the temple as one guilty of sacrilege and profaning thy house, so that it may be plain to all how great a reverence and religion is owed to thy temple, and how truly thou art present within it, and that thou mightest manifest thy spiritual power. For so this phrase is explained in 2 Chronicles 6:22–23. So says the Abulensis. Thus in ancient times persons suspected of some crime were led to the oratory of Saint Stephen, so that they might purge themselves there by oath; for if they swore falsely, they were immediately punished by God; examples of this are reviewed by Saint Augustine, *City of God*, Book 22, Chapter 8. The same occurred to those who perjured themselves in the temple of Saint Martin at his relics, as Gregory of Tours relates, Book 8, Chapter 16.

Hebrew: *Every prayer and supplication which shall be made by any man in the temple*, etc. So also the Septuagint and Chaldean. For *devotion* is prayer and the making of a vow, and this word was formerly in use, as I showed from Apuleius and Cicero at Proverbs 20:25, on the words: *It is a ruin to a man to devour*, or as Bede, Lyran. and others read, *to devote*, *holy things.* But *imprecation* is the same as *precation*; for the compound form is placed in use for the simple; it is therefore less properly taken by Saint Augustine in question 21 to mean a curse or anathema. # GEM *If any man shall know the plague of his own heart*, that is, if any man shall feel a sharp sorrow of soul and shall pour it forth in prayer before thee in the temple, thou wilt hear and deliver him from it.

As if to say: This house is called your house. "You shall execute judgment for them" — that is, you shall declare and render justice to them, by avenging the wrongs done to them, and by delivering them from unjust and violent oppressors.

As if to say: If Jews situated outside Judaea among the Gentiles shall pray with their faces turned toward their own land — namely, toward Jerusalem and the Temple. Thus Daniel, chapter 6, verse 10, a captive in Babylon, bent his knees three times a day toward Jerusalem. And the Psalmist, Psalm 133, verse 2, says: "In the nights, lift up your hands toward the holy places" — that is, toward the holy Temple. Indeed, even the Turks today in their mosques, when praying, superstitiously turn themselves toward the South, so as to face toward their Mahomet buried there in Mecca.

In the Hebrew: "You shall give them into mercies" — as if to say: You shall win for them favor with their enemies, so that they may obtain mercy from them, and be treated mercifully and be set free. "For he had fixed both knees to the ground." Let those noble, delicate, and vainglorious persons take note of this — those who pray with one knee fixed to the ground and the other raised, as if about to do battle with God unless He grant what they ask. Solomon, the King of kings, behold, bent both knees when praying, and stretched forth both hands toward heaven, and maintained this posture of supplication throughout the entire length of his prolonged prayer. And shall we, vile and poor little creatures that we are, disdain to bend both knees — so that by kneeling on one we seem to supplicate God, and by keeping the other raised we seem to place ourselves on a lesser level of reverence toward Him? "Blessed be the whole assembly of Israel" — as if to say: He gave his blessing to the whole congregation and assembly of the people of Israel. "May my words be near to the Lord" — as if to say: May the Lord be perpetually mindful of this prayer of mine, which I have poured out to Him on behalf of future posterity and ages to come. So Vatablus. "Because the Lord Himself is God, and there is none else besides Him" — as if to say: There is no other God besides Him, but He alone and solely is the true God. "Just as we have done today" — understand: may you likewise do in the future, so that with a perfect heart you may love God and keep all His commandments. There is added in 2 Paralipomenon 6:41: "Now therefore arise, O Lord God, into your resting place, you and the ark of your strength" — namely, that resting upon the ark you may rest in this temple built by me for you. He calls it the ark of strength, because through it God powerfully conquered Jericho and Canaan under the leadership of Joshua. Paralipomenon continues: "Your priests, O Lord God, are clothed with salvation" — so that they may be sound and safe on all sides, and clothed and girded round about with salvation as with a garment, that they may also be able to impart it to others, especially to the laity. "O Lord God, turn not away the face of your Christ" — as if to say: Do not turn away, O Lord, the prayers I have now set forth — mine, that is, Solomon's, who am your Christ, that is, the king of Israel anointed and established by you — but hear and fulfill them. He applies a further spur in his conclusion: "Remember the mercies of David your servant" — in the Hebrew, non chasde, that is, the acts of piety — as if to say: Grant me what I ask for the sake of the merits of David my father, who was a man of piety and merciful deeds, both actively and passively, for he showed many and illustrious works of mercy toward Saul and the other Israelites, and therefore in return received from you, O Lord, great mercy and grace. Moreover, that God heard the prayer of Solomon will be evident in the following chapter, verse 2. Note here that God hears more readily the prayers offered in the Temple as in His own house than those offered elsewhere, for the reasons I have set forth in 1 Timothy 2, verses 1 and 2. "And Solomon sacrificed peace offerings" — which were offered for peace, that is, for the well-being and happiness of himself and the people, both that already obtained and that to be obtained hereafter, according to the law of Leviticus 3 — "twenty-two thousand oxen and a hundred and twenty thousand sheep." Behold here once more the piety and magnificence of Solomon in the worship of God. "On that day the king consecrated the middle of the court that was before the house of the Lord." As if to say: On that day of the dedication of the Temple, Solomon through the high priest and the priests consecrated the middle part of the court of the priests, which was before the Sanctuary, so that in it many altars might be erected for offering upon them all these thousands of victims. For the single altar prescribed by God in Exodus 27, verse 1, could not contain all of these. Therefore the necessity of the occasion excused Solomon from sin, that he did not act against the divine law forbidding the building of any other altar — especially since at the same time...

That is, He appeared to him by night, as is said in 2 Chronicles 7:12, in a dream: for so He had appeared to him at Gibeon in chapter 3. *I have sanctified this house,*] that is, I have chosen this place for myself as a house of sacrifice, as is explained there.

Namely, for as long as the Temple shall exist and stand, it shall be called the house of the Lord, and there I will manifest myself, my presence, my grace, and my glory. *And mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually,*] as if to say: I will fix my eyes upon my house, and it shall be dear to me, held close to my heart and my care. 4. *But if thou also wilt walk before me, as thy father walked, in simplicity* (Hebrew: *betom*, that is, in integrity; Chaldean: in truth; Septuagint: in holiness) *of heart and in uprightness,*] Hebrew: *beioscer*, that is, in rectitude: meaning, if thou shalt serve me with a whole, true, holy, and perfect heart, so that thou givest thy whole heart to me, and dost not divide it, giving one part to me and the other to idols and concupiscences. For God demands our whole heart and our whole love, according to that saying: *Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength*, Deuteronomy 6:5 — see what is said there. Wherefore, explaining this further, He adds: *And if thou shalt do all things that I have commanded thee, and shalt keep my statutes* (laws) *and my judgments* (civil rights and judicial laws enacted by me) *and observe them.*

From me and my law. 7. *I will cut off Israel from the face of the land which I have given them: and the temple which I have sanctified* (dedicated) *to my name I will cast away,*] even as we now see, on account of the slain Christ, that the Jews are wanderers and vagabonds throughout the whole world, and their Temple demolished. So too has God frequently punished, and still punishes, Christians who slide into heresy and other crimes, by the destruction of their nation and kingdom, permitting their temples and monasteries to be burned and overthrown. 10. *And it came to pass at the end of twenty years* (from the beginning of the Temple's construction, namely in the twenty-fourth year of Solomon's reign) *after Solomon had built the two houses, that is, the house of the Lord and the house of the king,* etc.

You will ask whether Solomon could lawfully have alienated these cities from Israel and handed them over to Hiram, the gentile king of Tyre. The answer is that he could not lawfully have alienated them in perpetuity so as to make them part of Phoenician territory, because he would have wronged the Israelites against the law of God, who had assigned and given those cities to them. Furthermore, Hiram would have drawn these Galileans toward his idols and paganism. Wherefore he did not hand over to Hiram an absolute and full dominion over them, but only the usufruct — that is, so that Hiram might receive from them the revenues and tribute which Solomon was accustomed to collect, until he had recouped the expenses he had incurred on Solomon's behalf; or at least until the end of Hiram's own life, so that upon his death they would revert to the kingdom and kings of Israel. So hold Abulensis, Serarius, Salianus, and others.

Cabul in the Phoenician language, as Josephus says (lib. 8, c. 5), signifies "not pleasing" or "displeasing." So hold Abulensis, Cajetan, and others. In the Hebrew tongue, however, "sankhebet" signifies fetters or shackles; hence "Cabul" signifies a tenacious and muddy land which delays those who walk through it and, as it were, casts fetters upon them, so that they can scarcely make progress. So say R. David, Vatablus, and Pagninus. It could also be that "chabul" is derived by metathesis from "keleb," that is, "dog," as if to say "canine land"—vile and filthy like a dog. Hence Josephus says that Hiram was unwilling to accept these cities, and so they remained with Israel. **VERSE 15: THIS IS THE ACCOUNT OF THE LEVY WHICH KING SOLOMON RAISED TO BUILD THE HOUSE OF THE LORD, AND HIS OWN HOUSE, AND MELLO, AND THE WALL OF JERUSALEM, AND HAZER, ETC.** That is to say: this is the cause or the subject matter of so many expenses and outlays which Solomon made—namely, the construction of the Temple, his own house, Mello, and the rest. For in these building works he incurred the greatest expenses and exhausted his most opulent treasury. For in the Hebrew it reads: "This is the word of the tribute," that is, this is the reckoning and the cause of the great tribute which Solomon imposed upon the people (on account of which the people murmured and defected from Rehoboam to Jeroboam)—namely, that from it he might build the Temple and so many cities. For building works exhaust even the greatest royal resources. So hold Vatablus, Serarius, Sanchez, and others. A second rendering, following the Septuagint, would be: "This is the sum of the distribution"—that is, the sum into which Solomon distributed and expended his wealth and such great tributes—so that the total of the expenditures equals the total of the things upon which the expenditures were made, namely the construction of the Temple and of so many cities. For otherwise, strictly speaking, no actual total of Solomon's tribute or wealth is set out here. **"And he gave it as a dowry to his daughter, the wife of Solomon."** Now before, when she had been given in marriage to Solomon, he had given her a dowry. Therefore "as a dowry" here means as an increase or addition to the dowry. Hence the Hebrew has: "He gave her shillukhim"; the Septuagint has "apodolas," that is, dismissal-gifts, namely presents—meaning he gave it to her as a gift, as the Chaldean renders it.

In the Hebrew and in the Septuagint, the name is "Thamor," that is, "Palm." This city therefore appears to have been called Palmyra from the abundance of palm trees and palm groves flourishing there, as St. Jerome says in his commentary on Ezekiel, chapters 47 and 48. It was situated beyond the Jordan on the borders of Israel, belonging to the half-tribe of Manasseh, and lay at a distance of twenty-seven miles from Damascus. Solomon therefore built it there as if on the outermost limit of his empire and surrounded it with very strong walls. Afterward it was restored by the Emperor Hadrian and called Hadrianopolis. From this celebrated city the entire surrounding region and neighboring wilderness was called Palmyrena. See Adrichomius, page 92, number 73.

That is, all the Canaanites who remained in Judaea, whom the Hebrews had been unable to exterminate. You will ask whether Solomon sinned here, since God had commanded that all the Canaanites be slain. Abulensis replies that he did not sin, because not even his father David had slain them; hence either God had granted a dispensation in his law, or rather the law was not then binding, for the reason that all the danger which had been feared to Israel from the Canaanites had by then ceased—namely, the danger that the Canaanites would entice the Hebrews to their idols; for they had already been utterly suppressed by David and Solomon, so that they dared not do such a thing or raise their heads. Moreover, Solomon could have feared that, if he were to slay all the Canaanites, the Philistines and other nations might in revenge slaughter in like manner all the Jews dwelling among them, or even make war upon him. Add to this that the law commanding the slaying of the Canaanites pertained principally to those Canaanites who were living in the time of Joshua and thereafter, when the land was being conquered and possessed, as is indicated in this verse. So hold Cajetan and others.

Pineda is of the opinion that Solomon fitted out this fleet at the beginning of his reign, in order to gather gold for the building of the Temple. But David had already procured this in abundance beforehand. Hence Salianus thinks that Solomon equipped this fleet after the building of the Temple, in the twenty-fourth year of his reign, and draws this conclusion from verse 10; which statement is to be understood of his later and larger fleets. For it is clear from chapter 10, verse 12, that before the building of the Temple—in order to meet the immense cost of building both it and the palaces and cities—he fitted out this fleet to procure gold, since it is there said that Solomon made from the thyine wood brought from Ophir supports for the house of the Lord and for the royal house.
For the Tyrians and Phoenicians, being as they were dwellers upon the sea, were in every age and among all nations celebrated for their skill in seamanship and navigation.

You will ask: what is Ophir, and where is it? Some think it was Angola, where there are Ethiopians who were brought as slaves to Solomon, just as today they are brought from there into Spain. Others hold it to have been Malacca; others Sofala; others, with Gubbio, inner Persia; others Africa. There are two more probable opinions. The first is that Ophir is the kingdom of Peru in the West Indies — both because there is found there gold in the greatest abundance and of the finest quality, which is exported from there almost every year into Spain; and because "Ophir" by a metathesis of letters is "Peru"; and furthermore because the gold of Ophir is called the gold of Peruaim, that is, of the two Perus, namely lesser and greater Peru (2 Chronicles 3:7). So hold Vatablus, Arias, Genebrardus, and others, who are of the opinion that the New World was discovered and made known by Solomon. The second and more common opinion is that Ophir was situated in the East Indies, and accordingly embraces several of its gold-bearing regions: such as the Golden Chersonese, so named from its abundance of gold; Sumatra and Taprobane, Pegu, Ceylon, and the other gold-bearing regions of the East Indies. So hold Sanchez, Serarius, Salianus, Gaspar Varerius in his book on Ophir, and Josephus Acosta in his first book on the Nature of the New World, chapters 13 and 14. In favor of this view are Josephus, Hesychius, Theodoret, Procopius, and Saint Jerome in his Epistle 140 to Principia, who asserts that Ophir is named after Ophir the son of Heber (Genesis 10, verse 29). This is proved in the first place because this fleet set sail from Ezion-geber, situated on the Red Sea, that is, in the Arabian Gulf. From this point the voyage is easy, direct, and short, by way of the Cape of Comorin to Sumatra and Taprobane, which lies directly opposite, as is clear from cosmographical charts. It would, however, have been an exceedingly long, plainly dangerous, and tortuous voyage from the Red Sea to Peru, for it would have been necessary either to sail eastward around all of the East Indies, Malacca, Japan, and China, and from there to turn the route toward America; or certainly to circumnavigate westward the Cape of Good Hope and the whole of Africa and the vast ocean, and thence by way of Brazil, Patagonia, Chile, and the Strait of Magellan to reach Peru. Yet the voyage to Peru would have been far easier and shorter from Joppa and Tyre, that is, from the Mediterranean Sea, just as it is today from Seville in Spain. In the second place, because in that age the use of the magnet was unknown; wherefore sailors measured their courses by the pole and the polar stars, and thus kept close to familiar shores, nor did they dare to pass beyond Cadiz — where Hercules fixed his columns as if to mark the boundary of navigation, which no man should venture to cross — much less to cross the equinoctial line. Hence Cadiz was regarded as the outermost limit of the earth, even by the Tyrians themselves, as Strabo testifies in book 3, Diodorus in book 5, and Velleius Paterculus in book 1. Whence arose the old proverb: "As far as Cadiz." Moreover, in the work of Peter Apianus the First on Antiquities, the following monument at Cadiz is recorded: *Heliodorus the madman of Carthage commanded that he be buried here at the edge of the world, with his tomb as his testament, so that he might see whether anyone more mad than himself should ever penetrate to this place to visit him.* In the third place, if the West Indies and the navigation thither had been discovered and well-traveled by Solomon, the memory and continuation of such discovery would surely have persisted; for the desire for gold would have driven all men to pursue it. But in fact no memory of it survives whatsoever; indeed, before Amerigo Vespucci — from whom the land is called America — that entire territory was regarded by all as new and unknown. Add to this that from the Arabian Gulf, that is, the Red Sea, the navigation of the Tyrians and others to the East Indies was at that time frequent, as Pineda demonstrates from ancient authorities. In the fourth place, because Sumatra, Taprobane, and the neighboring regions abound in excellent gold, gems, thyine wood, ivory, and other precious commodities which are recorded as having been found in Ophir and Tarshish.

That is to say, she was carried out of herself by prior admiration and astonishment, as Vatable states. Allegorically, St. Gregory on Psalm 7, the seventh penitential psalm, verse 7, taking this queen to signify the Church, says: "The Church of the Elect, gathered from the Gentiles, having come to know the grace of Christ and having found the teachers of evangelical doctrine, casting aside the spirit of pride and laying down all the arrogance of self-exaltation, learned to distrust herself and to place great hope in the mercy of her King."

This is more truly fulfilled in Christ, the antitype of Solomon, as He Himself teaches in Luke 11:31. In the anagogical sense, it will be most perfectly true in heaven, where we shall see the God of gods in Sion, and shall no longer have breath left us for admiration of that glory which eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, nor hath it entered into the heart of man. So says Angelomus. And indeed, as Eucherius says, that which God prepares for those who love Him is not comprehended by faith, is not attained by hope, is not contained by charity, and surpasses all desires and prayers; it can be acquired, but it cannot be valued. Man shall see his merits grow beyond measure through an unsuspected largesse of rewards; he shall have from the receiving of it fruitfulness, but he shall have from its abundance no weariness.

In Hebrew, *almugim*, or *algumim*, concerning which I have spoken at Apocalypse 10:12. The Chaldean renders it: "Woods of corals, which beneath the water have the appearance of woody shrubs, but when brought up into the air harden into stone." Vatable renders it: "Brazilian wood"; others: "Ebony." The Septuagint renders it *πελεκητά*, that is, hewn timber. Angelomus says: "Thyine woods are incorruptible and thorny, in the likeness of the white thorn." Our Vulgate and the Septuagint render it thus at 2 Paralipomenon 2:8. Here our Vulgate renders it *thyina*, because these unknown woods which the Hebrews call *almugim* were similar to the wood of the *thyia* tree, concerning which Pliny writes as follows in book 3, chapter 16: Theophrastus, who was the first to write after the age of Alexander the Great concerning events that took place around the four hundred and fortieth year of the city of Rome, already bestows great honour upon this tree, recording how the crossbeams of ancient temples were made from it. By crossbeams he understands transverse beams, such as are also described as the supporting timbers of the house of the Lord; from which it is clear that the tree was of very great height. He adds further that there is a kind of immortality in its timber, which in its straight grain is incorruptible against all decay. Nothing is more intricate than its root, nor from any other source are there more precious works. So writes Pliny from Theophrastus. Hence you may rightly conclude that the musical instruments were fashioned from this tree. Moreover, Theophrastus himself states that this tree grows in Egypt near the shrine of Ammon and in the region of Cyrene, resembling the cypress in its branches, leaves, trunk, and fruit, and most especially like the wild cypress. So Theophrastus in book 5, chapter 5. Furthermore, at 2 Paralipomenon 2:8, our Vulgate and the Septuagint add *ligna arceuthina*, that is, juniper wood; more recent translators render it as fir wood: for the Hebrew word *ברושים* signifies fir; because Hebrew names apply to many things and signify several different objects.
By *fulcra* understand beams. It is added at 2 Paralipomenon 9:11 that from these same thyine woods Solomon also made steps for the house of the Lord.

After she had inspected all the works and words of Solomon, and had therefore remained with him for many months, or a year, as some hold, conversing and meeting with him frequently and intimately, and indeed, as many writers report, conceiving a son by him. For it is so firmly established among the Ethiopians that their Emperors trace their origin from Solomon and the Queen of Sheba, that the matter is entirely beyond controversy among them, and all their books are found to be full of it. And for this reason their Emperors are called Israelites, and they glory in that name. And lest the kingdom might ever pass by inheritance to others, the sons of the Emperors are customarily kept on the mountain of Guixen-Amba, so that, should the male offspring who would succeed the reigning Emperor be lacking, they might be able to receive an Emperor from that place. The history of the Queen of Sheba, and the manner in which she received offspring from Solomon, is related thus in the most ancient books preserved in the Church of Axum. A certain merchant named Tamarin, returning from Jerusalem, had described to the Queen of Sheba the glory and wisdom of Solomon which he had seen and heard; and she was inflamed with so great a desire to behold the king that she immediately began to set about making the journey, and at last, leaving her homeland and kingdom, set out for Jerusalem attended by a great retinue of her nobles and escorted with truly royal splendour, so great indeed that even in Jerusalem itself she was an object of wonder. There she was received most magnificently by Solomon, and after seven months, being now instructed in the true worship of the one God and in all those things she had desired to know, when she was preparing to return to her homeland and kingdom, she was invited by Solomon to a most sumptuous banquet, spent the night in his palace, and conceived a child by Solomon, whom she afterwards brought to light after nine months and five days had passed, just as she was entering the borders of her own kingdom. The son born of her bears multiple names in the books preserved in the Church of Axum, namely: BaineLehequem, EbnaaLehaquon, EbnehaqiLem, Menilehec — which last name is most frequently heard as Preto Janes among the Ethiopians in common usage. All these names mean the same thing in the ancient tongue, namely "he resembles him"; the remaining names mean the same as "son of the wise man." When he had grown older, he repeatedly asked his mother about his father, and at last, in his twenty-second year, having come to know the truth of the matter, he obtained by entreaty from her the permission to go to Solomon; and guided by that merchant Tamarin, he arrived at Jerusalem with a great retinue and retinue, and there was received by his father and recognized as his son. When he wished to return to his mother, Solomon commanded him to be duly inaugurated as King of Ethiopia with the customary insignia, and accordingly, having been brought into the Temple by the priests Sadoc and Joas, he was anointed by the very hand of the son of Solomon's chief prince, and was called David after his grandfather; and with the whole people acclaiming "Long live the king!", he was led through the city riding upon the royal mule. After these things he departed to his mother, laden with the gifts received from his father, and took with him the son of the priest Sadoc, named Zacharias; and he introduced into his household all those offices which were in use in the house of Solomon, some of which those Emperors retain even to this day. The Ethiopians claim that he also carried away with him together with the aforesaid Zacharias the very Ark of the Lord, which they themselves call "Sion" — unless one should say that another ark, fashioned in likeness of that of Moses and Solomon, was made and given to him. Received by his mother, he was raised to the kingship, and from that time the custom was introduced of creating sons of the king as kings, whereas before this only virgins who had always remained unmarried were accustomed to be created queens — such as the Candaces who was queen after Christ, Acts 8:27. All these things are reported by Father Emmanuel Paz of our Society, who...

In 2 Chronicles, chapter 9, verse 15, shields are put in place of spears, but these are called "spear-shields" (hastae) because they are shields fashioned after the likeness of spears; thus they are also "bossed shields" (clypei hastati), that is, having a boss and a point, and they serve equally to protect the body and to pierce the enemy through. Vilalpandus shows the same thing at page 527, drawing on Polybius, who says that the more violent blows of enemies can be deflected by the boss; and on Suetonius, who in his Life of Julius Caesar, chapter 68, says that by the same means the enemy can be pushed back, or even laid low, as Livy in book 10 also signifies. This is the Hebrew word *isinna*, from the root *Isanum*, meaning "to sharpen" and "to be sharp." Hence our translator renders *isinna* sometimes as "shield," sometimes as "spear," sometimes as "breastplate," sometimes as "pike." So also the Septuagint. See Pineda, book 5 on Solomon, chapter 6, number 1. **He gave six hundred shekels of gold for the plates of one shield.** A shekel is a half-ounce; six hundred shekels are therefore six hundred half-ounces, which make 25 pounds. Wherefore these shields, no less than the pelts, were fashioned not so much for warfare — for what soldier could carry into battle a shield of 25 pounds weight? — as for magnificence and display. Hence Solomon placed them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon, as follows.

A *scutum* is a large shield covering almost the whole man; a *pelta* is a smaller shield, having the form of a crescent moon. Hence Virgil in Aeneid 1: *"He leads the companies of the Amazons with their crescent-shaped shields."* **Three hundred minas of gold clothed one pelta.** The Hebrew mina contains 60 shekels, that is, 30 ounces, or two and a half pounds; three hundred minas of gold therefore make 750 pounds. But because this weight seems enormous for a small shield, some commentators understand by "mina" here the shekel, of which mention was made in the preceding verse; for in the Hebrew text of this verse the expression is entirely parallel: for just as there it said that each large shield was of six hundred pieces of gold, namely shekels — since the weight of the shekel was the most common unit of reckoning among the Hebrews — so here it says that each pelta was of three hundred pieces of gold, namely shekels. Thus also Josephus uses "mina" in place of "shekel," when he says that Joseph was sold by his brothers for twenty minas, that is, twenty pieces of silver. Hence the Septuagint and the Chaldaic paraphrase render "three minas" in place of "three hundred shekels" — from which one might suspect that our translator, who commonly follows the Septuagint, rendered "three" rather than "thirty." Furthermore, the Roman mina, like the Attic mina, contained 25 shekels, that is, 100 drachmas, equivalent to one hundred gold pieces; for a gold piece has the weight of one drachma; wherefore three Attic minas weigh and are worth three hundred gold pieces. It is better, as I have said, to understand by "three hundred gold pieces" the shekels, each of which weighed and was worth four drachmas, that is, four gold pieces. # GEM

The throne served both that the king might sit upon it magnificently, as it were upon a royal seat, and also that he might as a judge decide the suits and causes of his people thereon — for to sit upon a throne is to judge — so that to all who desired to see him, hear him, and consult him, he might give ear and answer. **Allegorically.** Solomon here represented the judicial power given by God to Christ as man, and His glorious throne, upon which He will sit at the Day of Judgment and judge all the Tribes of Israel. So say Rupert, Eucherius, and Angelomus. Hear Eucherius: *"The ivory throne bearing the resplendent power of eternal judgment gleaming with the gold of divinity, which the Lord as man received from the Father, there is no doubt bore a figure. The six steps of this throne typologically demonstrate the whole visible and invisible creation, which was made in six days and subjected to Christ the Lord by the Father. But that the throne itself is described as rounded at its rear part, this is doubtless given to be understood because the present world, which revolves through the boundaries of the ages, is in its final extent to be judged by the Lord. But the twelve lions standing two by two beside each of the six steps prefigured the power of the holy Apostles, to whom the Lord said: 'You shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve Tribes of Israel.'"* **Symbolically.** This throne signifies the Blessed Virgin, from whom the true Solomon, that is, the eternal Wisdom of God, took flesh, and dwelt as upon His throne — more pure than ivory, more noble than the lions, and more brilliant than gold through charity — for nine months; whence she is called and invoked by the Church in the Litanies as the Throne of Solomon. So Lyran. Here the Blessed Peter Damian delivered a notable sermon on this Throne of Solomon at the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin, in which he elegantly adapts and applies it to the Virgin, saying: *"He made a throne — that is, the womb of the inviolate Virgin — in which that Majesty sat. This dwelling of the Son both the Father approved and acknowledged, He Himself saying: 'Thou hast known my sitting down.' And: 'Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever.' And: 'This throne shall be as the sun before Thee.' And after a little: 'And a voice went out from the throne saying: Give praise to our God, all ye His saints.' And from the throne,"* he says, *"that is, from the Virgin, the praise of angels and men bursts forth; because when the one is restored, the other is repaired, and both owe their devotion in thanksgiving. Dost thou know what He says who sits upon the throne? 'Behold, I make all things new.' Blessed the throne upon which sat the Lord of lords, in whom and through whom not only all persons but all things are renewed."* And after a little more: *"What is greater than the Virgin Mary, who enclosed within the secret place of her womb the greatness of the most high divinity? Contemplate the Seraphim, and soar above the dignity of that higher nature, and thou shalt see that whatsoever is greater is yet less than the Virgin, and that only the Maker surpasses this His work."* See more in the same author, where he applies the individual details of this throne of Solomon to the Blessed Virgin.

For the lion was the symbol of the tribe of Judah, of David and Solomon, on account of their strength and dominion (Genesis 49:5), and likewise of Christ, their antitype (Apocalypse 5:5; see what is said there). Symbolically, the lion denotes vigilance (for the lion sleeps with open eyes) and fortitude, which a king and judge ought to possess, so that he may repel and break the strength of the wicked. See Ecclesiasticus 7:6: "Seek not," it says, "to be made a judge, unless thou have strength enough to extirpate iniquities, lest thou fear the person of the mighty, and lay a stumbling-block in the way of thy integrity." See what is said there. **Verse 21.** THERE WAS NO SILVER — that is, among the vessels and furnishings of Solomon, because all of these were of gold; or rather, it was not, that is, in estimation. Hence, explaining this, the text adds: NOR WAS IT ACCOUNTED OF ANY VALUE — for in verse 22 it adds that from Tarshish there was brought to Solomon both silver and gold. **Verse 22.** FOR THE KING'S FLEET SAILED THE SEA TOGETHER WITH THE FLEET OF HIRAM; ONCE IN THREE YEARS IT WENT TO TARSHISH, BRINGING THENCE GOLD AND SILVER, AND THE TEETH OF ELEPHANTS (ivory), AND APES, AND PEACOCKS. You may ask whether this fleet of Solomon sailing to Tarshish is the same as the fleet sailing to Ophir, of which verse 11 speaks. Some hold that it was one and the same: for both Ophir and Tarshish, they say, denote the vast sea and most remote regions, such as India, whether Eastern or Western. So Joseph Acosta, book 1, *On the New World*, chapter 14, and others. These scholars therefore take Ophir and Tarshish to mean the Indian Sea and its trading-ports, to which the fleet of Solomon sailed, departing from Ezion-geber situated on the Red Sea; just as the fleet of Jehoshaphat, departing from the same place, sailed to Tarshish, that is, to the Eastern Indies, as is said in 2 Chronicles 20:36. Joseph the Hebrew suggests the same. Others more plainly hold that there were two fleets: for the one went to Ophir, this one to Tarshish; the former brought from Ophir the gold of Ophir and thyine wood; the latter brought from Tarshish gold and silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks; the former went annually to Ophir, as is clear from verse 14; this one went to Tarshish once every three years, as is said here; the former set sail from Ezion-geber situated in the Arabian Gulf; this one from Joppa situated on the Mediterranean Sea. Therefore, by Tarshish here understand the Mediterranean Sea. For Tarshish was a son of Javan (Genesis 10), who was the first after the flood to inhabit Cilicia, whose metropolis is Tarsus; thence the neighbouring sea, and finally the remainder of the Mediterranean, came to be called Tarshish, for the Cilicians once held dominion over nearly all of it. Thus in Jonah 1:3: *He fled to Tarshish*, that is, to the Mediterranean Sea. So Ribera on that passage, Salianus, and others. This fleet, therefore, setting sail from Tyre or from Joppa, sailed into Tarshish, that is, into the Mediterranean Sea adjacent to it, and visited various shores, ports, and trading-stations of Africa, Europe, and Asia — of Arabia, Anatolia, Greece, Dalmatia, Gaul, Italy, Spain, Mauritania, Cyprus, Crete, Sicily, Sardinia, the Cyclades, and so on. It traversed the seas adjacent to all these places, and whatever was anywhere distinguished and precious it purchased on Solomon's behalf. Hence, making use of many stopping-places, it did not return home until the third year, as Josephus says. If anyone wishes to hold that it proceeded further from the Mediterranean into the Ocean and sailed to Brazil, Peru, and Mexico, as the Spaniards do today, I shall not object; and I would grant this more readily of this fleet than of the other which sailed to Ophir, for this one spent three years in the voyage out and back, whereas the other —

*From Coa* — in the Hebrew this is מִקְוֵה, which our Vulgate and the Septuagint take as a proper name of a place, pointing and reading מִקּוֹא *miccoa*, and translating: *from Coa* — though in the Septuagint it is corruptly read as *de Ecue* or *Thecue*. Coa appears to have been a city or region of western Egypt where there was a most celebrated trade in horses, of which Stephanus Byzantius speaks. So Serarius. The Chaldean, however, reading with different vowel-points מִקְוֵה *micue*, translates: *A company of the king's merchants bought a collection at a price*. Those who follow the Chaldean — Rabbi David, Rabbi Solomon, Rabbi Joseph, and Rabbi Levi, as cited by Pagninus — explain this variously, but the sum of all their interpretations is this: that a multitude of merchants, for a given price, brought out of Egypt a multitude either of linen, or of linen cloth, or of horses, or of other goods. But one must stand by the version of St. Jerome and the Septuagint. Some have thought that Coa is Goa, which is the seat of the viceroy of the King of Portugal in the Eastern Indies; but this is very far distant from Egypt and Jerusalem. Furthermore, that the dignity and magnificence of a king consists chiefly in the number and splendour of his bodyguard, cavalry, and chariots is taught from Athenaeus, Aelian, Xenophon, and others by Brisson, book 1, *On the Kingdom of the Persians*. Hence Julian the Apostate, when he had stripped away the pomp of the palace, rendered his reign contemptible, as Socrates says, book 5, *History*, chapter 1, and Cassiodorus, book 6, *Tripartite History*, chapter 1. But in this matter, as in all others, moderation must be observed.

*A chariot* — that is, four horses which draw a chariot or quadriga. For each horse cost one hundred and fifty shekels; therefore four horses cost six hundred shekels; and accordingly the chariot itself was to be purchased at a further price. So Abulensis. A shekel of silver is a half-ounce, or four silver drachmas — that is, four Roman *julii*, or four Spanish *reales*, which make one Brabantine or Belgian florin. Six hundred shekels therefore are six hundred Brabantine florins. AND IN THIS MANNER ALL THE KINGS OF THE HITTITES AND OF SYRIA SOLD HORSES. Our Interpreter has translated correctly. For the particle *lamed* prefixed, though it ordinarily marks the dative case, is here subtly the mark of the ablative, and is equivalent to *from*, *out of*, *concerning*. Hence from the Hebrew one may translate: *And thus from all the kings of the Hittites and of the Syrians they went out through their hands* — that is, they were sold to Solomon. The Septuagint, the Chaldean, and the Rabbis, however, taking *lamed* as a mark of the dative, translate in the contrary sense: *Thus (at six hundred shekels) they brought horses out of Egypt to all the kings of the Hittites and of the Syrians*. See Abulensis, who ably defends the Vulgate version here. # GEM Finally, so great an abundance of all things, such happiness and glory, was given by God to Solomon on account of his wisdom and virtue, to the end that all the Israelites might imitate the same, in hope of obtaining the same rewards — and thus serve the true God, not idols, seeing that the true God gave to Solomon alone more than all the idols gave to their worshippers. For the Israelites, being coarse and rude, apprehended and savoured nothing but earthly things, as their descendants the Jews likewise do today. Secondly, so that the kings of the Syrians, Egyptians, Arabians, and indeed of the whole world, might be stirred to worship the God of Solomon, that they might obtain from God a like magnificence to that which Solomon had attained — as the Queen of Sheba did, and other wise kings. Thirdly, so that in Solomon He might give a foretaste and a faint image of that eternal happiness and glory which He has prepared from eternity for His elect in heaven. Fourthly, to show in Solomon how vain is all the wealth and glory of this world, inasmuch as it vanished in an instant, like smoke, with Solomon himself, so that men might say: *Thus passes the glory of the world*. Wherefore true happiness and glory consist in heavenly and eternal goods, and it is these that men should desire — and God showed this clearly in Solomon, whose happiness was pure unhappiness, as will presently appear; for his glory was soon turned to disgrace, his fame to infamy, and his wealth to poverty: for his son Rehoboam, having lost ten tribes of Israel, barely retained the single tribe of Judah alone. CHAPTER ELEVEN.

Chamosh is the god of drunkenness, that is, of Bacchus; whence from him the word "carousing" (*comessatio*) is derived. See the remarks on Romans 13:13, "not in carousing." The cause of so great a fall was Solomon's excessive prosperity, wealth, pleasures, and lusts. The proximate cause was his wives, whom he loved with the most ardent passion; for these drove him to madness, according to that saying: "Wine and women cause the wise to apostatize." Learn here how greatly women are to be avoided; for love of them is irresistible, and extorts from those who are captivated by it whatever one might wish, however precious or however wicked. Flee therefore from them, for you are neither wiser than Solomon, nor stronger than Samson, nor holier than David, all of whom fell through women. Wisely does Eucherius say here: "Those must be warned," he says, "upon whom this age bestows prosperity, who are struck by no adversities of this world, because Solomon is described as having fallen even into idolatry after wisdom had been received, since it is recorded that before his fall he experienced no adversity whatsoever in this world; but wisdom, once granted, utterly forsook him, because no discipline of even slight tribulation preserved him, until after he had forsaken God and recognized that he had been forsaken by God." And St. Bernard, in Book 2 of *On Consideration* to Eugenius, near the end, says: "Mark how rare it has ever been that anyone has not relaxed his soul, at least in some small degree, from self-custody and discipline in times of prosperity. When has this not worked upon the incautious as fire upon wax, as the sun's ray upon snow or ice? David was wise, Solomon was wise; but one, seduced by the excessive allurements of prosperous circumstances, fell away partly, the other entirely. Great is he who, falling into adversity, does not deviate even a little from wisdom. Nor lesser is he upon whom present happiness has smiled, not mocked. Though you will more easily find those who retained wisdom when fortune was contrary to them than those who did not lose it when fortune was favorable."

Because you have brought it into your heart to apostatize from Me and to worship idols; or, "because there has been found in you the wickedness of apostasy and idolatry." "I WILL TEAR YOUR KINGDOM" — into two parts, namely into the kingdom of Judah and of Israel (that is, the ten tribes) — "AND I WILL GIVE IT TO YOUR SERVANT," that is, to Jeroboam, whom I will make king of Israel in chapter 12. # GEM

One tribe, namely beyond his own — meaning: beyond the tribe of Judah, from which David was descended, and which on that account is considered the proper inheritance of his descendants — I will add also one other tribe, namely Benjamin. So Abulensis; or, as Cajetan holds, the tribes of Judah and Benjamin were regarded as one and the same, and were called the kingdom of Judah. Furthermore, Abulensis, Torniellus, and Salianus are of the opinion that God spoke these things to Solomon not directly nor through an angel, since Solomon was unworthy of such a visitation, but through Ahijah the Shilonite, of whom verse 29 speaks. Pineda, however, in Book 3, chapter 2, number 5, is of the opinion that God here appeared to Solomon a third time in person; for the words simply signify this, when in verse 11 it is said: "And the Lord said to Solomon." Finally, Solomon made no reply to God; hence he appears to have persisted in his sin. You will ask whether Solomon was damned or saved: this is an ancient problem. Those who hold that he was damned include Tertullian, Book 2 *On Prescription*, chapter 2; Cyprian, Letter 73; Augustine, *City of God* 17, chapters 8 and 20; St. Chrysostom, sermon *On Penance*; Bede, on Book 3 of Kings, chapter 6; Hrabanus, on Book 4 of Kings, chapter 23; Isidore, *On the Life and Death of the Prophets*, chapter 24; Lyranus, Abulensis, and others in this place. The first reason is that Scripture, in recording his so grievous fall, does not add an account of his repentance, as it does in the case of David's fall. Secondly, because he himself did not destroy the shrines he had built for the idols: for it was Josiah who at last destroyed them. Some reply that he had blocked them up or demolished them to their foundations, and that they were rebuilt by the impious and came to be called the high places of Solomon; but they assert this without proving it. Thirdly, because God seems to have heaped all good things upon Solomon in this life for this purpose — that his merits might be rewarded in this life and his crimes punished in the life to come — according to the word of Abraham to the rich man: "Son, remember that you received your good things in your lifetime, and likewise Lazarus evil things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in torment." Fourthly, because an ardent love of women, confirmed by long habit, is almost irresistible; and such was that of Solomon, who was so hemmed in on all sides by the deeply beloved objects of his passion that even if he had wished to repent, he could not. To this point is what befell one of our Society a few years ago in Italy. He was summoned to attend a certain man who kept a concubine at the hour of death. He attended with all his strength, exhorting him to repentance, urging him to bid farewell to his illicit loves — now by gentle entreaties, now by threatening the impending judgment of God and hell. But even the concubine, weeping, begged him to repent and to take thought for his soul's salvation in dying; all was sung in vain to deaf ears. For he said that he neither would nor could renounce the beloved friend he had cherished for so long. And so he breathed forth his wretched soul unrepentant. On the other side, the ancient Rabbis, and following them St. Jerome, St. Gregory Thaumaturgus, Rupertus, Pineda, Salianus, Barradius, Serrarius, Sanchez, Delrio, and others, are of the opinion that he repented and was saved. They assert that after his fall he wrote Ecclesiastes, in which, as one penitent, he cries out that he had given himself over to vain loves and idols: *Vanity of vanities, and all is vanity*. This argument would be compelling if it were established that Ecclesiastes was written by Solomon after his fall and not before; concerning which matter I have spoken in the preface to Ecclesiastes. For his sayings and writings are reviewed at the beginning of this book, chapters 4 and 5, before his fall; whereas his fall is narrated only in old age, in this chapter 11. They prove their case first because God promised David in 2 Samuel 7 that if Solomon sinned, He would correct him with the rod of men — that is, with the rod by which men...

It is probable, as Cajetan judges, that this came about as follows: the garrison of David, which had been stationed in Edom after he had subjugated it, was slain by the Edomites after his death. Joab therefore went there to bury the fallen and to avenge their slaughter; and thus he slew all the males of the Edomites, with the exception perhaps of young children, who are exempted by the law of Deuteronomy 20:14.

This appears to have been the historical sequence which Scripture here briefly summarizes. Hadad, a boy of royal Edomite blood, fled—or rather was carried away by servants—into Egypt when David was devastating Edom. In Egypt he found favor with Pharaoh, so much so that Pharaoh gave him the sister of his own wife as a spouse. When Hadad heard that David, the devastator of Edom, had died, he sought from Pharaoh permission to return to his kingdom of Edom, which Pharaoh at length granted. In the third year of Solomon, therefore, he returned to Edom, being then about thirty years of age, as the Abulensis says; and there, it appears, with Solomon's goodwill and through the mediation of Pharaoh—who had bound both men to himself by ties of kinship—he reigned in Edom as a tributary king, under the same terms and conditions of tribute that David had imposed. For from that time onward nothing is found to have been altered or disturbed in Edom. But in the final years of Solomon, seeing that king grown effeminate, enfeebled, and contemptible, Hadad refused him tribute, and by rebellion vindicated his own freedom and that of his Edom. So say the Abulensis, Salianus, and others.

Rezon, as the text continues, having made himself a captain of brigands, established himself as king of Damascus. This punishment was just and fitting: that against Solomon, who was rebelling against God, both his subjects and those conquered in war should likewise rebel. Thus God raises up adversaries against a sinner, to goad him and compel him to return to God. **Verse 25: And Rezon was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon; and this is the evil of Hadad, and the hatred against Israel, and he reigned in Syria.** Josephus, Cajetan, and others explain this as follows: that Hadad joined himself to Rezon, and strengthened by his aid, rebelled against Solomon and made himself king of Syria. But they are mistaken: for it was Rezon who made himself king of Syria, that is, of Damascus, as was said in the preceding verse concerning Hadad. Hadad, as son of the king of Edom, reigned in his own Edom. The meaning therefore is: Rezon was an adversary of Israel and of Solomon, and thus increased the evil of Hadad's hostility against Israel, and reigned in Syria—that is, Rezon himself reigned, not Hadad. Hence the Chaldean renders it: *And Rezon was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon, and together with the evil that Hadad had done, he rebelled against Israel and reigned over Aram*, that is, Syria—meaning that Rezon along with Hadad rebelled against Solomon and afflicted Israel, and made himself king of Syria. So Vatablus, Pineda, and Salianus. This is clearly indicated by the Roman Septuagint, which transposes and joins these words to verse 22, reading thus: *And Ader returned to his own land; this is the evil that Ader did, and he weighed heavily upon, or afflicted, Israel, and reigned in the land of Edom.* For instead of Aram they read Edom—the letters resh and daleth being very similar, so that one is frequently substituted for the other; just as here the Septuagint reads Ader for Adad. Scripture therefore concludes by joining together Solomon's two enemies, namely Hadad and Rezon, and then adding in the following verse a third and greatest enemy, namely Jeroboam. Finally, the two interpretations can be reconciled by saying that Hadad first reigned in Edom, and then migrated from it—being mountainous and barren—to Syria, which was rich and fertile, and upon the death of Rezon, his partner in rebellion, succeeded him in the kingdom of Syria. For Rezon's son Benhadad was king of Syria, as is clear from 3 Kings 20:1; indeed, from Hadad onward the subsequent kings of Syria were called Benhadad, that is, sons and successors of Hadad, and therefore enemies of Israel. So say St. Jerome in his commentary on Amos chapter 1, the Rabbis, and Vatablus on 3 Kings 6:28.

Jeroboam was the third enemy of Solomon. The Hebrews cited by St. Jerome in his *Hebrew Traditions* hold that Nabat is the same as Shimei who cursed David, and who was therefore slain by Solomon; and that consequently his son Jeroboam conceived a deep and, as it were, congenital hatred of David and Solomon, inherited from his father, and for this reason rebelled. But they are mistaken: for Shimei was of the tribe of Benjamin, whereas Jeroboam was an Ephrathean, that is, of the tribe of Ephraim—a tribe that was arrogant, jealous of Judah, and hungry for dominion. Hence in Jeroboam that ambition prevailed and held sway through nearly the entire period of the kings of Israel. Salianus demonstrates the arrogance and turbulent character of the Ephraimites with many examples, in the year of the world 3057, number 22. **A widow woman.** So read the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Septuagint here; but in the following chapter the Roman Septuagint has *a harlot* or *prostitute*, and so reads Lucifer of Cagliari in his first book *On the Apostate Kings*. **He lifted his hand against the king** — after he had received from the prophet Ahijah ten strips of the torn garment, and through them the promise from God of the ten tribes' kingdom, as follows; for then he appears to have solicited the ten tribes through the revelation of this prophecy, that they should defect from Solomon, so harsh a collector of tribute, to himself as one who would be more lenient. Perhaps also he openly rebelled against Solomon and seized the kingdom, as these words suggest. So Josephus, Pineda, and Salianus; although Abulensis and Torniellus hold that Jeroboam rebelled even before Ahijah's prophecy. In this he acted wrongly: for God here promised him through Ahijah only the kingdom of the ten tribes, but did not actually give it to him at this time—rather, only after Solomon's death under Rehoboam. Therefore Jeroboam rose up unjustly and iniquitously against Solomon his king. So the Abulensis. Just as when Samuel secretly anointed David as king, he did not thereby give him the right to the kingdom immediately, but that he should reign after the death of Saul—hence David, while Saul lived, attempted nothing regarding the kingdom. Finally, Salianus is of the opinion that Jeroboam likewise attempted nothing against Solomon, but is said only to have "lifted his hand against the king" because he had been designated king against Solomon by Ahijah, and had accepted this designation of Ahijah's. For in common speech, one who accepts a kingdom offered to him—even if it is offered—is said to rebel against the reigning king. Serarius, however, says that he lifted his hand against Solomon because he raised it against Rehoboam, Solomon's son. But it is the proper meaning of this scriptural phrase that he devised something against Solomon immediately, and this is confirmed by the text adding: *And this is the reason for his rebellion against him.* He therefore rebelled against Solomon himself. Hence Josephus in book 8, chapter 2, says: *Jeroboam, elated by the words of the prophet to great hopes, being by nature of a restless and ambitious disposition, as soon as he arrived in his own province, not unmindful of the prophetic presage, began to solicit the people to defect from Solomon and confer the principate upon himself.* Josephus is followed by Torniellus, Pineda, Abulensis, and others.

# GEM so that you may know by this that you shall lift from the twelve tribes of Solomon's kingdom, and shall claim for yourself ten tribes of Israel, as Ahijah presently explains. Symbolically, these ten tears signified that the kingdom of the ten tribes would be rent into ten families succeeding one another in turn, and divided ten times, says Rupert. For such was the order and succession of the kings of Israel, or of the ten tribes. The first tearing away of the ten tribes from the two was made by Jeroboam. His son, says Rupert in book 5, chapter 14, named Nadab, was struck down by Baasha. This was the second division of the kingdom of Israel. The same Baasha's son Elah was killed by Zimri, who reigned in his place. Zimri burned himself in the palace when Omri besieged him, and Omri reigned in his place. After him reigned Ahab; after him his son Ahaziah; and after him his brother Joram. But Jehu slew Joram and reigned in his place unto the fourth generation. The fifth from Jehu was struck down by Shallum, who reigned in his place. But Shallum too was struck down by Menahem, who reigned in his place. His son Pekahiah likewise was struck down by Pekah son of Remaliah, who reigned in his place. And against Pekah Hoshea conspired, struck him, slew him, and reigned in his place. Against Hoshea came up the king of Assyria and carried Israel away into Assyria. This was the tenth division. For in Jeroboam was the first, in Baasha the second, in Zimri the third, in Omri the fourth, in Jehu the fifth, in Shallum the sixth, in Menahem the seventh, in Pekah son of Remaliah the eighth, in Hoshea the ninth, and in the king of Assyria—who divided those already divided from God and from David their king, by dividing them also in place, transferring them to Assyria—is rightly counted the tenth. Tropologically, Jeroboam the first schismatic represents heretics who rend the Church by their heresy, and especially princes who, in order to seize the kingdom of their orthodox sovereign, introduce heresy and summon heretics to their aid—as Jeroboam did (and we have seen the same done in the Low Countries)—and who for this reason lose the kingdom, as God here threatens Jeroboam in prophecy. Hence Angelomus aptly says: *Jeroboam in Hebrew is interpreted as "one who judges the people"; for heretics too seem to judge the people when they make them followers of their error. Then is fulfilled that word: Take to yourself ten tears.* So too Rupert in book 5, chapter 5; Eucherius, Bede, and others. **Verse 32: Moreover, one tribe—Judah, with Benjamin joined to it—shall remain to him**, that is, to Solomon, meaning to Rehoboam, Solomon's son and successor.

The posterity of King David is called a lamp — not just any posterity, but a royal, splendid, and glorious one. For just as the light of the sun, propagating itself, scatters new light through the air, and just as one candle or lamp kindles another, and that one another, and so on in succession; so likewise King David begot King Solomon, and Solomon begot King Rehoboam, and so it continued through the rest of his descendants. The royal succession of David's posterity is therefore here called a lamp — it was, as it were, a lamp, and a certain remnant of light, that is, of the splendor and glory of David, shining forth in Jerusalem. So Cajetan.

That is, a royal family, continuous and stable — namely, that your descendants might succeed one another in unbroken series in the kingdom of the ten Tribes. It is remarkable that Jeroboam and princes like him do not believe the words and promises of God, who is the first and supreme Truth; but instead attempt by contrary means — through impiety, fraud, and crime — to secure for themselves the stability of their kingdom and royal lineage. The result is that they provoke God against themselves, and are therefore utterly overthrown by Him, as happened to Jeroboam and to all the kings of Israel who succeeded him.

That is, on account of this — namely, on account of you — if you shall be faithful to me; meaning: if you shall serve me faithfully, I will exalt you, and I will humble and bring low the descendants of David, your rivals. *Nevertheless, not for all days.* The Rabbis in Seder Olam, chapter 15, refer this to the reign of Asa, king of Judah, who greatly elevated the kingdom of Judah. Salianus and others refer it to Hezekiah, for in the sixth year of his reign the ten Tribes were carried away into Assyria, and thereupon ceased the kingdom and kings of the ten Tribes as rivals of the kings of Judah. Mystically, this was most fully fulfilled in the Messiah — that is, in Christ — who, as the Son of David, reigned again over all Israel and over the whole world, and is worshipped by them as God, Messiah, and King of kings. So Cajetan, Abulensis, Serarius, and others. Hence the disciples asked Christ as He was about to ascend into heaven: *Lord, wilt thou at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?* (Acts 1). Furthermore, God so earnestly impresses upon Jeroboam the true religion and worship of Himself because He foresaw that Jeroboam would make a schism from God and from the Temple for the sake of his kingdom — even though God wished through him to repair the schism from Solomon in the worship of God. But why did God, foreknowing this, nonetheless appoint him king? I answer: in order to punish the people, and to give to a people like unto the impious, an impious king — just as He had previously given them the impious Saul — as Rupert says, and Salianus following him. For the people were already in large part corrupt, drawn to corruption by idleness, luxury, and abundance of all things. Therefore, wishing to chastise an impious people, God gave them Jeroboam as king, who would comply with the lusts and desires of the people to worship idols, and would thus become the cause of so many calamities and ultimately of their final ruin — according to that word of Job, chapter 30: *Who maketh a hypocrite* (that is, an impious man) *to reign because of the sins of the people.* Solomon therefore wished (having understood the matter — namely, that Ahijah had conferred upon Jeroboam the kingdom of the ten Tribes) to kill Jeroboam, as a rival and usurper of his kingdom. The Chaldean on Canticles 8:12 adds that he also wished to kill Ahijah, because it was he who had conferred the kingdom, and that therefore Ahijah likewise fled into Egypt; but Scripture says nothing here of Ahijah — yet this is probable. For kings are sensitive, and pursue even holy men who work to raise up other kings, charging them with treason; for whoever touches the kingdom touches the apple of their eye. But this was, says Dionysius, a great blindness, ambition, and impatience on Solomon's part — as if he wished and were able to impede the purpose of God concerning the division of the kingdom. *In the book of the words* (that is, of the deeds and acts) *of Solomon* — namely, in the journals, chronicles, and annals of Solomon. # GEM

At the age of sixty, say Lyranus, Abulensis, and others. Zonaras and Cedrenus relate that his tomb burst apart and collapsed under the Emperor Hadrian, and that this event portended the Jewish uprisings and tumults. So also Dio, in book 69 on Hadrian, treating of the revolt of the Jews — on account of which nearly all of them were slaughtered by him and all Judea laid waste — says: *This had been announced to them beforehand by the fact that the monument of Solomon, whom they venerate with the highest reverence, had of itself split apart and fallen in ruin.* The figure and appearance of Solomon is gathered from Canticles 5:10 — *My beloved is white and ruddy* — as Pineda collects in book 6, chapter 4, and from him Salianus. Solomon was of fair and ruddy complexion, by nature warm and moist, though somewhat more warm than moist; his hair was golden and moderately curling, like the shoots of palm trees; his eyes of just proportion, filling their orbits, bright and blue-grey, with a calm and pleasing gaze; his cheeks blooming and cheerful, like lilies mingled with roses; his lips firm and soft, like the red lilies of Syria; his very mouth breathing a sweet fragrance; his hands delicate, smooth, articulated, as if polished by the lathe; his fingers long, his arms well-formed; his chest and breast white as a box of ivory, of moderate size and shapely; his legs like columns of marble or of the whitest Parian stone; his feet like bases of finely wrought gold, of moderate size; his gait firm and steadfast; his stature tall and erect; his voice most sweet — worthy indeed to represent Him who is *beautiful above the sons of men.* But most of these gifts the advancing years took from Solomon on both sides; and especially his unbridled lust, and the sicknesses, sorrows, and anguishes sent upon him by God in old age on account of his idolatry. Hence Ecclesiasticus, chapter 47, concludes his praises with this noble and mournful close: *Thou didst bow thy loins unto women, and by thy body thou wast brought into subjection. Thou didst give a stain upon thy glory, and didst profane thy seed, so as to bring wrath upon thy children, and to be grieved for thy folly, that the kingdom was divided in two.* See what is said there. About the twentieth year of the reign of Solomon, Homer was born — the wisest of the poets of the Greeks — whom Virgil, the prince of Latin poets, everywhere imitated and followed in Latin verse. Hence seven Greek cities contend over the birthplace of Homer, namely Smyrna, Rhodes, Colophon, Salamis, Ios, Argos, and Athens. Indeed, that the inhabitants of the island of Ios erected an altar to Homer and sacrificed to him as to a god is attested by Gellius, book 3, chapter 11. Hesiod was a contemporary and near-equal of Homer. It was fitting that under Solomon, the wisest of the Hebrews — indeed of all mortals — wisdom should arise among the Greeks, and thence be transferred to the Latins. So from St. Cyril, book 7 against Julian, and from him Salianus, in the twentieth year of Solomon, which was the 168th year from the destruction of Troy and the 160th before the founding of Rome; at which time also the sixth king Silvius reigned in Latium, son of Aeneas Silvius, and Dido founded Carthage, as Eusebius says in the Chronicle. Finally, Josephus, book 8, chapter 3, sets this epitaph upon Solomon: *Solomon dies, the most fortunate of all kings, the most wealthy and the most wise — excepting the sin into which he was drawn by women in his old age.* For Solomon had already proclaimed to and preached to the whole world: *Vanity of vanities, and all is vanity* — and yet vanity and the mere appearance of women drove him to madness in his old age.

For Shechem was a place celebrated for assemblies and burials, and as it were sacred; hence Joshua held an assembly there (final chapter), as did Abimelech (Judges chapter 9). Here, therefore, the Israelites celebrated the councils of the kingdom at Shechem, so that they might there appoint Rehoboam as their king. "THY FATHER DID LAY A MOST GRIEVOUS YOKE UPON US." For Solomon, in order to sustain the magnificence of himself and his thousand wives in clothing, table, servants, horses, and chariots, had imposed enormous tributes upon the people, from which the people here rightly demanded to be in part relieved. "IF THOU WILT BE A SERVANT UNTO THIS PEOPLE TODAY, AND WILT SERVE THEM, AND SPEAK GOOD WORDS TO THEM, THEN THEY WILL BE THY SERVANTS FOR EVER." This was a most prudent counsel of the elders, because it was most useful both for removing the people's bitterness against Solomon and Rehoboam, and for relieving the weight of that bitterness. For excessive tributes drain the resources, spirit, and blood of subjects, and consequently their very lives; therefore a king deprives himself of both the subjects and their tributes and service when he oppresses them with the same. For the welfare of subjects is the good and advantage of rulers; therefore gentleness and clemency is the chief endowment of princes, by which they win their subjects to themselves, so that they allow themselves to be governed, and readily make themselves available to carry out all the commands of their ruler. Political prudence therefore dictates that you begin your reign with gentleness, and bind your subjects to yourself by gentleness; afterward, when they are already bound to you, you may easily impose upon them the necessary burdens. Well known is that saying of Lucan: "Nothing shames those accustomed to the sceptre; the mildest lot of kingdoms is under a new king." For a long reign has acquired for itself strength and authority, as well as boldness to command whatever it will; but a new reign must gradually acquire these three things for itself by gentleness. Hear Saint Jerome, in his 62nd epistle, celebrating the clemency of a certain bishop toward his subjects: "A father and a bishop," he says, "ought to be loved, not feared. It is an ancient saying: Whom one fears, one hates; whom one hates, one wishes destroyed. Hence even in our own letters, since the beginnings of little ones consist in fear, perfect love casteth out fear. You do not seek to have monks subject to you, and therefore you have them the more subject. You offer a kiss, and they bow their necks; you show yourself a soldier, and you obtain the character of a commander; you are as one in many, that you may be one of many. Liberty quickly takes offense if it is oppressed. No one obtains more from a free man than he who does not compel him to serve. We know the ecclesiastical canons, we are not ignorant of the orders of individuals, we have learned many things by reading and daily examples, we have experienced many things up to this age. He who smites with scorpions, and thinks the fingers of his loins are thicker than his father's back, quickly scatters the kingdom of the meek David. Certainly the Roman people did not tolerate arrogance even in a king.
