That leader of the Israelite army, who had afflicted Egypt with ten plagues, and at whose command heaven and earth and seas were obedient, is proclaimed the most meek of all men whom the earth then brought forth; and therefore for forty years he held the principate, because he tempered the pride of power with gentleness and meekness. He was stoned by the people, and prayed for those who stoned him, because he preferred to be himself blotted out from the book of God, lest the flock committed to him should perish; for he desired to imitate that shepherd whom he knew would carry even the straying sheep upon his own shoulders. 'The good shepherd,' he says, 'giveth his life for his sheep.'" "BUT HE FORSOOK THE COUNSEL OF THE OLD MEN, AND TOOK COUNSEL WITH THE YOUNG MEN THAT HAD GROWN UP WITH HIM." See here the folly and arrogance of Rehoboam, who, having scorned the counsel of the mature elders, follows the youthful and foolish opinion of the young men, and thereby offends his subjects.

It is a proverb, meaning as if to say: I am far stronger and more excellent than my father; I possess greater majesty and magnificence than he possessed; therefore I shall impose upon you greater tributes and burdens than my father Solomon imposed. Hence the Chaldean renders it: "My weakness is stronger than the strength of my father"; the Septuagint: "My little finger is thicker than my father's back"; Vatablus: "My smallest part shall be thicker than my father's loins." See how arrogance makes Rehoboam stupid and foolish. "I WILL CHASTISE YOU WITH SCORPIONS," that is, with barbed scourges, as Vatablus translates. This expression is more worthy of an executioner than of a king. "WHAT PORTION HAVE WE IN DAVID? OR WHAT INHERITANCE IN THE SON OF JESSE?" — meaning as if to say: What communion do we have, what fellowship, what hope and profit in the line of David, that is in Rehoboam, that we should appoint him king over us? As if to say: None — therefore let us not make him our king. In contempt they call David the son of Jesse, meaning: David was a common and poor man, being the son of Jesse our fellow citizen; we exalted him to be king so that he might protect us; but now Rehoboam his grandson, growing insolent on the name of David, does not protect us but tears and plunders us. Why then should we make him king over us, merely because he is of the family of David, when that family was only recently raised to the kingship by us? Let us therefore bring him down together with his family, reduce him to order, restore him to his former state of common life, and from another family choose for ourselves a more moderate and benign king. "NOW SEE TO THINE OWN HOUSE, DAVID." The Chaldean translates: "Now reign over the men of the house of David"; the Septuagint: "Now feed" (that is, rule) "thine own house, David." Meaning as if to say: You, O Rehoboam, son of David, rule your own tribesmen, namely the tribe of Judah; we ten tribes will provide for ourselves another who is more merciful toward us.

— creating Jeroboam as king for themselves in place of Rehoboam. You may ask whether the ten tribes acted lawfully in making a schism and separating themselves from Rehoboam, who was the legitimate heir of the kingdom and the successor of Solomon. The Abulensis replies that they acted lawfully, because, he says, they were free men; but Rehoboam wished to reduce them to servitude; therefore they could withdraw from him. For a people or commonwealth, in creating a king, gives him authority and the right to rule; therefore it can likewise take that authority away from him or diminish it, if he abuses it to the destruction of the people; for the people does not hand itself over to be governed by a ruler absolutely, but under certain conditions, as it were on the understanding that if the ruler does not observe them, the people may depose him. These are the views of the Abulensis, about which more must be said elsewhere. More truly you would say that this was done by the will and decree of God, as is clear from verse 24. Cornelius Tacitus warns the ruler to consider this, in Book 14 of the Annals: "Heavy taxes," he says, "are not to be imposed upon the people; for excessive severity in exacting tribute, and the very excessive tribute imposed, frequently moves subjects to sedition."
Add also Benjamin; for this tribe, being united with Judah, formed with it one single kingdom. "NOW SEE TO—"

Jeroboam feared that the people, going to Jerusalem to the Temple, would return likewise to King Rehoboam reigning in Jerusalem, and would say: "We wish to remain attached to Jerusalem and the Temple, and therefore also to its king Rehoboam." Hence he commanded that no one should go to Jerusalem and the Temple, but that all should sacrifice to the golden calves set up in Dan and Bethel. This counsel of his seemed politically prudent and salutary for maintaining his kingdom and political standing; but in truth it was imprudent and pernicious, and utterly undermined and overturned his standing and his kingdom. For God had threatened this against him — in whose hand lie all the rights of kingdoms — and so it came to pass. For because Jeroboam, in order to maintain the schism of his kingdom, made a schism likewise in religion and in faith, therefore his entire house and family was destroyed from the face of the earth, as is told in chapter 13. For he himself reigned only 22 years, with many sorrows and hardships. His son Nadab succeeded him, who had barely reigned for two years before being deprived of both kingdom and life by his servant Baasha, who immediately exterminated the entire family of Jeroboam so thoroughly that not a single one of them survived — just as God had previously threatened him through the prophet Ahijah. All of this is evident in 3 Kings 15, v. 25 and following. He was therefore as foolish as he was impious, in that he did not believe God, who promised stability of the kingdom in his posterity if he would serve Him alone. For even if his people had gone to Jerusalem to worship God in the Temple, God would nevertheless have kept the people subject and obedient to him, and would have prevented them from returning to Rehoboam — for this He had expressly promised him, and had a thousand means of accomplishing it in His mind and His hand. Such for the most part are the counsels of politicians and Machiavellians — of whom this Jeroboam is the most illustrious example — who make religion serve politics, and embrace such faith and religion as they judge most useful for obtaining, retaining, or enlarging their kingdom, or for other purposes they set before themselves. For even if their counsels seem at first glance attractive and profitable, yet in the course of time they generally entangle princes in the greatest difficulties and bring ruin upon them, so disposing divine Providence, which governs all human counsels. Let them hear Gregory Nazianzen, in his oration in praise of St. Athanasius, Oration 21: "The Emperor Constantius," he says, "grieved over three things at his death: first, that he had inflicted death upon his kinsmen; second, that he had named the apostate Julian as Emperor; third, that he had taken a stand for novel dogmas of faith — and with these words on his lips he departed from life. For a prince, by strengthening religion, is in turn strengthened by it." In their schism the Donatists followed Jeroboam, concerning whom St. Augustine, Epistle 171, says: "The Donatists ordained bishop against bishop, raised altar against altar, and this in the city of Carthage, the capital of Africa, just as Jeroboam raised altar against altar." Finally, that true religion, the worship of God, and piety are the foundation of kingdom and commonwealth is taught by St. Augustine, Book 1 of the City of God and following; by St. Ambrose, On Faith to Gratian; by St. Cyril to the Queens; and indeed also by philosophers. Hear Plato, Book 4 of the Laws: "Before all things let us invoke God, that we may establish our city, and let us beseech Him to hear us, and to be gracious and kind to us, to come to us and teach us the laws Himself, and to adorn our city." And Mercury Trismegistus, cited by Rhodiginus, Book 9, Chapter 19: "The measure of man is before all things religion, which goodness follows; and a household is seen to be perfect only when it has also mastered desire."

Jeroboam made calves rather than rams or horses, because the fathers of the Jews had cast and worshipped a golden calf in the desert, following the likeness of Apis, whom they had worshipped in the form of a calf or ox in Egypt, Exodus 32. See what was said there. Hence the prophets Hosea and the others thunder against these calves. For Jeroboam here uses the same words that his ancestors used in Exodus 32, saying: "These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you out of the land of Egypt." I have said much about these matters in my commentaries on Hosea and the Prophets. Furthermore, Jeroboam, fleeing from Solomon, went to Egypt to King Shishak, and there saw the worship of Apis in the form of a calf — and perhaps he himself, in order to win the favor of Shishak, participated in that worship. Hence when Solomon died and he returned to Judea, making a schism from Judah and Jerusalem, he erected golden calves such as he had seen worshipped in Egypt, so that, relying on Egyptian aid, he might resist Rehoboam and the men of Judah.

— as it were on two boundaries of his kingdom: for Bethel was the border of Israel to the south, and Dan to the north. Moreover, Bethel was a celebrated and sacred place on account of the vision of God displayed to the patriarch Jacob in the ladder, Genesis 32, and from this it was called Bethel, that is, the House of God. But Jeroboam turned Bethel, that is, the House of God, into Beth-aven, that is, the house of iniquity and of the idol, as Hosea says. Bethel is sometimes located in the tribe of Benjamin and sometimes in the tribe of Ephraim; hence many scholars think there were two Bethels, and that the one in Ephraim is meant here, since Jeroboam was king of that region. Others, however, with Adrichomius, hold that there was one and the same Bethel, which is attributed now to Benjamin and now to Ephraim, either because it was situated on the boundary between the two tribes, or because this one portion from Benjamin had adhered to Jeroboam and permitted this idol of the calf to be placed within its territory.

— that is to say: on the occasion of these calves, all Israel under Jeroboam fell into idolatry and adored them as their gods. Therefore Jeroboam sinned most gravely, not only with the sin of idolatry, but also with the sin of scandal; because he was the cause why all Israel thereafter, until its own destruction, worshipped these calves as their idols and gods for 256 years, during which nineteen kings following Jeroboam worshipped his calves.

He established high places on hills, on which idolaters were accustomed to place and adore their idols. AND HE MADE PRIESTS FROM THE LOWEST OF THE PEOPLE — that is to say, not from the tribe of Levi, as God had ordained, but from any class whatsoever he appointed the vilest men as priests of his calves and idols. Thus, by profaning religion, he profaned all other things besides. But this was fitting for the most vile of calves — that they should have the most vile of priests. This was therefore a just judgment of God. So also in this age, heretics have appointed as ministers of their foolish heresy: weavers, cobblers, apostates, and the very dregs of mankind. A fitting lid for such a pot. Even Aristotle, by the pure light of nature and philosophy (Politics, Book 7, Chapter 9), commands that vile men not be appointed as priests, on account of the honor owed to religion and to the divine majesty. Observe, then, one and the same spirit of the devil operating in both cases — a spirit that, being a contemner of sacred things, drags them all down to vileness, that it may mock them and set them forth for all to mock, and thus gradually leads men to atheism, so that they believe nothing and deny the immortality of the soul, hell, and all divinity — as in this age we see many heretics become atheists, as Cardinal Hosius wisely observed and noted. Hear Tertullian, in his book On the Prescription Against Heretics, Chapter 41: *Their ordinations are rash, fickle, and inconsistent; now they place neophytes, now those bound to the world, now apostates of our number, that they may bind them by ambition, since they cannot bind them by truth. Nowhere is advancement more easily obtained than in the camp of rebels, where the mere fact of being there counts as promotion. And so one man is bishop today, another tomorrow; today's deacon is tomorrow's reader; today's presbyter is tomorrow's layman — for they impose priestly functions even upon laymen.*

That is, the Feast of Tabernacles, which the Jews celebrated by God's decree on the fifteenth day of the seventh month; but Jeroboam, out of his own head, transferred it to the sixteenth day of the eighth month, lest he should seem to be following the Jews, but rather that he himself might appear to be the author of a new feast, as well as of a new religion and new gods — though ridiculous ones, namely calves. For what is more stupid than a calf? Nothing certainly, nor is any man other than he who treats irrational calves as though they were rational, and attributes to them reason, providence, and divinity.

# GEM Observe here how Jeroboam, from the single sin of schism, slides into the abyss of crimes. For he arrogates to himself here not merely a kingdom, but also the high priesthood, making himself both pontiff and head of the Church. For, acting as a pontiff, he burns incense, institutes feasts, consecrates priests, and builds shrines. So Josephus. Wherefore one may aptly apply to Jeroboam the words of Synesius, Bishop of Cyrene, Epistle 101 to Pylaeemenes: *By no other means is it possible to truly grow rich in the public sphere, unless you confound all things both divine and human, and transform yourself from a man of good birth into an utter scoundrel.* HERE BEGINS CHAPTER THIRTEEN.

The prophet gave this sign not under Josiah, but at this very moment when Jeroboam stretched out his hand against him, as is said in verse 4. Hear St. Chrysostom, Homily 6 on the words of Isaiah: "Christ," he says, "when He was Himself struck with blows upon His face, did nothing harsh against the servant who had struck Him; but Jeroboam, when he stretched out his hand and attempted to seize the prophet by whom he was rebuked, had his hand withered. By these things He instructs you, that you should bear with meekness those insults which are directed against yourself, but should avenge with great vehemence those which are heaped upon the Lord."

St. Ambrose gives the reason, in Book 2 On Virgins, near the end: "He begged for pardon," he says, "and immediately his hand, which had been withered by sacrilege, was healed by an act of religion. So swift a double example of divine mercy and of divine indignation appeared in one instance: that in the very act of sacrifice his right hand was suddenly taken from him, and by the pardon of repentance it was restored." Furthermore, the Prophet here worked three remarkable miracles: for first, he rent the altar, so that Jeroboam might see it split and broken asunder, as though God held it in abomination; second, the hand he stretched out against the prophet stiffened; third, it was healed through the prayers of the prophet. And yet Jeroboam the arch-heretic did not repent of his heresy and idolatry, but remained in it, hardened and obdurate. Wondrous, therefore, was his blindness, hardness, and malice.

God had commanded this of the prophet out of detestation of idolatry, so that by the very act he might show that the idolatrous people of Bethel were so detestable and so, as it were, excommunicated by God, that He wished the faithful to have no fellowship with them in food or drink, nor even in sharing a road. He therefore commanded him to take a different route on his return, as though the road through Bethel were polluted and accursed on account of its idols, and in no way to be traveled again. So strictly did God wish him to refrain from eating in Bethel, that had he been cast into prison by Jeroboam, he ought rather to have died of hunger than to accept food offered by the people of Bethel. This was a figure of ecclesiastical censure and excommunication, by which the Church forbids communicating with excommunicates and sharing meals with them, so that, excluded from the fellowship of the good, they may be brought to shame and repentance. So say St. Cyprian in Letter 75, Lucifer of Cagliari in his book On Not Associating with Heretics, and following them, Rupert in Book 5, Chapter 6, and Abulensis here, Question 8, arguing that this is not a harm but a benefit to such persons — as indeed it was to Jeroboam himself, of whom Josephus says: "The king, marveling at the man's self-restraint, began to grow all the more anxious about his own affairs, drawing from what he had seen and heard an ill-omened forecast of the outcome of his undertakings."

The Chaldean renders it: a false prophet by the name of Micah, etc. Josephus likewise calls him a pseudo-prophet. So also do St. Gregory, Rupert, Angelomus, Eucherius, Lyra, Hugo, Dionysius, Cajetan, and others. Hence some are of the opinion that he was an idolater and a soothsayer of the devil. Abulensis, however, thinks he was a believer, but a wicked and impious one. Yet the subsequent events refute this view, and argue that he was a believer and a prophet of God, not a pseudo-prophet — because, as Theodoret says, God foretold through him to the man of God what was to come to pass; and because he believed the things that had been prophesied concerning Josiah; and because he charged his sons that when he died they should bury him together with the body of the man of God. All these things show that he was a faithful and pious man. He is therefore called a pseudo-prophet by St. Gregory, Rupert, and the others already cited — that is, a lying prophet — because he lied in saying that it had been revealed to him by an angel that God wished the man of God to eat with him. Theodoret is supported by St. Augustine, to be cited presently, as well as by St. Jerome, Serarius, Tornielus, Salianus, and others.

That prophet lies in order to bring another prophet home to dine with him. For he saw him wearied from the journey, hungry, and unwilling to eat or drink in Bethel, but rather to return fasting; and so, moved by compassion and a zeal for hospitality, he invited the man of God to his house, and by means of a lie, as it were, compelled him to come. In just this way, worldly men sometimes deceive religious persons who abstain from meat and wine, so that they eat flesh hidden and dissolved beneath vegetables. When I was living in Belgium, there was a certain hermit surnamed "Simple" for his guilelessness — one John the Simple — who, coming to a monastery dedicated to the Blessed Virgin, refused wine offered by the steward, saying that he had professed perpetual abstinence from wine. Whereupon the steward, deceiving him, said: "We shall give you, then, the milk of our Blessed Virgin," and ordered that another, sweeter wine be set before him, saying: "Drink the milk offered to you by the Blessed Virgin in place of the wine you refuse." When he had tasted it, he was greatly cheered and said: "Would that in our hermitage we had such milk, since we do not drink wine!" Now the hospitality of this prophet was ill-timed and imprudent, for it brought death upon the other prophet. Yet he himself was ignorant of this, and therefore sinned only venially; his lie was merely an officious one, not a pernicious one. Furthermore, God permitted this so that He might ratify the truth of His prophecy by the death of the disobedient prophet himself, who had eaten food in Bethel against His command — so that from this, Jeroboam might recognize that he had been a true prophet sent by God, and that the threats directed against him would truly come to pass. Note: Josephus, whom the Gloss and Abulensis follow, holds the opinion that this prophet invited the other prophet out of malicious guile, in order to make him appear a liar or to bring about his ruin. Hear the Gloss: *This false prophet was the chief of the priests and was greatly honored by the king as a diviner; fearing, however, that through the words of the man of God the king might withdraw from the worship of idols and put him to death as a sorcerer, he devised a scheme to deceive the man of God, acting with considerable cunning so that, by transgressing God's command, he would incur God's wrath — as indeed happened afterwards, when he believed a man rather than God — and thereby it might be shown that what he had foretold was false, and so he could persuade the king, as indeed he did.* But what I said earlier is far nearer the truth.

# GEM This prophet sinned because he too readily and rashly believed another prophet who lied, claiming that God had revoked the command given to him not to eat in Bethel; for he ought rather to have examined the man and his oracle more carefully, since it contradicted his own. For since God Himself had clearly commanded him in person not to eat in Bethel, he ought not to have believed another prophet claiming that God had revoked this precept. He could and ought to have reasoned thus: God cannot lie; this man can lie and deceive — why then, forsaking my God, should I believe a lying man? Why should I embrace the uncertain and doubtful in place of the certain? For I know with certainty that God absolutely forbade me to eat in Bethel; therefore this man, saying the contrary has been decreed by God, is rightly suspected of fraud and falsehood. For, as Abulensis notes, God foresees and foreknows all things that can arise in relation to His commandments; therefore in His precepts there is no room for interpretation and epieikeia, as there is in the commands of men, who cannot foresee all future things and thus admit of epieikeia. Nevertheless, this sin of his was only venial, because he believed the prophet to be truthful rather than a liar. Hence, although he was slain by a lion for this sin, yet his body, as though that of a saint, was preserved intact and untouched by the lion. So say St. Augustine (cited shortly below), Eucherius, Angelomus, Cajetan, Salianus, Serarius, and others. *AND WHEN THEY SAT DOWN AT TABLE, THE WORD OF THE LORD CAME TO THE PROPHET WHO HAD BROUGHT HIM BACK.*] The prophet who sinned at table through disobedience, eating contrary to the command of the Lord, is punished likewise at table, and receives from God the sentence of death — and this through his very host, namely the prophet who had seduced and invited him — so that he might truly receive punishment from that source from which he had carelessly incurred guilt. So says St. Gregory, and from him Eucherius and Angelomus. *AND WHEN HE HAD DEPARTED, A LION FOUND HIM IN THE WAY AND KILLED HIM* — that is, suffocated him or struck him dead with a bite — yet did not devour him, but preserved the body intact. Thus was the prophet punished by God, so that Jeroboam, from this punishment, might recognize that he had been a true prophet. For among other things he had said that he had been commanded by God not to eat in Bethel, to show how greatly God hated Bethel. Note. Here God punishes a merely venial sin with death, because He Himself is the supreme Lord of life and death over all; it is otherwise with a prince or judge, who must punish sins proportionally according to their gravity — grave sins gravely, light sins lightly. Hence human law cannot punish venial sins with death. So likewise God punished Moses with death in the desert for a venial sin of unbelief (Numbers 20); so the wife of Lot, because she looked back — which was only a venial sin — was struck dead and turned into a pillar of salt (Genesis 19). So say Cassian, *Conferences*, Book 7, Chapter 25, and St. Thomas, *Summa Theologica*, Part III, Q. 8, Art. 2, ad 3. *Moral.* St. Chrysostom, commenting on Psalm 48, says: Just as a dog or any beast that a man has tamed by kindness and made obedient to himself in all things, if a man disguises his face or alters or obscures it with soot, mud, or an unfamiliar color, will nonetheless be barked at and bitten by the domestic dog or torn by the tamed lion as though he were a stranger — so, says Chrysostom, because this prophet had defiled his face with the soot of disobedience, the lion did not recognize him as his master, and therefore killed him as though he were a stranger. Far differently did the lions behave toward Daniel, even though they were famished in the pit, because in him they found such a form and countenance as they were bidden to revere and honor; for he had not defiled or altered his face...

...so that he might bury him honorably and mourn for him according to custom, especially because he himself had been the cause of his death through his lie. From what follows it is evident that this prophet was faithful and devout, and had led the other prophet astray through a misguided hospitality. Therefore Josephus, in Book 8 of the *Antiquities*, Chapter 3, departs far from the account of Holy Scripture and errs greatly, when he says that this false prophet confirmed King Jeroboam — who had been terrified by the threats and miracles of the prophet and wished to repent of his idols — in his idolatry, and disparaged and refuted the miracles of the prophet in the king's presence. For he claimed, according to Josephus, that his hand had grown numb from weariness when he was offering sacrifices at the altar, and that afterwards, following rest, it had recovered its former vigor; and that the altar likewise, being of recent construction and unable to bear so great a weight of sacrificial victims, had collapsed and fallen apart. Finally, he declared that the man's death, announced beforehand, brought about by a lion, showed that there was nothing divine either in his life or his words. Having said these things, he persuaded the king, and turned his mind wholly away from God and from just and pious works, plunging him into the depths of impiety. For from that time forward Jeroboam raged against all right and justice to such a degree that he sought nothing other than how he might daily surpass himself in fresh wickedness. But these things are foreign to and nearly contrary to the account of Holy Scripture in what follows. *AND HE RETURNED AND BROUGHT IT INTO THE CITY OF THE OLD PROPHET*] — that is, into his own city, namely Bethel, as is clear from verse 32 — for it was the old prophet himself who had led him astray by his lie, and who therefore buried and mourned the one slain by the lion.

He gave this command with the purpose that his own bones should not be burned by Josiah; for Josiah dug up all the other tombs, belonging to idolaters, and burned their bones in Bethel — excepting only the tomb of the prophet who had prophesied against Jeroboam here, and who had foretold that Josiah would overthrow his idols and his idolaters, as is clear from 2 Kings, Chapter 23. Therefore the old prophet wished his own bones to be buried in that prophet's tomb, so that his bones too might be preserved intact.

With oil and victims — that is, by having his hands anointed by another priest, and himself consecrated, and by offering with his own hand the victims customarily offered at consecration. Hence what follows: AND BY THIS RITE HE BECAME A PRIEST OF THE HIGH PLACES — that is, of the calves, which on the high mountain of Bethel were set up to be adored. For Jeroboam expelled the Levites and the Aaronic priests, who accordingly, together with the rest of the faithful Israelites who detested the idols of Jeroboam, fled to Jerusalem to Rehoboam and strengthened his kingdom, as is recounted in 2 Chronicles 11, verse 13.

AND FOR THIS CAUSE THE HOUSE OF JEROBOAM SINNED — the great sin of idolatry, because, namely, he erected golden calves and compelled the whole people to adore them — AND WAS THEREFORE OVERTHROWN AND UTTERLY DESTROYED FROM THE FACE OF THE EARTH — as though razed from the earth to its very foundations, so that no one from it should remain alive, as is clear from the following chapter, verse 10. Note here the true cause of the ruin and destruction of families, even royal ones such as that of Jeroboam: it is idolatry, heresy, and the other crimes that follow from it. This is shown throughout this entire book and the fourth book that follows, through the examples of all the kings of Israel who followed Jeroboam both in the idolatry of the calves and in his manner of reigning. Herein their astonishing blindness is evident: that though they heard these threats of God, and saw them in very deed fulfilled in Jeroboam and their other predecessors, they themselves nevertheless, as if bereft of reason, followed their idolatry — and so they likewise, together with their families, were cut off by God. CHAPTER FOURTEEN

...to announce to the son for whom you consult me that he shall die, and that I shall by God's command inflict other calamities far greater: because Jeroboam was hard and refractory toward God; and for a hard knot, a hard wedge must be sought.

That is, every male: for males alone urinate against a wall, says Elias the Tishbite. Or rather the meaning is: I will slay every living thing in the house of Jeroboam, even unto the dog, so that I shall not leave so much as a living dog within it; for dogs alone, with leg lifted, sprinkle the wall with their water. The meaning is: I will spare not even the dogs. So Theodoret, Vatablus, Serarius. *And him that is shut up* — that is, confined in prison, or hidden within the house. *And him that is last* — that is, the last remaining, namely whoever shall have survived to the very end of the war; the meaning being: all the rest shall at length perish. Or *last* means the most vile, the most abject. Scripture employs this turn of phrase when it wishes to signify absolutely all without exception, as if to say: I will destroy all the remnants and dregs of your family. In like manner God slew all the firstborn of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh even to the firstborn of the captive woman who was in prison, Exodus 12:29. *And I will sweep away* (sweep out, scour with a broom, overturn) *the remnants of the house of Jeroboam, as dung* (Chaldean: filth) *is swept away until the place is clean* — so that no dung whatsoever remains, but the whole floor of the house be clean.

...so that it may be plain that death was threatened and sent upon the son by God through me, on account of your idols and those of your husband, and that you may grieve the more bitterly, in that you have been, as it were, the murderers of your own son. Thus the Emperor Valens, when illness was sent upon his son on account of his Arianism, had it driven away through the prayers of St. Basil; but shortly afterward, summoning the Arians to visit the boy, he brought the illness back upon him.

Some think that this son had done something good, for which he deserved burial in the sight of God — for example, that he had permitted the faithful to go up to Jerusalem to worship God in the Temple. So the Rabbis. But this son was a young child, as was said at verse 12. Others more rightly attribute this not to the merits of the son, but to those of some member of the household; for this is what the phrase *in the house of Jeroboam* signifies — as if to say: Some of the family of Jeroboam did certain good things, for which God will reward them with this recompense, that this son of Jeroboam shall be granted an honourable burial. So Vatablus. Moreover, *the Lord has appointed for Himself a king over Israel* (Baasha, the servant of Jeroboam) *who shall strike down the house of Jeroboam this day, and even now* — that is, shortly, after a few days, after a little time. For the phrase *at this time*, the Hebrew has literally: *And what? even now* — meaning: nay rather, from this very moment, from this present instant, I will begin to strike Jeroboam by slaying his little son who lies sick. Otherwise Vatablus reads it: What now? that is, how small and slight is this punishment and tribulation, compared with those being prepared for King Jeroboam in the future, namely in Gehenna? The Chaldean paraphrase likewise renders it differently: *He shall exterminate the house of Jeroboam, him who lives today, and also him who shall be born from henceforth and beyond.*

That is to say: God will smite King Jeroboam and his people Israel continually from every quarter and on all sides, just as a reed is ceaselessly tossed about by every wind, so that no rest shall be granted to them, but they shall be shaken and shattered by the continual incursions and depredations of enemies, until they fall and perish. Furthermore, "He shall scatter them," that is, He shall disperse them to every wind and shall dissipate them throughout the four quarters of the world, beyond the river — namely the Euphrates, which was the boundary of the land promised to the Jews. This was indeed accomplished in fact when, under Hosea, the last king, the ten tribes were carried away by Shalmaneser into Assyria in the sixth year of King Hezekiah's reign, as we shall hear in chapters 16 and 17. "Because they made for themselves groves" — that is, woodlands planted with trees, shady, pleasant, and densely wooded, in which, after the manner of the Gentiles, they worshipped their idols, to which they had dedicated these groves. Therein they served Bacchus and Priapus through feasting, drunkenness, and fornication, as is evident from chapter 15, verse 13. Pliny sets forth other reasons for the groves, in Book 12, chapter 1: "These," he says, "were formerly the temples of the gods, and in the ancient rite even simple rural places were sacred; even now we dedicate a tree of surpassing excellence to a god; nor do we adore images gleaming with gold and ivory more than we adore groves and the very silence within them. The kinds of trees dedicated to their respective deities are kept perpetually: thus the oak to Jupiter, the laurel to Apollo, the olive to Minerva, the myrtle to Venus, the poplar to Hercules. Moreover, we believe that Silvani and Fauns, and the various orders of goddesses, are assigned to the forests, together with their own divine powers, no less than to the heavens." The reasons why God so strictly forbade groves are enumerated by Philo in his book On Monarchy. "First," he says, "because the true temple of God demands not pleasant amenities, but austere purity. Secondly, because it is not lawful to bring there the things that contribute to the greenness of trees — that is, the excrement of brutes and of men. Thirdly, because timber from the forest is of no use, since it is, as the poets say, a useless weight upon the earth; moreover, fruit-bearing trees would draw minds of little wisdom and piety away from the intention and religious devotion owed to sacred things. Furthermore, the dense thickets of forests are convenient for evil-doers who seek safety and occasion for ambush in their hiding places; whereas open places exposed to the eyes, where those who enter can both be seen by others and see others, are most fitting and suited to sacred worship."

Note here four most grievous punishments with which God chastised the public idolatry of Jeroboam: first, He foretells that his young son shall die; second, that Jeroboam himself shall perish along with all his posterity, and this with infamy, so that they shall be deprived of burial and torn apart by dogs and wild beasts; third, that the kingdom shall be transferred from Jeroboam and his family to another king; fourth, that the entire people shall be led away into captivity. Verse 19: "Now the rest of the acts" — that is, the deeds (this is a Metonymy) — "of Jeroboam, etc., are written in the Book of the Acts of the Days of the Kings of Israel" — namely, in the Annals, Chronicles, and Diaries, in which the deeds of the kings and of the people of Israel were recorded day by day. Some hold this book to be the Book of Paralipomenon, but they are mistaken, because this book is cited within the Book of Paralipomenon; therefore it was composed before the Book of Paralipomenon, just as it was composed before the Books of Kings, in which it is cited here and elsewhere. See Abulensis, Proem to the Book of Paralipomenon.

Having finished the deeds of Jeroboam, the first king of Israel, the author passes to the deeds of Rehoboam, the first king of Judah. "That He might place His name there." — Name, that is, the knowledge of His name, faith, worship, adoration, and likewise renown, fame, glory, miracles, oracles, and so forth. For in Jerusalem there were the Temple, the priests, the pontiffs, and the prophets, who instructed the people in the faith and worship of God and advanced them therein. "Now the name of his mother was Naamah an Ammonitess" — namely, the sister or daughter of Shobi, whom David had appointed king of the Ammonites in place of Hanun, who had been conquered in war; and who therefore, in order to bind David more closely to himself, requested that Naamah be given as wife to Solomon, David's son, as I noted on 2 Kings 17:27. David consented to this on the grounds that Naamah professed her willingness to convert to Judaism; but Vatablus and Emmanuel Sa, along with others, suspect that her conversion was feigned and simulated, and that she therefore remained in her heart a Gentile and a worshipper of her own gods; indeed, that she had drawn her son Rehoboam into the worship of those gods — and that this was the cause of the idolatry that took hold in Judah. For it is for this reason that Scripture immediately adds here: "And Judah did evil before the Lord, etc. For they also built for themselves altars, and statues, and groves." # GEM Let princes learn from this how dangerous and displeasing to God it is to take as wives the daughters of unbelievers or heretics, even though they declare their willingness to convert to the faith — for they make such declarations in hope of a kingdom or a principality, and it is difficult for them to sincerely and wholeheartedly root out the heresy they have imbibed from childhood. Hence also David, in taking Maachah the daughter of the king of Geshur, begot by her Absalom. But what manner of man was Absalom? A fratricide and a parricide, inasmuch as he conspired with all Israel for the death of his father. Moreover, Absalom's daughter or granddaughter was Maachah, who received that name from her grandmother — that is, from Absalom's mother — and she became the wife of Rehoboam, and imitating the idolatry and crimes of her ancestress, she herself, or rather her sister or niece, became the princess and priestess of the rites of Priapus; Asa her son therefore deposed her from that office, as we shall hear in the following chapter, verse 13. The propagation of idolatry, as also of heresy (as I demonstrated from St. Jerome on 2 Timothy 3:6), was accomplished through women, inasmuch as they are lovers of novelty and obstinate in their own judgment.

That is, idols, which by antonomasia are called "evil" or "sin," because they are themselves the greatest of crimes. Note that Rehoboam, during the first three years of his reign, persisted with his tribe of Judah in the faith and worship of the true God; but once those years had passed he abandoned it and permitted the various impurities of effeminates — for which reason God sent against him in the fifth year of his reign Shishak king of Egypt, who captured and plundered Jerusalem. All this is clear from what follows, and from 2 Chronicles 11:17 and chapter 12. The cause of the idolatry was in part Rehoboam's mother, namely the aforementioned Naamah; in part his wife Maachah, of whom see verse 21; and in part the proximity of idolaters — for all the surrounding nations, and especially the ten tribes of Israel, worshipped idols, and many of them flourished in peace, wealth, and pleasures, thereby enticing the neighboring Judeans to worship their idols, promising them from their gods a similar abundance of earthly goods. Verse 24: "But there were also effeminates in the land." — That is to say: the idolaters, with Rehoboam's permission, established houses of prostitution — not only of harlots, but also of male prostitutes and catamites, for the purpose of an abominable and execrable lust, such as the impious Simon established, as recorded in 2 Maccabees 4:9. Behold the fruit of idolatry, just as of heresy: it is lust, and lust of the most infamous and horrifying kind. For by this the filthy demons whom they worshipped in their idols wish to be honored — so that they may drive men to every impurity and every crime, to the injury of God their Creator, and to provoke His offense and vengeance.

With sixty thousand horsemen and an innumerable infantry gathered from Egypt, Libya, the Troglodytes, and Ethiopia, as is related in 2 Chronicles 12:3. Shishak, whom Herodotus (as cited by Josephus) calls Sesostris, is called Sesoosis by Diodorus in Book 1, who also adds that columns were erected by him in Palestine upon which female genitalia were inscribed, in order to mark the licentiousness of the inhabitants and, through that effeminacy and sloth, to signify that they had lost their manhood and, timid like women, had not dared to take up arms and resist him. This Shishak appears to have been a kinsman of Rehoboam, for his father Solomon had taken to wife the daughter of Pharaoh, of whose kingdom in Egypt Shishak appears to have been the successor and son.

Shishak plundered Jerusalem, yet did not utterly destroy it — first, because there were found in Jerusalem the good works of penitence, namely of religion, of prayer, of sacrifices, and of almsgiving, by which pious men entreated God to turn away its destruction, as related in 2 Chronicles 12:12; and second, because King Rehoboam, seeing Shishak laying waste Judea, came to his senses together with his people and said: "The Lord is just," as is recorded in 2 Chronicles 12:6, where it is also added: "And when the Lord saw that they had humbled themselves, the word of the Lord came to Shemaiah, saying: Because they have humbled themselves, I will not destroy them, and I will give them some little help, and my wrath shall not be poured out upon Jerusalem by the hand of Shishak. Nevertheless they shall serve him, that they may know the difference between my service and the service of the kingdoms of the earth" — that is, what difference there is between serving God and serving man, namely the king Shishak. For God exalts and elevates His servants; Shishak, however, oppresses and despoils them. Finally, in the same place concerning Rehoboam, verse 14, it is said: "And he did evil, and did not prepare his heart to seek the Lord."

You will ask: At verse 9, Abijah is said to have died in the twentieth year of Jeroboam; therefore he reigned not three years, but only two: for from the eighteenth to the twentieth year, there are but two years. I reply that the eighteenth year must here be counted as the first year of Abijah's reign, the nineteenth as his second, and the twentieth of Jeroboam's reign as his third. So hold Abulensis, Cajetan, Salianus, and others. Note here, in chronology, that a year is sometimes reckoned from its beginning, sometimes from its end; and therefore one year added or subtracted in chronology makes no real difference. Furthermore, Abulensis here assigns three other methods of reconciling divergent chronology, which are very useful and worthy of note. The first is by Synecdoche, whereby a part of a year, month, or day is put for a whole year, month, or day — just as Christ is said to have been three days in the tomb, because He was in the tomb for part of the first day and part of the third, while on the second day alone He remained there in its entirety. The second is that sometimes the years during which a son reigned together with his father are counted, and sometimes only the years during which he reigned alone after his father. The third is that sometimes an interregnum is joined to the years of the succeeding king or judge, as I showed in Judges chapter 3, and sometimes it is not. The same methods are handed down by Richard of Saint Victor in his treatise On the Concordance of the Times of the Kings of Judah. **"The name of his mother was Maacah, the daughter of Abishalom"** — that is, Absalom, as it is said in 2 Chronicles 11:21. For in Hebrew, Abishalom is the same as Absalom. Therefore there is scarcely any reason to doubt that this Absalom is the son of David, of whom frequent mention is made in the second book; for we read of no other Absalom. Absalom is named here because, from the account of his deeds recorded in the second book, he was most well known throughout all Israel. Therefore this Maacah is one and the same as Tamar; for Absalom begot only this one daughter, as is clear from 2 Samuel 14:27. So judge the Hebrews, Josephus, Lyran, Cajetan, Sanchez, and others. Less correctly, therefore, do Abulensis and Salianus hold that this Absalom is a different person from Absalom the son of David. You will object: In 2 Chronicles 13:2, this Maacah is called Michaiah, daughter of Uriel of Gibeah. I reply with Cajetan that Absalom had two names, and was called by another name Uriel, perhaps on account of his beauty — because he was so beautiful that he seemed an angel, and could be called Uriel, that is, "the light and splendor of God." Or certainly Absalom was the natural father of Maacah, and Uriel was her legal father — though this is difficult to conceive, since no son of David is read to have been called Uriel, upon whose behalf, as a brother dying without children, Absalom would have been required to raise up seed. It could be stated more plainly that Maacah is called the daughter — that is, the granddaughter — of Absalom, after the manner of Scripture, in which grandchildren are called sons. For it appears that Tamar, the only daughter of Absalom, married Uriel, and by him bore this Maacah, who became the wife of Rehoboam and the mother of Abijah. This is confirmed by various conjectures. For Uriel was from Gibeah, which was a city in the tribe of Benjamin; he was therefore himself a Benjaminite. Therefore Uriel is not Absalom, for Absalom was of the tribe of Judah, even as David was. Secondly, because Tamar seems to have been too advanced in age at this time to have married Rehoboam. For she was born easily twelve or thirteen years before the death of David — since her father Absalom was slain nine years before David's death, as I noted in the Chronicle of David at the end of the second book of Kings. Add the forty years of Solomon's reign, and you will have fifty-two years of Tamar's life. For indeed Rehoboam appears to have taken her before the death of Solomon, since by her he begot Abijah, who, dying after a reign of three years, left his son Asa as his successor. Now Rehoboam reigned only seventeen years; and so Abijah was begotten by Rehoboam before the beginning of his reign.

— by worshipping the idols of Rehoboam, by permitting effeminates, and so forth. These, along with the idols, were removed by his son Asa, as follows. **"And the Lord his God gave him a lamp"** — that is, a glorious and royal offspring — **"that he might raise up his son after him"** — that is, that Asa his son might succeed him in the kingdom — **"and that he might establish Jerusalem"** — by confirming it in the faith and worship of the true God, and thereby equally establishing its peace, its wealth, and its kingdom.

You will ask: David committed many other sins, as when out of pride he numbered the people, which sin God punished with a plague and the death of seventy thousand men (2 Samuel 24; 15); when he wished to kill Nabal and all his household (1 Samuel 25:21); when he took from the innocent Mephibosheth half his property (2 Samuel 19:29). But these sins were neither so grave as the adultery and the murder of Uriah, nor so certain and censured by the common people; nay, they had some veil and pretext of honesty and justice, at least in the eyes of men, who therefore either excused them or made little account of them. But this sin against Uriah was regarded by all as unjust, monstrous, and infamous; and therefore God, who does not leave such great and scandalous sins unpunished, punished it severely. For on account of this, all evils befell David, and it was said to him by God that the sword should never depart from his house — and that this indeed came to pass in reality is shown here, where it is added that between Rehoboam and Jeroboam, and likewise Abijah, there were perpetual wars, slaughters, and plunderings. So hold Abulensis, Cajetan, and others.

The sense is: God, on account of the merits of David, willed to give him a lamp — that is, a royal posterity, namely Solomon, Rehoboam, and Abijah — but nevertheless, on account of their sins, especially that of idolatry, He brought it about that they had perpetual wars with Jeroboam.

This is recounted at length in 2 Chronicles 13, where it is said that Abijah led out 400,000 men into battle, while Jeroboam had 800,000; and when the latter, being the more numerous, surrounded the former, they called upon God, and by His aid 500,000 fell from the 800,000.

That is, of Absalom, son of David. "Daughter" here means granddaughter, as is evident. For this Maacah was either the mother of Asa, or the same person as Maacah the mother of Abijah, as Lyran and Serarius would have it — so that she is called "mother" in the sense of grandmother of Asa, just as in verse 3 David is called the "father," that is, the grandfather, of Abijah — or more likely she was Absalom's younger sister, born of the same mother Tamar, daughter of Absalom, as I noted from Cajetan at verse 2; or at any rate she was his granddaughter. Maacah in Hebrew signifies compression, crushing, fixion, as Pagninus notes. This name appears to have been common among the queens or daughters of the kings of Geshur; hence the daughter of the king of Geshur whom David married was called Maacah, who was the mother of Absalom, and thence Tamar, daughter of Absalom, named her own daughter — who married Rehoboam — Maacah; and she named her other daughter, who married Abijah, likewise Maacah; for this latter is consistently called the mother of Asa by the Hebrew text, the Septuagint, the Vulgate, and Josephus alike. In like manner, Saint Paula, Melania, Macrina the aunt of Saint Basil, Eudoxia, and Constantia transmitted their names to their daughters and granddaughters. Moreover, the mother or grandmother of Asa is named here in order to display the outstanding virtue and piety of Asa — that though reared by so impious a mother, he nonetheless emerged so devout, and even removed that very mother from the priesthood of idols. Thus Pliny, in Book 7 of his Letters, praises the young Quadratus because, though living amid the indulgences of his grandmother, he nevertheless lived most austerely. # GEM Verse 13: FURTHERMORE HE ALSO REMOVED MAACAH HIS MOTHER, THAT SHE SHOULD NOT BE CHIEF (that is, high priestess) IN THE RITES OF PRIAPUS. The Hebrew reads literally: that she should not be mistress, because she made a horrible idol; where the Septuagint, Chaldee, Vatablus, Pagninus, and others render "that she should not be mistress" as: that she should not be queen, that she should not rule the kingdom. Hence also the Vulgate at 2 Chronicles 15:16 renders it: But also Maacah the mother of King Asa he deposed from her exalted authority, because she had made in the grove an image of Priapus. Here however it renders: that she should not be chief in the rites of Priapus; for among the Jews and idolaters this chieftaincy over the rites of Priapus was an exalted office and the highest dignity. The Hebrew particle "baasher" is to be taken as meaning "because" or "in that," that is, on account of what she did — namely in the worship of her Priapus. For Priapus the Hebrew has mipletzet, meaning terror, horror; thus was Priapus named, both because he was depicted or sculpted in a shameful and horrible form, and because by his fearsome aspect he struck terror into thieves and birds wishing to plunder gardens, of which he was the god and guardian; hence that passage of Horace, Book 1, Satire 8, concerning Priapus: "Therefore a god, the greatest dread of thieves and birds." Hence also, from the Hebrew mipletzet, Priapus is called in Latin Phallus and Mutinus; for so he is styled by Juvenal in Satire 6, and by Virgil and Ovid. So Serarius, Vatablus, and Sanchez. Maacah therefore was obscene and sacrilegious — indeed, the leader of obscenity and sacrilege, the queen of harlots and of prostitutes — and therefore she consecrated a grove to her Priapus; because lust, being a thing of shame, seeks groves, that is, shadowy forests, and darkness, in which to conceal its vileness. Hence "lucus" (grove) is so called by antiphrasis, because it lacks light and is dark. So Eucherius. Furthermore, the Hebrews worshipped Priapus under the name of Baal-Peor, as a god of obscenity, with the most shameless rites and acts. See the commentary on Numbers 25:3. Now Asa did these things at the beginning of his reign, when he was almost a boy; all the greater therefore was his zeal and piety. Wherefore God rewarded this zeal of Asa: first, with long and peaceful tranquility; second, with the building of many cities; third, with an illustrious victory in which he laid low a million Ethiopians, as recounted in 2 Chronicles 14. Verse 14: BUT THE HIGH PLACES HE DID NOT TAKE AWAY. Here Abulensis [comments] on 3 Kings, Chapter 15.

Asa sinned in this matter: first, in that he took and alienated the gold of the Temple; and second, in that he placed his hope in the impious Ben-hadad rather than in God — and therefore he was rebuked by the prophet, as recorded in 2 Chronicles 15:1.

This matter is recounted more fully in 2 Chronicles 16:12. It says: Asa became diseased in the thirty-ninth year of his reign, with an exceedingly grievous pain in his feet (that is, gout), and even in his illness he did not seek the Lord, but placed his trust rather in the skill of physicians — and therefore he died; for immediately thereafter it is added: And he slept with his fathers, and died in the forty-first year of his reign. CHAPTER SIXTEEN
As if to say: You were the disciple of his idolatry, and you compelled the people to worship the golden calves erected as idols by Jeroboam.

As if to say: Just as through you I utterly destroyed the family of Jeroboam on account of the idolatry of the calves, so for the same reason I will root out your offspring through Zimri, who rebelled against your son Elah, as in verse 9. Remarkable was the perversity and folly of the kings of Israel, who, when they saw that by God's command the families of the preceding kings were entirely overthrown on account of the worship of the calves — indeed, when they themselves were overthrowing those very families — nevertheless continued to worship those same calves and to set them before the people for veneration, lest the people should return to the Temple and to Asa, king of Jerusalem; even though the prophets cried out against them and threatened them with destruction. Truly, the devil and the ambition of ruling blinded and maddened them. Hence it came to pass, by the just judgment of God, that they all became each other's executioners: for Baasha was the executioner of the sons of Jeroboam, Zimri was the executioner of the sons of Baasha, and Zimri in turn was the executioner of Omri. So it was among the Romans: Julius Caesar destroyed Pompey; Brutus and Cassius destroyed Julius; Augustus destroyed them. Soon the Senate drove Nero to his death; Otho destroyed Galba, Vitellius destroyed Otho, Vespasian destroyed Vitellius, Domitian destroyed Titus, and Nerva and Trajan destroyed Domitian — as Sextus Aurelius Victor recounts in his Epitome of the Caesars. Here indeed is that true saying of Juvenal: *To the son-in-law of Ceres* (that is, Pluto) *without slaughter and blood, few kings descend, and tyrants with a dry death.* Hence Tacitus said of Galba: "By universal consent capable of empire, had he never been emperor." Of Vespasian he wrote: "He alone among emperors was changed for the better." But on this matter there will be fuller discussion at verse 31, concerning Ahab.
That is, in the Diaries and Annals of the Kings of Israel, as I have said above.
That is, on account of the prophecy by which Jehu threatened the idolater with destruction. This Jehu was therefore truly both a prophet and a martyr, and his prophecy made him a martyr.

Note here the just judgment of God upon faithless and idolatrous kings: for because Elah had rebelled against God, his supreme Lord, therefore by the just vengeance of God his own servant and commander of his forces, Zimri, rebelled against him in turn. Just as Elah had slain Jehu, the prophet of God, so he himself was slain by Zimri at God's nod.

Indeed Zimri, that tyrant who coveted the palace, finding himself surrounded, ascended into the royal residence and cast himself upon the pyre. Sardanapalus, the last king of the Assyrians, did the same: besieged in Nineveh by Arbaces, the prefect of his own army, lest he should fall into his hands, he built a great pyre in the palace, upon which he heaped all his gold and silver and whatever belonged to the royal furnishings. Then, having enclosed his concubines and eunuchs within a chamber he had constructed in the midst of the pyre, he consumed himself and the palace together with all of them in the fire. So report Diodorus, Justin, Strabo, and others everywhere. So likewise the wife of Hasdrubal, when Carthage was captured by Scipio and set ablaze, dragging on her right hand and left her three sons who did not shrink from death, threw herself into the flames of her burning homeland — as Valerius Maximus relates, book 2, chapter 2. So too the Numantines, besieged by the Romans for twenty-one years, when they were failing from famine, preferred to burn themselves and all their possessions rather than surrender to Scipio. See Florus, book 2.
He was slain by the opposing faction of Omri, as Josephus attests. The Septuagint adds that Tibni's brother also was slain, one named Joram.

For although Omri, after slaying Zimri, was acclaimed king by the people in the twenty-seventh year of Asa, as was said at verses 15 and 16, nevertheless he had Tibni as a rival for the kingdom, with whom he struggled for five years, until upon Tibni's death he reigned alone — which came about in the thirty-first year of Asa, as is stated here. So say Abulensis, Richard of Saint Victor, Torniellus, Salianus, and others. Whence explaining this the text adds: HE REIGNED OVER ISRAEL TWELVE YEARS: for he began to reign at the death of Zimri in the twenty-seventh year of Asa, as was said at verses 15 and 16; and he died in the thirty-eighth year of Asa, as is stated at verse 29. Now from the twenty-seventh year of Asa to the thirty-eighth is twelve years inclusive. Observe here how God prolongs the reigns of pious kings, but cuts short those of the impious. For behold, Asa reigned in Judah for forty-one years, during which years eight kings reigned successively in Israel, namely Jeroboam, Nadab, Baasa, Elah, Zimri, Tibni, Omri, and Ahab — all of whose rise and fall (excepting Ahab) Asa witnessed. IN TIRZAH HE REIGNED SIX YEARS. Tirzah before the time of Omri was the metropolis and royal seat of the kings of Israel. Wherefore Omri reigned in it for five years, during which he contended with Tibni for the kingdom; but upon Tibni's death, in the sixth year of his reign, he transferred the royal seat from Tirzah to Samaria, which he built, as follows, and there reigned another six years — that is, in total, both in Tirzah and in Samaria, twelve years, as has been said. The cause of the transfer was that Zimri had burned the palace in Tirzah along with himself, and the city had thereupon been taken by Omri, plundered, and laid waste, as was said at verse 18; and further, that Samaria, by reason of the mountain on which it stood, would be more defensible — whence the city long afterward sustained the assaults of besieging enemies. So Salianus. For these reasons, therefore, Omri transferred his kingdom and royal seat from Tirzah to Samaria. AND HE BOUGHT THE HILL OF SAMARIA FROM SHEMER (which is the name of the man who was the lord of the hill) FOR TWO TALENTS OF SILVER. The Hebrew talent contained three thousand shekels, or fifteen hundred ounces — for the shekel is half an ounce — which amounts to twelve thousand drachmas. A silver drachma is a Roman Julius, or a Spanish Real; wherefore a talent of silver contained three thousand Brabantine or French florins, which make twelve hundred Roman gold pieces; therefore two talents of silver make six thousand Brabantine florins, which amount to two thousand four hundred gold pieces. AND HE CALLED THE NAME OF THE CITY WHICH HE HAD BUILT, AFTER THE NAME OF SHEMER (or Somer) THE LORD OF THE HILL, SAMARIA. Some suppose that Samaria had been built earlier, for the name of Samaria is mentioned previously. After Jeroboam seized the kingdom of Israel on account of Solomon's sin, punished in the person of his son Rehoboam through the transfer of the ten tribes to Jeroboam, he reigned, and his son after him, in Israel. The punishment visited upon the son followed, and he together with all the seed and house of Nabat was wiped out by the new king Baasa. The tyrannical power of Baasa ended in his son Elah, who was slain together with all his seed by his servant Zimri. Zimri reigned only seven days, and being besieged by Omri, was compelled to burn himself in the palace, thus extinguishing the royal line. Omri and Ahab's line was extinguished by Jehu, a servant of King Joram, who was of the lineage of Ahab. But retribution came upon the lineage of Jehu in the fourth generation, according to the word of the Lord; and therefore Zechariah, who had descended from Jehu, was cut down by his servant named Shallum, who reigned in Israel in his place for only one month, himself likewise slain by Menahem son of Gadi at Tirzah, who reigned in his place ten years in Israel; and after him Pekahiah his son for two years only, for he was sla—

Baal, or Bat, Bel, and Belus, is Jupiter Belus — that is, Nimrod. For Nimrod is Belus, who was the father of Ninus, the first king of the Assyrians, by whom Nineveh was founded and named, whose wife was Semiramis. Nimrod, therefore, was called Baal, that is, "lord," and was the author of idolatry around the three hundredth year after the flood, as I noted at Genesis 10:9. From him the other gods and idols of the nations were thereafter derived, or surnamed Baal or Baalim. Hence Abulensis, in his commentary on 2 Kings, chapter 3, question 3, considers this Baal of the Sidonians and Tyrians to have been Hercules; for he was held in great honor among them. Thus Hiram, king of Tyre, in the time of Solomon, erected a statue to him, as Josephus attests in Book 8, chapter 2. From this arose the quinquennial games celebrated at Tyre in honor of Hercules, as is evident from 2 Maccabees 4:18. Therefore, to the idols and calves of Jeroboam, Ahab added the idol of Baal, or Jupiter Belus, which Jezebel his wife worshipped after the custom of her Sidonian people. Tropologically, learn from this how hateful to God, and therefore how wretched, is impiety — namely, that the life of impious princes is a continuous and most dismal tragedy. From what has been said in chapters 12, 15, and 16, and in Book 14, chapters 9, 10, 11, and 15, receive this tragic lesson: threats hang over them, dangers loom, spears fly from every side, their very guardians are feared, their very companions dreaded; food brings no pleasure, travel brings no dignity, wars no sound judgment, arms no zeal. Add to this that every age in power is found fault with: if a man is old, he seems unfit; if young, madness is imputed to him. For in desiring me as emperor, you drag me into the necessity of death; yet I have this one consolation in death — I alone shall not be able to perish. There comes to mind what Aelian wittily records from the writings of Aesop concerning tyrants: that if anyone seizes a pig, it cries out — and not without reason. For since it bears neither wool nor anything else of that kind, it immediately surmises by instinct that death is at hand, knowing itself to be taken for the use and eating of those who seize it. Tyrants resemble such swine, who are always beset by suspicions and fear, knowing, as swine do, that their life alone is owed to everyone. So Aelian, Book 9 of the Varia Historia, chapter 34. IN HIS DAYS HIEL OF BETHEL BUILT JERICHO; HE LAID ITS FOUNDATION IN ABIRAM HIS FIRSTBORN, AND SET UP ITS GATES IN SEGUB HIS YOUNGEST SON, ACCORDING TO THE WORD OF THE LORD WHICH HE SPOKE BY THE HAND OF JOSHUA.] When Joshua overthrew Jericho he declared it anathema and cursed the man who would wish to rebuild it, saying in chapter 6, verse 26: "Cursed before the Lord be the man who rises up and rebuilds this city of Jericho; at the cost of his firstborn shall he lay its foundation, and at the cost of his youngest son shall he set up its gates." For this reason no one until the time of Ahab had dared to rebuild Jericho; but at that time Hiel ventured to do so, enticed by the fertility of the place. For Jericho was exceedingly fruitful and pleasant, and produced balsam in its region alone. Scripture adds this in order to show how great was the forgetfulness of God and of the prophets under the wicked idolater Ahab, and how thoroughly morals were corrupted by greed — for it was greed that spurred Hiel to rebuild Jericho against the command of Joshua. It is added further so that his punishment might strike terror into Ahab and the idolaters. For if Hiel, for having rebuilt Jericho against the command of Joshua, was punished with the loss of all his sons, then Ahab, who to the dishonor of God wished Baal to be worshipped in God's place, ought all the more to have reflected that he would be punished by God with the loss of all his sons. IN ABIRAM HIS FIRSTBORN HE LAID ITS FOUNDATION, AND IN SEGUB HIS YOUNGEST SON HE SET UP ITS GATES.] That is to say, Hiel, in laying the foundations of Jericho, immediately lost his firstborn son, by name Abiram; then, continuing to build it, he lost the rest in order, one by one, down to the last, Segub.

One asks first why God raised up Elijah and sent him to Ahab. The answer is: to restrain and extinguish the ardor of both Ahab and Jezebel in promoting idolatry, and to preserve Israel in the true faith and religion of the one God. For this reason He armed Elijah with a remarkable zeal and spirit of fortitude, so that he alone might stand against tyrants, priests, and all idolaters. Thus, when Pharaoh was oppressing the faithful Hebrews, God raised up Moses to oppose him and lead the people out of servitude. Thus He set Ahijah the prophet against Jeroboam when he erected calves as gods, Isaiah against Manasseh, the Maccabees against Antiochus. Thus against an almost entirely Arian world He set one man, Athanasius, as the Atlas of Orthodox faith; against the Pelagians, Saint Augustine; against the Nestorians, Saint Cyril; against the Iconoclasts, Saint John Damascene; against the Albigensians, Saint Dominic; against Lutherans and Calvinists, Saint Ignatius and others like him. Elijah was therefore — as Saint Bernard writes to Pope Eugenius at the end of Book Four of his work *On Consideration* — a form of justice, a mirror of sanctity, a pattern of piety, an assertor of truth, a defender of the faith, a teacher of Israel, a master of the unlearned, a refuge of the oppressed, an advocate of the poor, a judge of widows, an eye of the blind, a tongue of the mute, an avenger of crimes, a terror to the wicked, a glory to the good, a rod against the powerful, a hammer of tyrants, a father of kings, the salt of the earth, a light of the world, a prophet of the Most High, a forerunner of Christ, the anointed of the Lord, the God of Ahab, the terror of the Baalites, a thunderbolt against idolaters. One asks secondly what the name Elijah signifies. The answer is: Elijah in Hebrew is written *Eliahu*, that is, "God of the Lord," or more precisely "my God is the Lord," as Pagninus says, or as if to say *El-ja-hu*, meaning "God himself is mighty." For *El* is the name of God, who is strong and mighty to overcome all things; *ja* is an abbreviation of Jehovah; *hu* means "himself." For Elijah's office was to show Ahab and Israel that not Baal but the God of Israel was the true God, strong and omnipotent Lord of all. So say Saint Jerome, Angelomus, and others. Hence the Hebrews consider that the name of Elijah was taken from that acclamation of the people to Elijah: *The Lord himself is God, the Lord himself is God* (Chapter 18, verse 39); for they hold, in the Great Genesis, chapter 27, that he was previously called Jaberscyth. Hence Saint Jerome on Micah, chapter 5, says: *Elijah means the same as Lord God.* Hear also Saint Isidore, Book 7 of the *Etymologies*, chapter 8: *Elijah is interpreted as Lord God. He is so called from a presage of the future.* For when he was contending in priesthood against four hundred priests of Baal, fire came down from heaven upon the holocaust at the invocation of the name of the Lord. When all the people saw this, they fell upon their faces and said: *The Lord himself is God.* For this reason he had previously received such a name, because through him the people afterward came to know the Lord God. It is also interpreted as "the strong Lord," either because he slew those same priests, or because he endured the hostility of Ahab. Again, Elijah means "the strong one of the Lord," says Saint Jerome, or "God of the Lord," says Pagninus, because just as Moses was constituted by the Lord as God to Pharaoh (Exodus 7:1), so too was Elijah by the same Lord constituted as God to Ahab and Jezebel: for just as Moses, by inflicting ten celestial and terrestrial plagues upon Pharaoh, compelled him to release the Hebrews, so also Elijah set all the elements in motion through stupendous miracles, as if he were their God and Lord, by which he struck — indeed slew — Ahab and the Baalites. Another tropological reason is given by Saint Ambrose, Book 1, *On Cain*, chapter 2, where he adorns Elijah with these titles of praise: *A conqueror of the passions*, he writes, *captured by no allurements, who trampled underfoot all this worldly prosperity...*

For King Ahab would seek him out as the author of the drought, and if he should find him, he would kill him. Flee therefore and hide, lest the cause of the dryness be turned back upon your own head. The brook Cherith descends from the mountains of Ephraim near the city of Phaselu, where on account of this there were many caverns and caves in which Elijah could conceal himself. There therefore he lay hidden for the three years of the drought, fed by ravens. In memory of this, Saint Helena built a church on that same spot, as Nicephorus attests, Book 8, chapter 30. *Cherith* in Hebrew means the same as *cutting* or *felling*, because there Elijah cut down the shoots and crops of the earth through the aridity that he had brought upon it. Hence Saint Chrysostom, in his homily on Peter and Elijah, says that Elijah brought upon the earth a fiery burning and as it were a fever: *Scarcely*, he says, *had his word gone forth, when the air was suddenly changed, the sky became as bronze, and that same word settled upon the bowels of the earth like a fever. And at once all things withered; all things bristled with desolation and waste; the grasses dried up; together with them the trees, both fruit-bearing and barren.* Menander, a Gentile historian, makes mention of this drought and famine in Josephus, Book 8, chapter 7, but mutilated and incompletely, as is the manner of Gentiles when they speak of Jewish matters. For he says this famine lasted only one year, whereas Saint James, chapter 5, verse 17, asserts that it lasted three years and six months. This famine appears to have been only in Israel; for Elijah was punishing Israel's idolatry. Therefore what is said in Luke 4:25, that it was *throughout all the land*, is to be understood suitably, that is, throughout all the land subject to King Ahab and contaminated by the sins of his idolatry. *And I have commanded the ravens.* That is, I ordained by way of commanding, and implanted in them this instinct, to bring you bread. So Cajetan.

# GEM The raven is a voracious, inhuman, and malignant bird; God therefore used such a bird to feed Elijah, in order to show that He commands all animals, can change and convert their very nature to whatever He wills, and that at God's command all creatures serve and obey the saints, such as Elijah was. Thus ravens daily brought half a loaf to Saint Paul the First Hermit, and a whole loaf when Saint Anthony arrived. Whereupon Saint Paul, turning to him, said: *See, the Lord who is truly pious, truly merciful, has sent us our dinner. For sixty years I have received each day the fragment of half a loaf; now at your coming Christ has doubled the ration for his soldiers.* So Saint Jerome in the Life of Saint Paul. Saint Benedict, however, commanded a raven — which was accustomed to come to him at the hour of dinner and take food from his hand — to take away a loaf poisoned by the envious Florentius and submitted to him, and carry it to a place where it could be found by no one; and the obedient raven performed this very thing, as Saint Gregory relates, Book 2 of the *Dialogues*, chapter 8. The progeny of this raven survives even to the present day, and is continually kept by the Benedictines on Monte Cassino. Moreover, the literal reason why God used ravens rather than other birds was that ravens love solitary places and especially torrents, since they are of a most dry and warm nature. Hence it is said in Proverbs 30:17: *The eye that mocks his father, etc., let the ravens of the torrent pluck it out.* Therefore God used ravens, as the birds most readily at hand, to feed Elijah at the brook Cherith, so that from the raven he might receive food, and from the torrent drink, amid such universal dryness. For water suffices for drink and is the most natural drink of all, which all the ancients used before the time of Noah. Tropologically, behold here and revere the wondrous providence of God toward His faithful: for the ravens, prompted by God, twice daily brought bread and flesh to Elijah — namely in the morning for his midday meal, and in the evening for his supper. But angels prepared these loaves and pieces of flesh, and therefore they were better and more savory than ours, which are prepared by bakers and cooks, according to that word of Psalm 77: *Man ate the bread of angels*. Then the angels gave them to the ravens to be carried to Elijah, restrained them lest they should touch the food, and impelled them to bring it untouched to Elijah. For as we read in the Life of Saint Onuphrius, God and the angels have a singular care for hermits, such as Elijah was, because they themselves have forsaken all things and are forsaken by all, according to that word: *To you is the poor man left, and you will be a helper to the orphan* (Psalm 9:14). Wherefore Saint Basil, Homily 8 on the Rich and Avaricious, says: *Carmel, that lofty and desert mountain, held Elijah, for whom hope in God was his provision and food; and though he lived thus, he was not destroyed by famine, but the most rapacious of birds, the ravens, brought him food; and those who were accustomed to seize the food of others became servants to a most just man. Forgetting their very nature, they obeyed the Lord's command in bringing him bread and flesh.* *And flesh.* God therefore sent to Elijah not only loaves necessary for life, but also flesh as a delicacy for his sustenance. Abulensis asks what Elijah ate on the days of fasting, on which it is not permitted to eat flesh; and he answers that he ate whatever the ravens brought, for God, by sending flesh to Elijah, thereby dispensed with him regarding the fast, and willed that he should eat it and enjoy so liberal a gift, lest He should seem to have sent it in vain. Allegorically, Saint Prosper, Book 2, *On Predestination and Promise*, chapter 28, says: *The bread of Elijah is the body of Christ, which the Jews as ravens ministered to the Gentiles, offering to the Gentiles the flesh cooked upon the wood of the cross.*

This city was situated between Tyre and Sidon on the public road, not far from the seashore, and from the river Eleutherus, two miles distant from Sidon. It was called Zarephath, that is, *a smelting place*, from the Hebrew *tsaraph*, meaning *to smelt*, because in it there were workshops for the smelting of vessels of bronze, iron, and other metals. God therefore commands Elijah to depart beyond the borders of Israel and the dominion of his persecutor Ahab, into the territory of the Sidonians, so that he might dwell there in quiet and safety under a Gentile king. Wondrous was the faith of this widow. Here allegorically Elijah signifies Christ, who, suffering persecution from the Jews, transferred His Gospel to the Gentiles, as Christ Himself explains in Luke 4:25. Hear the Author of the *Wonders of Sacred Scripture*, Book 2, chapter 18: *For this reason the prophet is sent to Zarephath of the Sidonians to be sustained, so that through him the good and faithful widow might be fed. And let no one be troubled by the fact that the land of the Sidonians had suffered the same plague equally with Israel, since Jezebel, the persecutress of the prophets and the cause of all vengeance and wickedness, drew her ancestral origin as daughter of the king of the Sidonians.* *For I have commanded a woman there.* *I have commanded* — that is, I have ordained, disposed, provided: for God had not properly speaking commanded the widow, since He had not spoken to her; indeed the widow, when Elijah asked her for a morsel of bread, denied that she had it (verse 12). If anyone, however, should contend that God revealed to the widow the coming of Elijah, and commanded her to receive and feed him in the manner that Elijah would request, I shall not object. This woman was a Gentile, being a Sidonian, but she was religious and devout, say Saint Chrysostom, Homily 3 on Elijah, and Abulensis, question 24.

That is to say: After we have eaten this little bit of flour, we shall die of hunger, for we have nothing else to eat. Allegorically, St. Augustine, Sermon 101 on the Seasons, notes that the two sticks signify the Cross of Christ; for in it there are two pieces of wood, namely one upright and the other crosswise. Therefore, says Augustine, she was gathering two sticks because, in the type of Elijah, she was receiving Christ. She wished to gather two sticks because she desired to know the mystery of the cross. For the cross of the Lord and Saviour was fitted together from two pieces of wood; therefore that widow was gathering two sticks, because the Church was going to believe in Him who hung upon two pieces of wood. The widow therefore said: I am gathering two sticks that I may make food for myself and my son, and we shall eat, and die. It is true, beloved brethren, that no one will be worthy to believe in the crucified Christ unless he dies to this world; for whosoever wishes to eat the body of Christ worthily must needs die to things past and live for things to come.
