"And for my son": so also the Chaldaic renders it, for this is the Hebrew לִבְנִי, Libni. But the Septuagint, reading with different pointing as lebeinal, translates it as "for my sons," thus indicating that she had several sons for whom she took the bread to give to Elijah. Her virtue was all the greater on this account. So Eucherius.

Which bread, namely, is baked quickly and in haste under the ashes, so that it might immediately relieve the present and pressing hunger of Elijah. Thus Abraham, making haste for the Angels, baked ash-cakes, Genesis 18:6. Mystically, Eucherius says: "Bread baked under the ashes is the satisfaction of penitents, according to that word of Psalm 101: For I did eat ashes like bread, and mingled my drink with weeping; for with these acts of satisfaction the Lord encourages Himself to be fed." "Make for me first."] St. Prosper gives the reason, Part 2, On Predictions, ch. 29: "That mercy," he says, "may first occupy its place, which Scripture testifies goes before the face of God; for from Him comes all abundance." Thus St. Louis, King of France, before he himself dined, would give dinner to a hundred poor men.

# GEM Obedience, generosity—that she should give from the little flour necessary for her own life to Elijah, who had brought on this very famine. Wherefore Eucherius exclaims: "O the magnanimous spirit of this woman! O the unwavering resolve of her mind! O the deed venerable through all ages! He asks for a drink, she straightway offers it; and what kings by then perhaps lacked, what the rich did not possess, this widow poured out from her abundance. She feeds with bread him with whom she herself, together with her son, was on the morrow to die, nor does she deny that she has it; but confesses it simply, neither fearing to reveal the truth, and not so much to one who requests as to one who in some manner demands, she lays open before him the whole matter: the quantity of her food and the number of persons, as though she wished to have as her guest not a judge but an assessor." And further: "There was at that time a spectacle most pleasing to angels and men alike, that among the Gentiles in a profane land a widowed woman should then be far more hospitable than Abraham himself, the father of the faith, far more humane than the progenitor of the faith. Abraham was indeed given to hospitality and held with great affection toward strangers; but he was truly wealthy, he was rich." And a little later: "The zeal of humanity scorned all the force of natural affection; she was not solicitous for herself, not for her little ones; nothing called her back from the resolve of her mind—neither the weakness of her own sex, nor a mother's compassionate bowels of piety toward her little children; she slew within herself the duties of nature for the sake of hospitality, and the heart of a mother devoted to her guest became the cruel tomb of her sons."

# GEM Not by rarefaction, but by the continual addition of new flour and oil, says Abulensis. Hence from that time forward the widow was sustained with her son, and indeed Elijah himself also, as is plain from verse 20. This was merited by the faith and holiness of Elijah, and likewise by the obedience and almsgiving of the widow, who had given to Elijah the little flour she possessed. So St. Prosper, book 2, On Predictions and Promises, ch. 29, whom hear: "Thus the soul lends to God; thus, while she gives in her necessity, she provides for her own salvation. Thus is that soul replenished who, when the Lord withdraws bodily, loving Him who chastises, preserves the faith of the one marriage-bed with chaste love, fortified by the sacrament of the flour and the anointing of oil, securely awaiting the welcome rain, when the Lord shall have said to her: Well done, thou good servant, because thou hast been faithful in a little, enter into the joy of thy Lord." Hear also Eucherius admiring the generosity and virtue of this widow, and rewarding her with this eulogy: "The hand of the widow became therefore a perennial wine-press and a mill continuously flowing. Why do I speak of the hand of a woman? In the word of the prophet, the whole house of the widow became the storehouse of the righteous. No dew there, no rain, no breath of spring, no cunning suns, no necessary shower, no plough, no farmer, no husbandman—but in all things and through all things the word of the prophet ministered abundantly to the widow. Elijah, himself fed by the widow, fed her in return." Hear also St. Jerome, Epistle 10: "Let us recall the widow of Zarephath, who preferred the hunger of Elijah to the welfare both of herself and of her sons; so that, on the very night she was to die with her son, she might leave her guest as the survivor, preferring to lose her life rather than her almsgiving; and in a handful of flour she prepared for herself the seedbed of a Dominical harvest. Flour is sown, and a vessel of oil is born: in Judaea there is a scarcity of wheat, for the grain of wheat had died there, and in the widow of the Gentiles the streams of oil flowed." In like manner, St. Euthymius—whose holiness became renowned throughout the whole world under the Emperor Justinian—when the flour in the monastery ran out, multiplied it, saying: "Be of good heart; for as much as we distribute to the needy, the Lord will bestow double upon us. For I hope that, as once the jar of flour did not fail, so now it shall not in the least fail." So Eustachius in his Life, and from him Baronius, in the year of Christ 564. By like reasoning, Maximus the Anchorite, in the manner of Elijah, multiplied grain and oil in two jars for the poor, as Theodoret relates in the Religious History, ch. 13, whom hear: "He used to care for guests and the poor with so cheerful a spirit that he threw open the gates to all who came. For he is said to have had two jars: one indeed of grain, the other of oil. From these he always supplied all who were in need; and he always had them full by the blessing which had been given to the widow of Zarephath and had been poured into these jars. For the Lord Himself pours out all His riches upon all those who call upon Him; and just as He commanded her jar and her drop of oil to gush forth, providing handfuls of seeds of hospitality, so also He furnished to this admirable man a supply that answered equally to the cheerfulness of his spirit." Similar things St. John the Almsgiver, Archbishop of Alexandria, frequently performed, as Leontius attests in his Life. Hence his axiom was: "The more I expend upon the poor, the more and greater things I always receive from God." Wherefore St. Basil, in his homily on Almsgiving, compares him to a well or a spring, which the more water it pours forth, the more abundantly it admits and receives of water welling up. See St. Chrysostom's homily entitled: That almsgiving is the most profitable of all arts. Allegorically, the jar of flour signified the Bread of the Eucharist, which—

Consider the widow already mentioned; for after the death of her husband she was sole mistress of the household. God permitted, or sent, this illness upon the boy for this purpose: that, having died, he might be raised up by Elijah, and thus Elijah's faith and power might shine forth before Ahab and the worshippers of Baal. So say Eucherius and others. Hear Saint Augustine, in his letter *To Simplicianus*, Question 5: *He did not cause the death of her son in order to do evil, but to display a miracle for the glory of His name, whereby He might commend so great a prophet both to those then living and to posterity. Just as the Lord says that Lazarus had not died unto death, but that God might be glorified in the Son; and therefore the consequences prove it, as does the very confidence with which Elijah believed that this had not befallen him so that the widow who had shown him hospitality should be afflicted with bitter grief, but rather that it had been done so that God might show the widow more magnificently what manner of servant of God she had received.* Some hold that this son of the widow, raised by Elijah, was the prophet Jonah. Saint Jerome, in his preface to Jonah, says: *The Hebrews report that he was the son of the widow of Zarephath, whom the prophet Elijah raised from the dead, his mother afterward saying to him: Now I know that thou art a man of God, and the word of the Lord in thy mouth is truth; and for this reason the boy himself was so named. For "Amathi" in our language means "truth," and from the fact that Elijah spoke truly, he who was raised is said to be the son of truth.* So Jerome himself — reciting the tradition, however, not endorsing it. For Jonah was a Hebrew, whereas this boy was the son of a Sidonian woman. See what was said in the Prologue to Jonah. **"That thou mightest call my iniquities to remembrance, and slay my son"** — that is, as if she said: Hast thou come to me, O Elijah, as a minister of Elohim — that is, of God the supreme judge and avenger — so that at thy entering in I might be punished by the death of my son? For she saw that Elijah, as it were a minister of God, was punishing crimes and sinners, as when he had brought sterility and famine upon all Israel for the worship of Baal. For there is a kind of natural antipathy between holiness and iniquity, and between the holy and the wicked, such that whichever prevails excludes and, as it were, destroys the other. Thus Elijah's stern holiness and holy sternness seemed here to strike down every iniquity in the widow's household, and in punishment thereof to slay the widow's son. Theodoret notes the humility and spirit of penitence in this widow, in that she ascribes the death of her son to her own sins; and therefore she merited that he be restored to life. Hear Theodoret here, in Question 62: *The words of the widow are worthy of admiration. She says: By thy light my sins, which were hidden, have been made manifest. She did not say: Thou hast been an ill omen to me; thy coming has brought evil upon me; but rather she attributed to her own sins what had happened. So greatly had the teaching of the prophet profited her — as if, in her humility, she grieved that she had received the prophet into her house unworthily.*

In Hebrew, *ויתמודד — vayitmoded* — that is, "he measured himself out," for it is of the Hitpa'el conjugation, which denotes the reflexive action of the agent upon himself; he measured himself along the boy and made himself equal to him, that is, applying his mouth to the boy's mouth, his hands to his hands, his chest to his chest, his shins to his shins, his feet to his feet, and equalizing himself to him as much as could be done by contraction. Hence the Chaldee renders it: *He pressed himself upon the boy*; the Septuagint: *He breathed upon the boy three times*; Vatablus: *He lay upon the boy*. One may ask: Why did Elijah do this? There are various reasons. The first is ethical and pathetic: to show his extraordinary love for the widow and her son, and hence his grief at the boy's death. For those whom we love most deeply, we embrace and clasp tightly, and we do not permit ourselves to be torn from them, as though we wished to breathe into them our own strength, spirit, and life. So Saint Ambrose, in his funeral oration on the death of his brother Satyrus, recalls having done the same to him: *Nothing profited me,* he says, *that I drew in his last breaths, nothing that I breathed my own breath into him as he lay dying.* The second cause is physical. Abulensis says that by this lying upon the boy and breathing into him, Elijah wished to warm the body, grown cold in death, and thus dispose it to receive the soul and life — just as a mother's body disposes the embryo in the womb, so that God, once it is warmed and prepared, may infuse the rational soul; for this infusion is the work of God alone, while it is the mother's work to prepare the matter. So a hen, by lying upon eggs and warming them, prepares them to be formed and quickened into chicks. The third cause is symbolic: to show how arduous and difficult a thing it is to raise the dead from death to life. Hence Elisha did the same, imitating his master Elijah, as in 2 Kings 4:34–35. Again, so that it might be evident how great is the distance between the miracles of Elijah and those of Christ; for Elijah raised the dead by laborious exertion and toil, whereas Christ did so most easily by a single command and word, as being the Lord of death and life. The fourth cause is theological: that by the touch of his holy body he might commend the boy's corpse to God, as Serarius says. For the bodies of the saints are not only living but life-giving; so much so that the body of Elisha, already dead, once raised a dead man — one slain by robbers and cast into Elisha's tomb — who, upon touching his sacred bones, revived by divine power, as narrated in 2 Kings 13:20–21. Another and more proximate cause is given by Abulensis: Elijah lay upon the boy three times. The first time he lay upon him, praying God to raise him; but seeing this did not happen, a second time he lay upon the boy and prayed more fervently. Yet when the boy still did not rise, a third time he lay upon him and prayed with the most ardent intensity, and thus at last obtained life for the boy. Elisha did likewise. It is fitting that we imitate and do the same. For in difficult matters, God wills to be asked more often and more fervently, so that we may, as it were, wrest from Him what we seek by prayers burning and armed and inflamed with zeal. For such is what the matter deserves, and thus our devotion, virtue, and merit increase. And this is the fruit of perseverance and of persevering prayer, ever pressing on and growing more ardent. The fifth cause is mathematical: for in order that a larger body be made equal to a smaller, the larger must embrace the smaller, and conversely the smaller must subject itself to the larger. This is evident in a smaller circle enclosed within a larger one; for the smaller submits itself to the larger, which embraces it and proportionally equals it to itself, since both circles share the same center and revolve about it, so that no diametric line can be drawn from the larger circle to the center without first cutting through and passing through the intermediate smaller circle. Hence no part of the larger circle can be assigned to which a corresponding part of the smaller does not also correspond. # GEM

For God was accustomed to appear in fire, and by fire sent from heaven to consume the sacrifices offered to Him, as He did with the sacrifice of Abel in Genesis 4, of Aaron in Leviticus 9, of Solomon in 2 Chronicles 7, of David in 1 Chronicles 21, of Gideon in Judges 6:21, of Manoah in Judges 13:20, and of others. For fire represents the majesty of God. Hence in Deuteronomy 4:24, Moses says to the Hebrews: *The Lord thy God is a consuming fire.* For fire is a symbol of chastity and purity, as well as of divine efficacy, as I have shown through seventeen analogies in my commentary on Leviticus 9:23. The Rabbis relate — or rather, as Lyra and Abulensis testify, they fabricate according to their custom — that this ox fled from the worshippers of Baal to Elijah, as though disdaining to be sacrificed to Baal and wishing rather to be offered by Elijah to the true God, so that by this prodigy the people might be turned to recognize that Baal is not God, and might return to the true God whom Elijah served. *They leaped about the altar which they had made.* So also the Septuagint; but the Hebrew and Chaldaic have: "which he had made," namely Elijah, as Serarius and Sanchez hold, who think that the Baalites here insulted the altar of Elijah, and overthrew it by their dancing — which is why shortly after, at verse 30, Elijah is said to have restored it. The Chaldaic favors this reading, rendering: "They were raging over the altar which he had made," namely Elijah. But verse 30 rather indicates the contrary, namely that Elijah had not yet erected his altar. Wherefore Vatablus, reading the Hebrew "which he had made," supplies by inference "one of them," that is, one of the Baalites, or their leader, King Ahab. The Baalites therefore leaped about in disordered frenzy — as though in fear, according to the Chaldaic rendering — when they saw that Baal, though invoked by them, gave no answer; partly from shame and anxiety lest they be punished a thousandfold by Elijah, as indeed shortly came to pass; and partly in order to appease Baal by this anxious leaping, to stir him and as it were compel him to answer. For by this gesture they signified their zeal and devotion toward Baal and his altar, as though they themselves desired to become victims and to immolate themselves to Baal upon the altar — and accordingly, cutting themselves with lances, they drenched the altar with their own blood, as follows. Similar frenzied leaps and disordered gestures in the manner of Bacchantes were performed by the Corybantes, the Luperci, and the Salii among the Romans. So Salianus, Vatablus, and others.

Aptly so; as if to say: Just as Jacob, wrestling with God (that is, with the Angel acting as God's vicegerent) and prevailing over him as he wished to depart, was called Israel, that is, one who prevails with God (Genesis 32); so likewise you, if you shall have imitated the faith and worship of the true God of your Patriarch Jacob, shall become true Israelites, that is, those who prevail with God, and shall obtain all things whatsoever you shall ask of Him. And that you may do this, behold — Israel shall be his name, that is, God shall prevail in this my sacrifice, through fire sent from heaven upon it, and shall cast down the worshippers of Baal. That is, as it were through two furrows, or through two spaces which could be ploughed. Thus in the Hebrew it is signified that the water-channel was so great as to hold two measures. Now a modius, when it is sown, fills an entire field. Furthermore, the Hebrews call a house any sack or vessel whatsoever that contains seed or some other thing. Elijah did this, and poured so great a quantity of water around the altar, lest anyone should think that he had concealed fire beneath the altar, and that therefore the fire called down from heaven by him had not descended from heaven, but had been hidden by him in the earth and had thence risen up into the altar in his usual manner. Hear Saint Chrysostom, in the homily on Peter and Elijah, which Surius quotes from Metaphrastes on the first day of August: *Attend to what I am about to say, for I myself have seen what I shall describe. In the altars of idols there are certain holes in the lower part of the altar, and a certain dark pit; and the artificers of error descend into that pit, and blow fire up through the holes to perform the sacrifice, so that many, being deceived, think that fire to be heavenly. Lest therefore Elijah too should come under suspicion of having contrived something of the sort, he poured out water, so that by that water he might show that there were no holes beneath the altar; for wherever water finds a hole, the water does not remain there, but must necessarily flow out through those very holes.* Rabbi Solomon fabulously relates that Elijah, since he could not fill so great a channel with the water brought in a jar, caused part of the water poured from the jar to touch his hands, and so by a new miracle waters flowed most abundantly from the hands of Elijah, as from two fountains, filling the entire channel; and that therefore it is said in 2 Kings 3:11 that water was poured upon the hands of Elijah by Elisha. # GEM

# GEM In the Hebrew: "Jehovah, He is Elohim; Jehovah, He is Elohim" — that is to say, Jehovah, who first revealed this His name to Moses in Exodus 6:3, and who was invoked by him and by the other Israelites under this name, He alone is the true Elohim, that is, God, and the divine Power governing all things, ordering heaven and earth, from whom therefore we and all other men ought to expect and seek all nourishment and every good. For "Elohim" signifies God insofar as He holds providence over all things, governs all, judges all, and vindicates all. Hence the Hebrews say that the name of Elijah — or, as he is called in Hebrew, Eliahu, who was previously called Jaberseuali — was derived from this very confession. For "Eliahu" is the same as "El la hu," that is, "God the strong, Himself." Now "El" is an abbreviation of Elohim, "la" is a contraction of Jehovah, and "hu" means "Himself." Similarly, the name "Maccabee," taken by Judas and his brothers, was drawn from that verse of Exodus 15: "Mi camochah Baelim Jehova," that is, "Who is like unto Thee among the mighty, O Lord?" For the Maccabees bore this as their device and emblem of their battle and victory, placing no hope in their own strength, but their whole trust in God. For Elijah, by the help of God, proved stronger than Ahab, Jezebel, and the Baalites — not only passively, by bravely enduring their persecutions, but actively, by slaying them all. So likewise Saint Lydwine, who through numerous and most grievous illnesses for thirty years and more was an admirable mirror of patience, received her very name as an omen of her future suffering. For "Lydwine" in Flemish signifies the same as "broad patience," as her Life at the beginning attests, in Surius, vol. 7, for the day of April. So too Mars in Greek is called "Ares," because his nature is held to be indomitable, invincible, and immutable, as Plato states in the Cratylus — whence Virgil, alluding to this etymology, calls the Latins "unbroken by adverse Mars." So too Agamemnon, says Plato, received his name on account of his endurance of the labors he was to undergo; for besieging Troy for ten years he suffered greatly — hence he was called "Agamemnon" as though "agan menon," that is, "persevering greatly in the siege of Troy." So Elijah, the divine Athlete, is likewise "the strong one of the Lord," or "the Lord is strong," in him and in his works. Benjamin notes in his Itinerary that the cave of Elijah on Carmel was still visible in his day, along with the remains of the ruined altar, whose site was circular, with a diameter of nearly four cubits. From this it is evident that an admirable virtue had been divinely implanted in this food and bread, so that Elijah's strength sufficed to travel without any other nourishment for forty days and nights. The journey from Beersheba to Mount Horeb is one of few days — namely four or five — especially since Elijah walked both by night and by day, and fear added wings to him, as Serarius says. Hence it is evident that he, struck down by fear, dread, sorrow, and anguish, wandered erratically, turning here and there wherever his will carried him, and did not take the direct road; and perhaps he had not even fixed in his mind a destination for the journey, namely to go to Horeb. So Abulensis. Note here that Elijah fasted forty days, just as Moses had done, and afterward Christ — in whose imitation the Church instituted the fast of Lent. St. Ambrose wrote a book *On Elijah and Fasting*, in which he sets forth the benefits of fasting and draws various examples from Sacred Scripture on this matter, as well as the harms of drunkenness and intemperance. **Horeb**] Horeb is Mount Sinai, as I showed at Exodus 17:6, on which Moses, receiving the law from God, spoke with him, Exodus 19; so that Elijah likewise, suffering and fighting for the law of God on that same mountain, might speak with that same God, and receive from him the manner of defending it. **He abode in a cave**] In this cave — as it seems — Moses was hidden by God, Exodus 33:22, and saw the back and the glory of God as he passed by; and therefore in that same cave Elijah now saw the same, but under a different form. For Moses saw it in the appearance of a great light and splendor; Elijah, however, saw it in the appearance of the whistling of a gentle breeze: so say R. Solomon, Lyran, and others. Hence this cave was held in great veneration by the ancients, and accordingly Josephus, Book 2 of the *Antiquities*, chapter 12, records that before the age of Moses, that place was seen by the ancients as so sacred that shepherds were restrained by a certain religious awe from driving their flocks to that mountain, because they suspected there was something divine there. By way of symbol, St. Gregory, Book 2 on Ezechiel, Homily 13, says: *Elijah, when he heard the voice of the Lord speaking with him, is described as standing at the door of his cave and veiling his face; because when through the grace of contemplation the voice of heavenly understanding comes into the mind, the whole man is no longer entirely within the cave, since care for the flesh does not possess his soul, but he stands at the door, because he meditates upon going out from the narrowness of mortality. But now he who stands at the door of the cave and perceives the words of God in the ear of his heart must needs veil his face; because while through heavenly grace he is lifted to understand higher things...*
