So Abulensis and Serarius. **And he said** (the Angel as God's vicar) **to him: Go forth and stand on the mountain before the Lord; and behold the Lord passeth, and a great and strong wind before the Lord overthrowing the mountains and breaking the rocks in pieces before the Lord: the Lord is not in the wind. And after the wind an earthquake: the Lord is not in the earthquake. And after the earthquake a fire: the Lord is not in the fire. And after the fire a whistling of a gentle air.**] In Hebrew: a whistle, or a silent voice, or a subdued and delicate one. The Septuagint adds *and there* (in the whistle) *was the Lord.* Note that the purpose and aim of this vision was that God might console, soften, and instruct Elijah, who was inflamed with anger, weariness, and grief over the license, impunity, and crimes of Jezebel and the Baalites. First, therefore, he placed before his eyes and senses a most violent wind, so great that it seemed it would overthrow mountains and shatter rocks; then a great earthquake; then a burning and blazing fire — as if to say: To me, O Elijah, winds, earth, fires, and indeed all the elements, and all creatures of the middle, highest, and lowest orders are at hand, so that I might destroy the idolaters and Jezebel; but this manner is not the ordinary and accustomed one of my providence and clemency, nor is it fitting; and therefore I do not wish to use it. Hence he says: *The Lord is not in it.* Then in the fourth place, God offers to Elijah's ears a gentle breeze, or spirit and whistle, representing himself and his clemency and providence; for this gentle spirit signifies the patience of God and his long-suffering in punishing sins, which Elijah — and every apostolic man — ought to imitate and put on. So St. Irenaeus, Book 4 against Heresies, chapter 37: *The prophet*, he says, *who was somewhat turbulent and inclined to vengeance, was being taught to act more gently.* And Tertullian, *On Patience*, chapter 15: *The spirit of the most mild and meek One* (that is, of God) *sits upon his throne, who is not gathered up in whirlwind, nor darkened in cloud, but is open and simple in tender serenity, whom Elijah saw in the third place; for where God is, there also is his nursling, namely patience.* And St. Macarius, Homily 6: *Behold*, he says, *a voice of gentle air, and in it the Lord. It is clear therefore that the repose of the Lord resides in peace and tranquility.* And Theodoret, Question 57: *By this*, he says, *he shows that gentleness, kindness, and clemency alone are pleasing to God; while each of the other things is attracted by the wickedness of men.* St. Chrysostom, Homily 1 on Elijah, asserts at the end that God therefore quickly took Elijah up, lest if he lived longer, the world should be destroyed by his fire and zeal; but Chrysostom is rather severe in his judgment of Elijah's severe zeal. So also Procopius. Secondly, this gentle spirit or whistle conveyed the voice of God to Elijah, by which God instructed him — being excessively disturbed, faint of heart, and supposing himself to be the only remaining worshiper and prophet of God...

For he heard the wind loudly roaring, violently shaking the place, and a gentle whisper hissing softly; the fire, however, he partly heard, inasmuch as it crackled, and partly saw, inasmuch as it gave light. [HE COVERED HIS FACE WITH HIS MANTLE,] that he might show reverence toward God manifesting Himself by these signs, as though he were unworthy to gaze upon Him and behold Him. [THE PROPHETS THOU HAST SLAIN WITH THE SWORD] (because they opposed the worship of Baal), [AND I AM LEFT ALONE] (a prophet and zealot of the true religion, who mightily oppose myself to the same), [AND THEY SEEK MY LIFE,] that they may kill me; I being killed, therefore, no prophet will remain, no zealot to defend Thy worship and oppose himself to the Baalites. All is lost, then, as regards the true religion, as regards Thy worship, as regards the whole Synagogue; the impious Jezebel occupies everything; Baal is worshipped everywhere; everywhere perfidy and idolatry overflow. This is the cause of my grief and sorrow, of my flight and exile, so that I would rather choose to die than to live and see such great evils befalling my race and the people of God. Thus Elias, consumed with grief, anger, and zeal. Verses 15 and 16. [THOU SHALT ANOINT HAZAEL KING OVER SYRIA, AND JEHU THE SON OF NAMSI THOU SHALT ANOINT KING OVER ISRAEL; AND ELISEUS, ETC., THOU SHALT ANOINT PROPHET IN THY PLACE,] so that they may truly execute the just vengeance upon the Baalites which thou, O Elias, so greatly desirest. Hence in the praises of Elias it is said in Ecclesiasticus 48, verse 8: *Who anointest kings unto repentance*, that is, unto the punishment and vengeance of the impious, that they might be avengers of divine worship. *Thou shalt anoint the king*, that is, thou shalt designate the king, thou shalt bestow the kingdom upon him. This is a catachresis; for since kings were created by anointing, hence "to anoint" means "to make a king." For Hazael was not anointed by Elias, but was designated king by Eliseus, whom Elias sent to him; Jehu, however, was anointed king of Israel by one of Eliseus's disciples, as is evident from 2 Kings 8, 13 and following. And so Elias carried out these divine commands through Eliseus, whom for this reason, at the command of God, he appointed as his successor. Thus we do not read that Eliseus was anointed, but was designated prophet by the mantle cast upon him by Elias, at verse 19. So hold Theodoret, Rabanus, Dionysius, Abulensis, and Salianus. Sanchez, however, takes "thou shalt anoint" in the strict and proper sense, and holds that all three were anointed by Elias, even though Scripture does not narrate this as having been done. For it seems that Elias returned by God's command through the desert to Damascus — a longer and more arduous route — in order to anoint Hazael there as king; and though Hazael was afterwards anointed again by another, nothing prevents him from having been anointed first by Elias. For so also David and Saul were anointed twice. This opinion is probable, and takes the verse properly and literally. [WHO IS OF ABELMEHOLA.] This city was in the tribe of Manasse on this side of the Jordan. Eliseus was therefore of the tribe of Manasse, just as Elias was of the tribe of Gad. So hold St. Jerome, Salianus, Adrichomius, and others; although St. Epiphanius and Isidore, in the Life of Eliseus, say that he was born of the tribe of Ruben, who also add that at the birth of Eliseus one of the golden calves which Jeroboam had made lowed, and that this lowing was heard in Jerusalem, and that from this a certain prudent priest conjectured that on that day one had been born who would in future times bring destruction upon all graven images and idols. Verse 17. [AND IT SHALL COME TO PASS, THAT WHOSOEVER SHALL ESCAPE THE SWORD OF HAZAEL, JEHU SHALL SLAY.] How Hazael and Jehu killed the idolaters we shall hear in what follows. [AND WHOSOEVER SHALL ESCAPE FROM THE SWORD OF JEHU, ELISEUS SHALL SLAY,] not with a bodily sword, but with a spiritual one, namely the sword of the tongue, with which he shall sharply rebuke them and threaten them with present and eternal death, as Angelomus, Rabanus, and Hugo say.

God sets against what Elias had said in verse 14 — *I am left alone* — seven thousand men; as if to say: Thou art mistaken, Elias, thou art not alone. Behold, Eliseus comes to thee; behold, seven thousand men who worship God, whom I shall preserve. The Hebrew *hisarti* means "I shall cause to remain, I shall reserve, I shall keep," lest they be slain by Jezabel, or perverted and drawn over to the worship of Baal. [AND EVERY MOUTH THAT HATH NOT BOWED DOWN TO IT, KISSING THE HANDS,] supply *I shall preserve*, that is, I shall keep and preserve alive. The sense of the whole verse is: I shall preserve seven thousand men who have not bent the knee before Baal, nor worshipped him by kissing the hand; for this was a twofold mode of adoration, genuflection and the kissing of the hand. *Kissing the hands* — in Hebrew, *kissing him*, namely Baal, to greet and adore him with this kiss as their own God and Lord. Our translator renders it *kissing the hands* in order to indicate the rite and manner of adoration, which was to kiss the hand of the idol, or to kiss one's own hand with that rite and gesture as if one were kissing the hand of the idol, being at a distance or far removed from it. Job alludes to this rite in chapter 31, verse 26: *If I beheld the sun when it shined, or the moon walking in brightness, and my heart hath been secretly enticed, and I have kissed my hand with my mouth.* Of this external custom the pagans themselves make mention: Pliny, book 28, chapter 2: *In the act of adoration we bring the right hand to our lips for a kiss*; and Minucius Felix in the Octavius: *Caecilius, having seen the statue of Serapis, after the manner of the superstitious common people, brought his hand to his mouth and pressed a kiss with his lips.* # GEM

Eliseus was therefore a wealthy farmer, inasmuch as he possessed twelve yoke of oxen and with them tilled an extensive field. That these oxen were his own is evident from the last verse, where he himself slaughtered a pair of them for the feast with which he bade farewell to his household. [AND HE HIMSELF WAS WITH THE TWELFTH YOKE OF OXEN THAT WERE PLOWING.] For each yoke of oxen, that is, each pair of oxen, had its own driver and plowman; wherefore there were here twelve yokes of oxen and likewise twelve plowmen, of whom Eliseus was one. [HE CAST HIS MANTLE UPON HIM,] to call him to follow him, and to make him his companion and sharer of his way of life, just as even now a novice in a Religious Order is given the habit, or garment, of that same Order. In a similar manner of old a bridegroom, in taking a bride to himself, would cover her with his own mantle. Hence that passage of Ruth seeking marriage with Boaz, chapter 3, verse 9: *Spread thy mantle over thy handmaid*; and that of Ezekiel 16: *I spread my garment over thee.* By this ceremony, therefore, Eliseus was instituted as companion and successor of Elias in the prophetic office, and his heart was altogether transformed by God, so that from a layman he became a Religious, from a farmer a Prophet, from an unlettered man one taught by God. Hence he immediately left his oxen, his home, his parents, and followed Elias. Sanchez adds that he was anointed by Elias; for this God had commanded at verse 16. The anointing, he says, made Eliseus a Prophet (for this was the custom in that anointing); but the mantle made him the companion and monk of Elias.

As if to say: I did but touch thee with the mantle; but God touched thy heart, and transformed it utterly from carnal to spiritual and heavenly, so that thou mightest leave all things and follow me readily, and contend as I do against the idolaters.

Elisha, because he was hastening and eager to follow Elijah immediately, therefore used the plough for firewood, to cook the flesh of the oxen. He thus gave us a type and example, that we should follow God's calling immediately, resolutely, and with constancy, as Cajetan and Abulensis observe. For he celebrated a feast at which he bade farewell to his own people, in order to show that he was leaving behind his oxen, his plough, his field, and all things whatsoever, and was joyfully following Elijah. From this Abulensis concludes that he was a young man and unmarried — that is, celibate — for otherwise he would have asked Elijah for leave to take farewell not only of his wife and children, but also of his father and mother. Furthermore, Abulensis says, Elisha wished nothing to remain with him in the world, so that, free and unencumbered, he might pass over entirely to the Lord; and therefore he consumed the plough and the oxen — in which act he gave an example to Religious, that they should do the same. Hence also St. Ignatius, in his Epistle to the Philadelphians, asserts that Elisha was celibate and a virgin: for indeed Elisha, by the instinct of God calling him, was already raised above every earthly desire. For, as St. Cyprian says in his treatise on the Lord's Prayer, concerning the words *Thy kingdom come*: "He who has already renounced the world is greater than its honours and its dominion; and therefore he who dedicates himself to God and to Christ desires not earthly but heavenly kingdoms" — and desires God Himself, because in Him we shall reign. And St. Ambrose, in Book 6 of *On the Sacraments*, chapter 5, says: "If God reigns in us, the adversary can find no place. Guilt does not reign, sin does not reign; but virtue reigns, chastity reigns, devotion reigns."

Ben-hadad is the same as Ben-Adad, that is, the son of Adad, as Josephus states (Book 8, Chapter 8). He was indeed the son of that rebellious Idumaean who, after Idumaea had been conquered by David, fled to Egypt, and returning thence after David's death, recovering his courage and his kingdom, rebelled against Solomon (Chapter 11, verse 25). Therefore, nurturing his paternal disposition and hatred against Israel, he afflicted them gravely and continually. Hence, in Chapter 11, verse 25, it was said and foretold of him: "This is the evil of Adad, and his hatred against Israel." A political lesson: learn here how dangerous is the obstinate rebellion of princes, and how it is handed down to their children through many generations. The Spanish experience this very thing in Holland. Rebellion, therefore, in its beginnings must either be cut down or reconciled, according to that maxim: *Resist beginnings.*

Not by right, but by power and command; for I wish to have it and demand it, being mightier than you, so that you cannot resist me. For the resources of Ahab and Israel were so afflicted that he could not put into the field more than seven thousand men (verse 15). Here, therefore, God begins to punish Ahab and the Israelites on account of the worship of Baal.

This is a new demand by Ben-hadad, by which he asks Ahab to permit his men to plunder not only the royal palace, but also the entire city of Samaria, as Josephus says — that is, to despoil the city and carry off from it whatever is most precious, at his pleasure.

The meaning is: "I swear I will destroy the entire city of Samaria and overthrow it so utterly that not so much as a handful of dust shall remain in it, but whatever is in it shall be carried off in the fists of my most numerous army." Hence the Chaldean renders it: *If the dust of Samaria suffice to be carried away in the soles of the feet of the people who shall be with me.* So Abulensis. This is a hyperbole and a proverbial expression, by which the boastful Ben-hadad, exulting in the thirty-two kings who followed him and his innumerable forces, boasts and swears that he will overturn Samaria, plunder it, and raze it so that nothing worth plundering shall remain. For his soldiers were not so painstaking or so idle as to carry off all the dust from Samaria — rather, they sought to carry off all the treasures and all precious things from it. They sought gold, not dirt.

This is a proverb, which says: Do not sing triumph before the victory. For the soldier goes forth to battle with his armor on; but when victory is won, he ungirds himself, lays down his arms, and rests upon the spoils of the enemy. Hence the Chaldean paraphrase says: "Let him who is ready praise himself, and who descends into battle, as a man who conquers and ascends victorious from it." Vatablus renders it: "Let him not glory who is about to enter battle, as though he had already conquered." **Verse 23.** But the servants of the king of Syria (Ben-hadad) said to him: *The gods of the mountains are their gods, therefore they overcame us; but it is better that we fight against them in the plains, and we shall prevail over them.* That is to say: We were defeated by the Israelites who dwell in the mountains, because we fought at Samaria, which is situated on a hill, and is therefore under the protection of the gods who preside over mountains and mountainous regions. Let us therefore descend into the valleys, where the gods of the valleys will be with us, that we may overcome the mountain-dwellers and the gods of the mountains while fighting in the valleys. Thus these foolish men supposed the God of the Hebrews to be merely a God of mountains. But whence came this error and foolish persuasion? The answer lies in several things gathered together. First, because Moses, the leader of the Hebrews, had spoken with God on Mount Sinai and received the law from Him there. Second, because on Mount Zion the Temple had been erected by Solomon, in which God dwelt. Third, because the Hebrews worshipped God on the high places, that is, on the hills. Fourth and finally, and what bears most directly upon the present disaster, because the Syrians had received this defeat from the Israelites on the mountain of Samaria. This was the superstition of the Gentiles: to assign Oreads, that is mountain goddesses, to the mountains; Silvani to the forests; sea-gods, such as Neptune, to the sea; concerning which Saint Augustine writes, in Book IV of *The City of God*, chapter 8, where he asserts that they assigned the god Jugatinus to the mountain ridges, Collina to the hills, and the goddess Vallonia to the valleys. Of the same Ovid sings in Book I of the *Fasti*, introducing them speaking thus: "We are rural gods, who hold dominion over the high mountains; the rule of his own dwelling belongs to Jove." **Verse 24.** *Do therefore this thing: Remove the kings every one out of their places, and put captains in their rooms*—that is, men skilled in war and experienced in military affairs, not idle men accustomed to luxury as kings are; men who will fight more vigorously in hope of obtaining glory and honor from you. So Abulensis. For these are what the Hebrew word *pachoth* signifies, that is, commanders; the Chaldean renders it *imperatores*. **Verse 28.** *Because the Syrians have said: The Lord* (Hebrew: Jehovah, that is, the God of the Israelites) *is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys; therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand*—that I may show that I am as much the God of the valleys as of the mountains, and that you, O Ahab, may acknowledge and worship Me as the true God, and not Baal.
That is, into the innermost and most secret chamber, that he might hide himself there.

These are like garments of servitude, and tokens of mourning and penitence, by which we profess that we repent of what we have done—namely, that we made war against Israel—and that we surrender ourselves as the conquered to the conqueror, as though into servitude. *And ropes upon our heads*—that we may profess that we are guilty of death and deserving of the cord and noose to be fastened about our necks to strangle us. Hear Josephus: "They, clothed in sackcloth and with their heads bound with cords—which was the ancient custom of supplication among the Syrians—approached Ahab, and signified that Hadad sought only the grace of his life, and that he would be his subject in perpetual submission."

Ahab had supposed that Ben-hadad had perished in so great a slaughter of the enemy. Hence, hearing that he had survived the disaster, and exulting in so great a victory, he took pity on him and rashly and imprudently burst out with these words: *If he yet live, he is my brother.* That is to say: He will be dear to me as a brother; I will treat him as a brother. Kings customarily call one another brothers. These words of Ahab were immediately seized upon by Ben-hadad's servants from his mouth, and they accepted them as an altogether favorable omen, as though that friendly word which had escaped him were a sign of mercy and salvation for Ben-hadad. They therefore immediately brought him to Ahab, and presented him as a suppliant; and Ahab, true to his word, received him as a brother and took him up into his chariot. But shortly afterward he paid the penalty for this rashness, when, Ben-hadad renewing the war, Ahab fell in it, as will be related in the final chapter. Symbolically, Ben-hadad is a type of the devil, who always persecutes the faithful and the saints, now by force, now by fraud and cunning. Therefore, whoever makes peace or a covenant with him, as Ahab did, will most certainly be overthrown by him. So Eucherius. **Verse 34.** *Streets for thee in Damascus*—in which you shall establish law and judges, likewise a marketplace for goods and customs dues, as a sign of subjection. That is to say: I will grant you a portion of my royal city of Damascus, to show that it is as subject to you as to me—indeed, that I am subject to you, and therefore pay this tribute. So Abulensis, Cajetan, and Vatablus.

Josephus considers this man to have been the Prophet Micaiah, who in the last chapter foretold the destruction of Ahab. Glycas and the Hebrews in the Seder Olam transmit the same opinion, and it may be gathered from the last chapter, verse 26, where in place of "carry" the Hebrew has *imlttum vahascibeliti*, that is, "make him return." But the matter is uncertain. Hence Serarius and others hold that this man was different from Micaiah, and for that reason is not named. HE SAID TO HIS COMPANION IN THE WORD OF THE LORD (that is, by oracle and command of God): Strike me — so that by the wound of my own head I might represent to King Ahab his own wound, which he received in his soul by sparing Benhadad; and the wound of the people, namely that which the whole people received in King Ahab as their head; and thirdly, the wound which both Ahab and the people shall receive, when he shall be slain by Benhadad who renews the war, as will be evident in the last chapter and in the present chapter, verse 8 and following. So Cajetan. Abulensis adds another reason for this wound: namely, that through it the Prophet might not be recognized by Ahab, but might appear to be a soldier, and thus gain access to him, and expound to him this parable of the wound — for if he had been recognized, Ahab the idolater would have refused to hear him. The third and most important reason for this striking and wounding was the example it provided: namely, that God, in this prophet — both in the one who struck and the one who was struck — might give an example and mirror of perfect obedience. Hence, says Abulensis, it must be believed that shortly afterward God healed this wound supernaturally, so that not even a scar remained, and this in order to show how great is the merit of pure and simple obedience, and how conversely disobedience is hateful to God — since on account of it, He delivered to be slain by a lion the man who had refused to strike the Prophet. # GEM Hear St. Chrysostom, Oration 1 against the Jews, near the beginning: *He who struck the Prophet came away unharmed; he who spared him paid the penalty — so that you might understand that in divine commands one must not be curious, nor examine the nature of the things commanded, but do nothing other than obey. For lest the first man, out of fear, should spare the Prophet, he did not say to him simply: "Strike me," but added: "In the word of the Lord" — that is, God commands; seek no further. It is the King who prescribes this law: reverence the authority of the one who commands, and obey with the utmost readiness of soul. But the other did not bring himself to do this, and paid the most grievous penalties, urging by his own example those who should follow, to obey in all things whatever God commands, and to comply henceforth.* Finally, Rabbi Solomon and Lyranus hold that the blood of this Prophet made satisfaction for the sins of the people of Israel, lest they perish with Ahab in the last chapter — but that people was defeated, and such a blow was neither able to satisfy for them, nor did the Prophet intend it so. BECAUSE THOU HAST LET GO A MAN WORTHY OF DEATH — namely Benhadad, king of Syria, who was guilty of death both on account of his continual hatred and warfare against the Israelites, and especially on account of the blasphemy by which he said that God was the God of the mountains and not of the valleys — for this greatly provoked God, as is evident from verse 28. So the Hebrews, Rupert, Lyranus, Dionysius, and others. *Thy life shall go for his life.* — Thou shalt be slain in place of Benhadad, whom thou shouldst have slain. *And thy people for his people* — shall be slain in place of the Syrians subject to Benhadad, whom he ought to have killed. And so it came to pass in reality, as we shall hear in the last chapter. This is an example of neglected justice and righteous vengeance, to be noted and deeply impressed upon the minds of kings and judges: *Thou art a murderer, in that thou didst release the enemy*, says St. Chrysostom in the passage cited. *And the King of Israel (Ahab) returned to his house, refusing to listen.*

Naboth was an upright man, as is clear from the text itself. The name Naboth in Hebrew signifies the same as "fruit of speech" or "fruit of eloquence," according to St. Jerome and Pagninus. Ahab coveted his vineyard because it lay adjacent to his own property; for the avarice and cupidity of the rich is insatiable. On this subject, St. Ambrose wrote a remarkable treatise concerning this very chapter, entitled *De Naboth* (or *De Nabuthe*), in which he sharply rebukes wealthy men who are greedy and rapacious, such as Ahab was. Thus in chapter 2 he writes: "O rich man, you do not know how poor you are, how destitute you appear to yourself — you who call yourself wealthy! The more you have acquired, the more you seek; and however much you have gained, you still find yourself in want. Avarice is inflamed by gain, not extinguished by it. Cupidity has, as it were, certain steps: the more of them it has climbed, the more it hastens toward greater heights, and thus the more grievous will be the fall of him who is about to stumble." And after a few lines: "Scripture teaches us how miserably the rich man is in need and how abjectly he begs. King Ahab was in Israel, and Naboth was a poor man. The one abounded in the resources of a kingdom; the other possessed but the turf of a narrow plot of ground. The poor man coveted nothing of the rich man's possessions; yet the king considered himself to be in want, because his poor neighbour had a vineyard. Who then seems poor to you — he who is content with his own, or he who covets what belongs to another? The one is clearly poor in his estate; the other is poor in his very character."

These words are powerfully pressed by St. Ambrose in the same chapter 2 already cited: "What other words," he says, "does one who publicly begs for alms use, except: *Give me* — that is: Give me, because I am in need; give me, because I can have no other means of subsistence; give me, because I have no bread for food, no coin for drink, no provision for sustenance, no substance for clothing; give me, because the Lord has given to you wherewith you ought to be generous, but has not given to me; give me, for unless you give, I shall have nothing; give me, because it is written: *Give alms*." And he adds: "How abject are these words, how vile!"

St. Ambrose in chapter 3 notes the impudence of the avaricious king. "This then," he says, "was the whole madness, the whole frenzy — that space should be sought in which to grow cheap vegetables. It is not so much that you desire to possess something useful for yourselves, as that you wish to exclude others. You are more concerned with despoiling the poor than with your own advantage. You count it an injury to yourselves if a poor man possesses anything that might be deemed worthy of a rich man's ownership. You reckon as your own loss whatever belongs to another." **Propitious be the LORD to me** — In Hebrew: *May the LORD forbid it*, that is, far be it from me. The Septuagint has: *Let it not happen to me by the Lord*; the Chaldean: *Far be it from me before the Lord.* **That I should give the inheritance of my fathers to you** — That is to say: The inheritance entrusted to me by my parents must be preserved, not so much as my own possession, but as their inheritance. For this memory and reverence a son owes to his parents — to protect their goods and transmit them to posterity. Moreover, the same St. Ambrose in chapter 3 already cited writes: "Let him reckon the money of the rich man as a kind of contamination of himself; as if to say: Your money perish with you; but I cannot sell the inheritance of my fathers." # GEM Tropologically, the vineyard signifies the soul, which is our entire inheritance; therefore, take care lest you sell it at the base price of pleasure or honour to the devil, to death, and to hell. Hence St. Ambrose, in his *Exhortation to Virgins*, takes the vineyard to signify virginity, Jezebel to signify concupiscence, and Naboth to signify Christ: "The vineyard," he says, "is a certain virginal fruitfulness; marriages are like garden-beds of vegetables, in which there is frequent contention; and therefore the herbs of the garden quickly fall and wither, unless old age puts an end to them, or continence brings them to perfection. Let not Ahab come upon you, who covets to destroy and extinguish your vineyard; nor let Jezebel come upon you — that vain and worldly overflow, for this is what the name signifies: vain and empty redundancy — but let Naboth come to you, who comes from the Father, as the very interpretation of his name declares, who defends the vineyard with his own blood and offers death for its sake. This is he who was stoned for us, who died for us, who for us was charged with false accusations." **And he did not eat bread** — This was out of anger and sorrow that Naboth had resisted his cupidity and had as it were despised him. Hear St. Ambrose in chapter 6: "He did not eat his own bread, because he was seeking what belonged to another. For indeed the rich, who live by plunder and sustain their expenditure through rapine, eat another's bread rather than their own. Or certainly he did not eat his bread, wishing to punish himself with death because something had been denied him." And elegantly, in chapter 6: "The pagans call Dis the lord of the underworld and the arbiter of death. They also call him the *rich* (*dives*), because the rich man knows nothing but how to bring death — he whose kingdom is made of the dead, whose throne is in hell. For what is a rich man but a certain insatiable abyss of riches, an insatiable hunger for gold? The more he has drunk in, the more he burns." And after some intervening passages: "Sweet is the sleep of the servant, whether he eats little or much; but for the man sated with riches there is none to let him sleep. Cupidity awakens him, the restless anxiety of seizing what belongs to others drives him on, envy torments him, delay vexes him, the barren unfruitfulness of his revenues disturbs him, and abundance itself gives him no rest."

To an impious husband, Jezebel was yet more impious; to an avaricious man, yet more avaricious; to a cruel man, yet more cruel; to a rapacious man, yet more rapacious; to a greedy man, yet more greedy. Hence St. Ambrose in chapter 9 teaches that Jezebel is a type of avarice, which promises to the rich, against all justice, the possessions they desire. For she was to Ahab and to Israel the torch and incitement of idolatry and of all evils — a very fury, as it were, from hell — as is clear from verse 25.

Jezebel orders a fast to be proclaimed, under the pretext of piety and zeal for God, so that through this public penance of the people the blasphemy of Naboth might be expiated — a blasphemy which she had falsely arranged to be charged against him. For blasphemy was a thing of horror to the Hebrews; wherefore, upon hearing it, they would tear their garments and produce other signs of public grief and detestation of so unworthy a deed.

He blessed — that is, he cursed. This is a euphemism by antiphrasis: for because blasphemy is so dreadful a thing, the Jews did not dare even to name it, but expressed it by its contrary, namely by the word "blessing" and "praise." So St. Jerome, in his letter 34 to Julian.

For a blasphemer was to be crushed with stones according to the law in Leviticus 16. It is further recorded in 2 Kings 9:26 that the sons of Naboth were also put to death, so that in this way their vineyard might pass from them to Ahab — as though, with no heir remaining, the king would be the heir. In a similar manner, Eudoxia, wife of the Emperor Arcadius, coveting the vineyard of the widow Callirrhoe, drove St. Chrysostom — who was pleading the widow's cause — into exile, and through a thousand miseries of the road, of hunger, thirst, and soldiers tormenting him, brought him to his death; so that St. Chrysostom is truly not only a Confessor but also a Martyr, having been unjustly slain for the sake of justice, not by one death but by a hundred. Allegorically: Naboth was a type of Christ, who was accused of blasphemy by false witnesses at the instigation of the Scribes and Pharisees who coveted His vineyard — that is, the Synagogue — because He declared Himself the Son of God, and was therefore put to death, in accordance with the parable of the vineyard which Christ Himself set before them on this very matter in Matthew 21, verse 23. For the voice of the Scribes concerning Christ is heard in verse 38: "This is the heir; come, let us kill him and seize his inheritance." Hence also the dogs — that is, the Gentiles (for the Jews despised them as dogs) — the Romans, under the command of Titus and Vespasian, licked up the blood of the Jews who had killed Christ, in the same place, namely Jerusalem, and at the same time, that is, at Passover, at which they themselves had slain Christ. So Angelomus.

The Hebrew word is *resch*, meaning "take possession," namely by title of confiscation; because Naboth had been accused of the crime of lèse-majesté, both divine and royal, and had accordingly been condemned. Therefore his vineyard was assigned by Jezebel to the treasury of King Ahab.

You have slain Naboth in order to seize and possess his vineyard; therefore to an unjust murder you have added an unjust possession: wherefore by the law of retaliation you shall likewise be slain, and you shall lose both the vineyard and all else, being deprived as much of your possession as of your life. In the place where the dogs licked the blood of Naboth, they shall likewise lick your blood. That this was fulfilled in very fact through the fitting and just vengeance of God is narrated in chapter 22, verse 38. You will object: it is said there that the dogs licked the blood of Ahab in Samaria, whereas the dogs licked the blood of Naboth in Jezreel, where he was slain. The answer is that Jezreel belonged to Samaria, which was the metropolis and capital of the whole kingdom of Israel. The same place is therefore to be understood here in a general sense, not in a particular one. So hold Abulensis, Lyran, and Vatablus. The Hebrews respond differently, namely that the arms of Ahab, stained with his blood, were washed in the pool of Jezreel when they were brought there to the armory, and that the dogs at that time licked his washed-away blood; but this Abulensis refutes on the grounds that in chapter 22, verse 38, this prophecy is said to have been fulfilled when the dogs licked his blood in Samaria, for which Josephus erroneously substitutes Jezreel. One might also say that God mitigated and withdrew this punishment on account of Ahab's penitence, as is intimated at verse 29. Finally, Salianus and Sanchez judge most clearly and fully that it was fulfilled in Joram the son of Ahab: for he was slain in the field of Jezreel. For blood frequently signifies sons, since they are begotten from the blood of their parents; whence that saying of Anchises concerning Julius Caesar: "Cast away your weapons, O my blood" — that is, "O my son Julius" — in the Aeneid, Book 6. The same is proved both from verse 29, where God, mitigating this punishment threatened against Ahab on account of his penitence, says that He will transfer it from Ahab to his son Joram, and also from 2 Kings, chapter 9, verses 25 and 26, where, after the slaying of Joram, Jehu commanded his corpse to be cast into the field of Naboth, to be licked and devoured by dogs, that this prophecy of God might be fulfilled concerning him. Thus Aristobulus, the first of the Maccabees to be king of the Jews, slew his brother Antigonus, but by the just judgment of God was tormented by pains and vomited blood, which was poured out in the very same place where he had shed his brother's blood. Whereupon, groaning, he cried out, as Josephus relates in Antiquities 13.19: "My impious and wicked deed is not hidden from God," he said, "seeing that I am so swiftly called to account for the blood of my kinsman. O shameless body, how long will you retain the soul, which the shades of my mother and my brother alike demand? Why do you not surrender it at once, lest my blood must thus be offered up piecemeal, and so make atonement to those crushed by wickedness and parricide?" Scarcely had he spoken these words when he breathed out his soul, having reigned but a single year.

Sold, as a slave surrendered to sin. Hence Cajetan says: "sold, that is, handed over to sin; for what is sold is surrendered to the buyer as to a master" — as if to say: You, O Ahab, by your own evil will, are wholly given over to sin, as though you had been sold or hired out for no other purpose than to commit sin. There is an allusion to slaves who hire themselves out for fixed tasks and devote themselves entirely to them: for so Ahab had given himself over not only to idols but to his Jezebel, to be driven by her as a slave to every wickedness; so Abulensis. The sense therefore is, as if to say: You, O Ahab, are in truth neither a king nor an Israelite — that is, neither free nor of noble condition — but a slave of Baal and of Jezebel; because you have wholly surrendered and enslaved yourself to them, to obey them in all things and to do whatever they will. The cause was that Ithobaal, king of Tyre and father of Jezebel, and therefore father-in-law of Ahab, was a priest of Ashtareth, as Josephus says in Book 1 Against Apion, and by his very name professed the worship of Baal; for he was called Ithobaal, or, as our text renders it at chapter 16, verse 31, Ethbaal. This man, then, most wholly given over to Baal and Ashtareth — indeed consecrated as their priest — poured his impiety and idolatry into his daughter Jezebel, and into her husband Ahab. Hence Josephus, Book 9, chapter 7, says: "Baal was the god of the Tyrians, whom Ahab worshipped to please Ithobaal, king of the Tyrians and Sidonians, his father-in-law, dedicating a temple to him in Samaria and assigning prophets and the rest of his worship." Observe here and admire the boldness of Elijah, who rebukes King Ahab to his face and charges him with being a slave to sin, and accordingly denounces upon him utter destruction. So likewise Saint Athanasius, Saint Hilary, and Lucifer of Cagliari sharply rebuke the Arian Emperor Constantius and call him a Herod, a Nero, and an Antichrist. I have quoted their words in the commentary on Apocalypse 11, verse 5. Tropologically, see here how vile, wretched, and miserable is the sinner who, after the manner of Ahab, for a small pleasure — nay, for a single gold coin — sells himself and his soul to the devil, and surrenders it as a slave to his own desire, that it may become food for death and fuel for hell.
That is: I will utterly destroy your posterity root and branch. # GEM

It appears that Ahab's penitence here was born of fear of punishment rather than of love of justice, and was therefore servile, imperfect, and compelled; hence it seems that God did not remit his guilt, but only deferred the punishment, transferring it from him to his son, as is said at verse 29. So hold Lyran, Dionysius, and Cajetan; and indeed Saint Gregory, Homily 19 on Ezekiel, says: "In these words it must be weighed how much more the bitterness of grief pleases Him in His elect, who fear to lose God, if such penitence as this pleased Him even in the reprobate, because he feared to lose the present world; or how welcome must spontaneous affliction for sins be in those who please Him, if this penitence pleased Him for a time even in those who displeased Him." Nevertheless, Abulensis, Hugo, Vatablus, Sanchez, Serarius, and indeed Saint Chrysostom, in his Epistle to the lapsed Theodore (of Mopsuestia), judge that Ahab's penitence at this time was genuine and sincere. Chrysostom says: "Ahab, shaken by the enormity of his crime, repented, and clothed in sackcloth wept over his deed, and thus drew down upon himself the mercy of the Lord, so that He absolved him from all his sins. For God said to Elijah: Have you seen how Ahab is contrite before me, because he wept in my presence? I will not bring evil in his days." So likewise Saint Ambrose in the book On Naboth, chapter 17, and Tertullian in Book 4 Against Marcion, chapter 10. This opinion is equally probable, and is suggested by what God says to Elijah at verse 29: "Have you not seen how Ahab is humbled?" etc. Nor does it stand as an objection that Ahab shortly after his penitence returned to his former wickedness; for he was, as Theodoret says, a most unstable man, wholly governed by his Jezebel. For so today many persons repent in truth, but soon, when occasion is given, relapse into their accustomed sins. One may reconcile both opinions by saying that the penitence of Ahab was true attrition, but did not rise to contrition formed by charity. For he genuinely grieved over his sins on account of the calamities threatened against him by Elijah; but he did not grieve over them out of love of God, that is, because he had offended Him who is the supreme good. Wherefore he escaped the punishment but did not blot out the guilt, nor was he justified — which opinion appears most true and most in accord with Holy Scripture. Hence Calvin is altogether wrong in judging that this penitence of Ahab was feigned. "Because therefore he has humbled himself before me" — namely, before me not so much out of love as out of dread and fear; and therefore I repay him only temporal pardon and reward, saying: I will not bring evil in his days, but in the days of his son. Wherefore Saint Jerome rightly exclaims in Epistle 30 to Oceanus: "O happy penitence, which drew the eyes of God upon it; which by the confession of error changed the furious sentence of God!" He is speaking of the penitence of Ahab.

These were not prophets of God, but of Baal, or rather of the demon — and therefore false prophets, idolaters, and heretics. Hence Lucifer of Cagliari, in Book 1 *On the Apostate Kings*, notes that heretical princes cling to their own false prophets — that is, to heretical preachers — and despise the true Prophets of God, indeed persecuting and killing them, as did Constantius, Valens, Julian, and others, who, having been wretchedly seduced by their own Arians, perished miserably. ASCEND, AND THE LORD SHALL DELIVER IT INTO THE HAND OF THE KING — that is, into your hand, O Ahab. The devil, consulted by these false prophets through auguries and the divinations customary to them, concerning the outcome of the war, promises victory — but lies. And so he himself deceives the prophets, and they in turn deceive Ahab, and, finding him altogether too credulous toward them, they lead him into the snare of his own death like a mouse into a trap.

Jehoshaphat was a worshipper of the true God; therefore it displeased him that Ahab had consulted the prophets of Baal, and Baal himself — that is, the demon — nor did he believe them, being as they were liars. He therefore asked that a true Prophet of God be sought out, that his oracle be obtained and followed. MICAIAH — This is a different and distinct man from Micah, who is counted sixth among the Minor Prophets; indeed, the present Micaiah preceded him by one hundred and fifty years. For this man prophesied under Jehoshaphat and Ahab, whereas the other prophesied under Jotham and Ahaz; this one was in Israel, the other in Judah.

First, Cajetan considers that Micaiah said this before he had received an oracle from God, and that he therefore agreed with the other prophets, erred, and foretold what was false; but that presently, having been adjured by the king and enlightened by God, he received from God and pronounced the true oracle concerning the king's death. Second, Dionysius says that this response is ambiguous, and that it can be understood either of Ben-hadad, king of Syria, the enemy of King Ahab, or of Ahab himself. For thus the demon, when consulted, deceived Croesus by answering ambiguously, saying: *Croesus, crossing the Halys, shall destroy a great power* — and deceived Pyrrhus, saying, as Ennius attests: *I say that you, son of Aeacus, the Romans can conquer* — see Cicero, Book 2 *On Divination*. Third, Theodoret and Lyra consider that Micaiah here asserts nothing, but that, in order to show that he does not hate King Ahab as the king supposed, he merely wishes him a prosperous outcome in the war — so that *let Him deliver* is equivalent to *I wish that He may deliver*. Fourth, and most genuinely, Micaiah here speaks ironically — as if to say: Since, O Ahab, your prophets pledge you victory, and you believe them, go to battle; for they say you shall return victorious. Try their oracle then, since you do not believe mine and my God's. For I know you shall perish in battle; but I am unwilling to tell you this, because you are unworthy to hear the truth from God, seeing that you consult lying Baal and prefer to hear his lying oracles rather than my most true ones which come from the true God. So say Abulensis, Vatablus, Sanchez, and Serarius.

The king perceived from Micaiah's countenance, bearing, and manner of speaking that he was not speaking seriously but figuratively and ironically. He therefore adjured him to speak nothing but the truth and a genuine oracle, seriously and from the true sense of his own mind.

Micah foretells the flight of Israel and the death of King Ahab, declaring that he shall be slain in battle — for the shepherd, that is, the king, shall be killed; wherefore his subjects and soldiers, like sheep without a shepherd, shall scatter in flight from the battle on Mount Samaria.

At the right hand stood Angels, at the left stood devils; and from among these latter came forth the one who said in verse 22: *I will go out and be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets.* So also in Job 1, when the sons of God (that is, the Angels) stood before the Lord, Satan also was present among them. Concerning this passage, St. Gregory, in Book 2 of the *Moralia*, chapter 16, citing this very vision of Micaiah, writes: *At the right and left hand of God, therefore, the host of Angels stands arrayed; because the will of the elect spirits accords with divine goodness, while the mind of the reprobate, serving its own malice, nonetheless obeys the judgment of His strict justice.* Note that this vision of Micaiah was not a real event that truly took place, but was imaginary: for God painted in the imagination of Micaiah this scene and form of a council, so that he seemed to behold God taking counsel with Angels and demons concerning the manner of deceiving King Ahab. For in truth God holds no council with Angels, much less with demons, nor does He consult with them, inasmuch as He knows with absolute certainty all the ways by which a thing may be accomplished, and which of them will be effective and will produce their effect, and which will not. *For who has been His counsellor?* (Romans 11). The same is evident from the disagreement and ignorance of the Angels which is tacitly implied here, when the counsel of the demon is preferred above the opinions of them all, in verse 22. This vision, therefore, was imaginary and symbolic, sent by God to the Prophet, so that through it King Ahab might understand that he was being deceived by his false prophets who promised him victory, and that this came to pass by God's permission and disposition, for the just purpose of his chastisement and destruction. For God accommodates Himself and His oracle to the capacity and understanding of King Ahab. So Serarius, Sanchez, and others.

That inquiry of God, beyond signifying the demerits and crimes of Ahab by which he deserved to be deceived and to perish, signifies two things. The first is that deception is foreign to God. Hence God here inquires after it as though it were not found within Himself, nor discovered among divine things; but rather in the realm of demons, who here among themselves were devising a plan for the manner of deceiving Ahab — wherefore God is represented in this vision as questioning them, as if to say: *Which of you, O demons, will deceive Ahab? For I know that you are deliberating this very matter.* The second thing signified is that the deception itself belonged to the demons, yet was fully known and ordered by God toward the punishment of Ahab; for God willed to punish Ahab, and therefore permitted him to consult the demon through his false prophets, and permitted the demon to lie and to deceive Ahab by promising him victory in the battle in which he was himself to be slain. So St. Augustine, *Quaestiones* on the book of Judges. Hence Theodoret and Procopius teach that what is here signified is that the divine will was the cause of Ahab's destruction; for that will desired to punish the impiety of Ahab, and therefore permitted certain of his sins — which were nonetheless committed freely — namely that he should consult his soothsayers, and should believe them, and thus be deceived and perish. These two things, says Cajetan, are signified by this inquiry of God. For otherwise God properly inquires into nothing, since He perfectly beholds and knows all things. Scripture therefore speaks here *anthropopathically*, that is, after the manner of men; because men, in matters that are doubtful and future, inquire and take counsel concerning the fitting manner of carrying a thing out. *And one spoke words of this kind, and another in a different way.* [*Aliter.*] The various ways of deceiving Ahab that were suggested to God by those standing present — who were not so much Angels as demons — in this symbolic vision are recounted, though they are not enumerated in particular, because God did not wish to make use of them. Yet they are reported in general terms, so that it may be apparent that all the means of deception are known to God and are ready at hand, that He might execute through them the decree of His providence and vengeance against the impious Ahab who was to be slain. So Cajetan.

The meaning is as follows: a certain devil presented himself before the Lord to deceive Ahab; for it is his office to lie and to deceive — this the good Angels cannot do, being therefore truthful and faithful. *For it is not right to believe*, says St. Gregory in Book 2 of the *Moralia*, *that a good spirit was willing to serve deception, so as to say: I will go out and be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets; but because Ahab, on account of his preceding sins, was worthy and deserved to be condemned by such a deception — inasmuch as he who had often willingly fallen into fault should at length be overtaken by punishment against his will. By a hidden justice, license is granted to malignant spirits, so that those whom they strangle willingly in the snare of sin, they may also drag, even against their will, into the punishment of sin.* The same teaching is given by St. Augustine, Book 83, *Quaestiones*, question 53.

As if to say: I will suggest falsehood and a lying promise to all the pseudo-prophets of Ahab, so that they with one voice may promise him victory in battle, though he himself is to be slain in that same battle. Furthermore, Philastrius, in his book *On Heresies*, in the heresy concerning the Pythoness, adds the following — from uncertain source: *And the unclean spirit said to the Lord: I will make myself as an Angel of Light, and I will speak similar words, and I will lead him into battle, and there I will abandon him, and so he shall die. And the Lord said: Go, so shalt thou do.* Again, St. Epiphanius, in heresy 25, says: *The spirit of error is like a breath in a flute, stirring up each foolish person in various ways against the truth; for the flute itself is an imitation of the serpent, through whom the devil spoke and deceived Eve.* And the Lord said: *Thou shalt deceive, and shalt prevail; go forth and do so.* These are words of God not commanding, but permitting the devil — who was already prepared to deceive — to go ahead and deceive Ahab. Thus Christ said to Judas: *What thou doest, do quickly* (John 13:27), that is: I permit thee to betray me at once.

He gave free rein to the demon — not by impeding him, but by allowing him, who was already willing to deceive, to carry out that deception. For the demon can deceive or tempt no one unless God positively permits it. Wherefore, just as one who holds a lion bound by a rope, if he loosens the rope so that the lion may attack and kill someone, is said to have caused that person's death by loosening the rope — so too is God said to give a spirit of lying, when He loosens the rope from the devil and permits him, who is most ready to lie, to lie and deceive. For by such turns of phrase, Scripture signifies the most profound providence of God, which holds in its own hand the wills of all men — both evil and good — in such a way that they can will or do absolutely nothing except what God has permitted. **HE SMOTE MICAIAH ON THE CHEEK** — which is a shameful and ignominious blow, as I noted at Lamentations 3:30. **Thou shalt see on that day, when thou goest into a chamber within a chamber** (that is, into the innermost and most hidden chamber, since thou shalt find no place where thou canst be safe from fear of the Syrian enemies) **to hide thyself.**

Or of affliction. Vatablus renders it: *Such as is customarily given to the afflicted* — as if to say: Sustain him with a little bread and a little water. The Chaldean paraphrase has: *bread such as is given in affliction*, that is, not unto satiety, but only unto bare necessity, so that he may live, and not die of hunger or thirst.
This is my oracle — mark it well, so that when you see it truly come to pass, you may acknowledge that I have foretold the truth.

...in royal garments and finery, as though he were the general of the army, so that you might add courage to the soldiers for fighting bravely. MOREOVER THE KING OF ISRAEL (Ahab) CHANGED HIS HABIT, and laying aside the royal garb, clothed himself in the common dress of an ordinary soldier. Ahab did this not to honor Jehoshaphat, as though he himself were the commander and general of the war (though he did simulate this before Jehoshaphat), but out of fear of the prophecy of Micaiah. For even though he did not fully believe that Micaiah spoke truly — namely, that he would be slain in battle — yet he feared this very thing, especially since the command of the king of Syria had become known, by which he had ordered that all his men should turn their weapons against Ahab alone. For this command seems to have become known to him. Furthermore, Josephus adds that Ahab did this so that he might more easily and certainly elude the prophecy of Micaiah and show it to be false: for with his garb changed and posted in the rearmost rank, he would be far from the swords and blows of the enemy, so that there would seem to be no reason to fear wound or death.

...fight against Ahab, so that you may slay or capture him alone; for he is the firebrand of the war. For Jehoshaphat follows him as a companion follows a leader. Therefore, once he is removed, all war will be ended. Again, the king of Syria gave this order at the instigation of God, as it seems, who willed to punish and destroy the impious Ahab. Hence Josephus adds that Ahab alone fell in the battle, and that Micaiah had foretold this. AND A CERTAIN MAN DREW HIS BOW, AIMING AN ARROW AT RANDOM, AND BY CHANCE STRUCK THE KING OF ISRAEL. This was a chance event with respect to the Syrian soldier who loosed the arrow from his bow, but with respect to God it was no chance at all, but a directed blow; for God through His angels guided this arrow so that it should strike none other than the impious Ahab. So Julian the Apostate, as a new Ahab restoring the worship of idols, was struck by a heavenly dart — as Nazianzus says, Oration 2 against Julian — and fell in the Persian war in the year of the Lord 363, pierced, as is taught from Helladius by Damascus, Oration 1 on Images, by Nicephorus, Book 10, Chapter 35, and others, by St. Mercurius the Martyr; and therefore, raging and gnashing his teeth, filling his hand with his own blood and casting it into the air, he cried: "Thou hast conquered, O Galilean!" — for so he called Christ in contempt — as Theodoret witnesses, Book 3, Chapter 20. Moreover, Julian was slain on the same day on which, in the preceding year, he had put to death the holy John and Paul, chief ministers of St. Constantia, daughter of Constantine the Great, and crowned them with martyrdom, namely on the 26th of June, as Baronius shrewdly observes. BETWEEN THE LUNG AND THE STOMACH. The Hebrew reads: ben debaldm uben hassirian, which the Rabbis translate: between the joints and the breastplate; the Chaldean: between the joints of the corselet; our Vulgate at 2 Paralipomenon 18:33 translates: between the neck and the shoulder-blades. Both are true, says the Abulensis; for this barbed arrow entered between the neck and shoulder-blades of Ahab, and passed out between the stomach and the lung: thus the exit of the arrow is described here, while in Paralipomenon the entry is narrated. AND THEY WASHED HIS CHARIOT IN THE POOL OF SAMARIA (where Josephus incorrectly has: Jezare or Jezrael), AND THE DOGS LICKED HIS BLOOD, AND THEY WASHED THE REINS, ACCORDING TO THE WORD OF THE LORD. For "reins," the Hebrew has zonoth, which the Septuagint and Josephus translate: harlots. The harlots, they say, bathed in his blood. Josephus indeed says: harlots thereafter drew water from that pool, because these women purify the night's defilement by washing themselves at dawn, as Serarius relates from Procopius and Theodoret. The Chaldean and the Rabbis translate: arms, and add that these were brought into the armory which was at Jezreel, and there the dogs licked the blood clinging to them, and thus Elijah's oracle was fulfilled to the letter. But our Vulgate most aptly translates: reins. For the passage concerns the chariot and its reins, stained with the blood of Ahab, and therefore washed in the pool of Samaria, whence the dogs licked it there. And thus the prophecy of Elijah was fulfilled — not in every particular, but in part; but in all particulars it was fulfilled in the slaying of Ahaziah, son of Ahab, as I said at Chapter 21, verse 19.

How houses are made of ivory I have explained at Amos 3, on the words: "The houses of ivory shall perish." THE NAME OF HIS MOTHER WAS AZUBAH. In the Books of Kings the mother of each king is frequently named, because upon the mother depends the upbringing of children, whether for good or for ill; and as the mother is, so is the offspring. Thus the Spartans were strong in wars and labors, because they had mothers who, following the instruction of Lycurgus, accustomed both themselves and their children to hardship and austerity. Hence when someone said to Gorgo, wife of Leonidas: "You Spartan women alone rule your men," she replied: "Because we alone also bear men." So Plutarch in the Apophthegmata. A clearer example is found in St. Wenceslaus, Duke of Bohemia, and his brother Boleslaus: for the former, piously raised by his devout grandmother Ludmilla, became pious and holy; but the latter, impiously nurtured by his wicked mother Drahomíra, turned out impious and a fratricide — for in order to seize the kingdom, he slew St. Wenceslaus, yet thereby made him a martyr, as the Life of St. Wenceslaus relates, which Surius, following Aeneas Silvius, records under the 28th of September. This is what the Apostle says: "But if the firstfruit be holy, so is the lump also; and if the root be holy, so are the branches." Romans 11:16. So the Maccabean mother, St. Symphorosa, and St. Felicitas — three mothers, I say, and heroines — were leaders of their seven sons to martyrdom. BUT THE HIGH PLACES HE DID NOT TAKE AWAY. You will say: at 2 Paralipomenon 17 it is said that he did take them away. I reply: He did not take them away at the beginning of his reign, lest he stir up sedition of the people against himself; but in the course of time, when he had sufficiently secured his kingdom. Hence in Paralipomenon it is added: "when he had taken courage." Or else he took them away, but not so thoroughly that they did not spring up again from the excessive inclination of the people toward them, as is intimated at 2 Paralipomenon 20:33. Finally, Lyranus and others answer that Jehoshaphat removed the high places erected to idols, but permitted the high places dedicated to the true God. This answer, as it is the more straightforward, so also is the more true. For when Scripture had said at 2 Paralipomenon 17:6 that Jehoshaphat had removed the high places and groves — that is, the idolatrous ones — afterward at Chapter 20:33 it adds: "But the high places" — namely, those dedicated to the true God but forbidden by Him — "he did not take away." THE REST OF THE WORDS (that is, the deeds and acts; this is a metonymy frequent in these books) OF JEHOSHAPHAT, etc., ARE THEY NOT WRITTEN IN THE BOOK OF THE WORDS OF THE DAYS OF THE KINGS OF JUDAH? Namely in the Chronicles and Diaries in which the words — that is, the deeds — of the kings of Judah were recorded day by day. For many heroic deeds of Jehoshaphat are here passed over in brevity, of which some are recounted at 2 Paralipomenon, Chapter 17 and 2, where the zeal of Jehoshaphat in propagating the worship of God and in instructing the people is celebrated, and therefore glory was rendered to him by God as a reward. Again, in the same book, Chapter 19, it is narrated that he reformed the tribunals throughout Judah and appointed as judges Levites and other godly and incorruptible men. Moreover, at Chapter 20 there is recounted the signal victory which he obtained from God by his piety and prayer against Ammon and Moab, where among other things at verse 12, praying he says: "We have not strength enough to be able to resist this great multitude which rusheth in upon us. But as we know not what to do, we have only this left, that we should turn our eyes to Thee." Wherefore God raised up the prophet Jahaziel, who says at verse 17: "Stand confident, and you shall see the help of the Lord over you." Trusting therefore in this, he went before the army, yet in such a way that he sent ahead singers praising God and singing: "Give praise to the Lord, for His mercy endureth forever." And when they began to sing, D[eus...]

...because Edom, having been subdued by David, was governed by his prefects; but shortly afterward, under Joram the son of Jehoshaphat, Edom revolted so as not to be under Judah, and set up a king for itself, as is said at 4 Kings 8:20. WHICH WERE TO SAIL TO OPHIR. Where Ophir is situated I have explained at Chapter 9:28. THEY WERE BROKEN AT EZIONGEBER, because they were confederated and joined to the fleet of the impious king of Israel, namely Ahaziah the son of Ahab, as is said at 2 Paralipomenon 20:37. Hence Ahaziah, seeing his ships broken, asked of Jehoshaphat that his servants might go with the servants of Jehoshaphat in his ships; but Jehoshaphat refused this, as follows; evidently because he feared the same or a worse punishment from God on account of the alliance with the impious Ahaziah. AND JEHOSHAPHAT SLEPT WITH HIS FATHERS, AND WAS BURIED WITH THEM IN THE CITY OF DAVID — that is, in Sion; yet a mausoleum was erected for him in the valley which thence was called the Valley of Jehoshaphat, which is situated between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives, where the universal judgment shall take place, as I have explained from Bede and others at Joel 3:2. Here ends the Third Book of Kings, which embraces the history of the Kings of Judah over the span of 126 years. For Solomon reigned 40 years, Rehoboam 17, Abijah 3, Asa 41, Jehoshaphat 25; add all these together and you will have 126 years. Finally, with regard to the synchronism with the history of the Gentiles, Eusebius in his Chronicle, Genebrard, Salianus, and others record the following: In the time of Jehoshaphat, Ahab ruled over Israel, and Ahaziah his son; in Assyria, Orphratanes; in Lacedaemon, Archelaus; in Athens, Mechiades; in Corinth, Agelas; among the Latins, Capetus Silvius, and Tiberinus Silvius — who gave the river Tiber its name, since it had previously been called the Albula — and Agrippa Silvius; in Egypt, Nepher, Cheras, Amenophis, Osochor, and Spinaces. COMMENTARY ON THE FOURTH BOOK OF KINGS. ARGUMENT. Under Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah, Manasseh, Amon, Josiah, Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, and Zedekiah — kings descending in unbroken succession from David over the span of 304 years — there reigned in Israel, that is, over the ten tribes: Ahaziah the son of Ahab, Joram, Jehu, Jehoahaz, Joash, Jeroboam, Zechariah, Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, Pekah, and Hoshea, under whom the kingdom of Israel came to an end in the sixth year of Hezekiah, when all its people were carried away by Shalmaneser into Assyria, as we shall hear at Chapter 18:10. The kingdom of Judah, however, came to an end in the last year of Zedekiah, when its people were taken away by Nebuchadnezzar into the Babylonian captivity, as we shall hear in the final chapter. The Hebrews join this book to the Third Book and make one volume of them. But the Latins divide them more aptly, and begin the Fourth Book here.
